(vii) Plant Tissue Culture - This technique involves separation of cells or tissues of plants and growing them in artificial medium containing hormones for growth and differentiation. The excised part of tissue called explant grows into a whole plant when they are nurtured with appropriate medium under controlled laboratory conditions. Normally, excised tissue grows into an unorganised mass of cells called callus, which is activated to grow into separate organs and finally developed into plantlets (organogenesis) after adding suitable mixture of cytokinin and auxin. The plantlets are transplanted to separate pots in the nursery and finally into the fields.
Fig. Plant Tissue culture
Plant tissue culture has developed as a separate branch of science after discovering the potentiality of each cell of plant to grow into a separate organism. This potential of a cell is called as totipotency. It has made significant contribution in the field of crop improvement. Some of them are :
(i) Micropropagation
(ii) Production of disease free plants
(iii) Production of haploids
(iv) Embryo culture
(v) Mutation and mutants
(vi) Creation of variations
(vii) Protoplast culture

theory_sections.topics: Advantages and Disadvantages of Asexual Reproduction :
theory_sections.markdown: ## Advantages and Disadvantages of Asexual Reproduction :

## Advantages :
- New generations can be created from a single individual.
- Rapid process of producing offsprings, which are exactly identical to the parents.

## Disadvantages :
- Variations are absent.
- Individuals may not be able to adapt to changing environment.
- It results into overcrowding and struggle for existence.
Time to
Check Your Knowledge
1. Why is regeneration not always same as reproduction?
2. Why can't all multi-cellular organisms reproduce by fragmentation?
3. What is the benefit of reproduction via spore formation?

## Sexual Reproduction
This mode of reproduction involves two individuals to produce a new generation. Hens alone can't produce chicks, nor can bulls alone produce new calves. Both sexes, males and females are needed to produce new generations. Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of male and female gametes to form a zygote, which then develops into an individual.
The various characteristics of this type of reproduction are as follows -
- Two different sexes i.e. male and female take part in this process.
- The male sex organ produces male gametes or sperms and the female sex organ produces the female gametes or ova.
- Gametes are formed by meiosis and are haploid in nature.
- Sperms are motile and move towards ovum for fertilisation and then form a diploid zygote.
- It is a slow process of multiplication.
- Variations appear due to new combination of genes during crossing over.
- The genetic makeup of offsprings is different from that of parent.
- Diploid zygote divides by mitosis and passes through different phases of development to form a multicellular individual.

## Types of Sexual Reproduction
Sexual reproduction is of two types: Syngamy and Conjugation.

## Syngamy
This is a complete and permanent fusion of two haploid gametes to form a diploid zygote. When the two fusing gametes are morphologically and physiologically similar to each other, the process is called isogamy e.g. Monocystis. When two organisms themselves act as gametes, it is called hologamy e.g. Yeast.

## Conjugation
This is a temporary pairing of two parents to exchange male and female gametes through a temporary cytoplasmic bridge e.g. ciliated protozoans like Paramoecium.
In majority of animals, including human beings, male and female individuals are separate. Such organisms are called unisexual, i.e. they have only one sex in one individual. Some plants are also unisexual e.g. papaya, cucurbits, etc. But majority of flowering plants and a few animals (like tapeworm, earthworm, garden snail, star fish, etc.) are bisexual or hermaphrodite, i.e. individuals have both male and female sex organs.

## Learn More
Differences between Asexual and Sexual Reproduction
| S. No. | Asexual Reproduction | Sexual Reproduction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Involves single parent. | Involves both parents i.e., male and female. |
| 2. | Offsprings are produced from the somatic cells. | Offsprings are produced from the sex cells of both parents. |
| 3. | There is no fusion of gametes. | Fusion of gametes occurs. |
| 4. | DNA copying mechanisms are accurate. | DNA copying mechanisms are not accurate. |
| 5. | Variations are absent. | Variations are present. |
| 6. | Genetic makeup of offsprings is identical to each other and to the parents. | Genetic makeup of offsprings is different from each other and also from the parents. |
1. Why are variations significant for evolution?

## SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN FLOWERING PLANTS
The reproductive units of sexually reproducing plants are flowers. The flower is a condensed shoot with the nodes present very close to each other. The different parts of the flower are attached to the nodes. All the structures present at one node are collectively called the whorl. The first or the outermost two whorls are called the non-reproductive or accessary whorls. They are calyx and corolla. The inner two whorls are androecium and gynoecium, the reproductive or necessary whorl. If a flower has either androecium or gynoecium then it is called as unisexual. For e.g. sunflower. While, If the flower contains both androecium and gynoecium then, it is called bisexual. For e.g. Hibiscus and Mustard.

## Parts of a flower
A typical flower consists of following parts -
(i) Sepals : These make outer whorl of the flower called calyx. It is usually green in colour but in some flowers it may be coloured to attract the insects. It protects the inner whorls of the flower.
(ii) Petals : These make the second whorl of flower, called corolla and consists of coloured petals. Being coloured in nature they attract various insects and animals for pollination.
(iii) Stamen : It is the male reproductive part of a flower. Each stamen consists of a stalk called filament and a flattened top called the anther. The anthers produce pollens grains which produces male gametes. The pollen grains appear as yellowish powder which stick to our hands. Stamens form the third whorl, called androecium.
(iv) Carpel : Carpels have a swollen ovary at the base, an elongated middle style and a terminal stigma. The ovary contains ovules. Each ovule possess an egg which is the female gamete. Carpel form the fourth whorld, called gynoecium.
Fig : Longitudinal section of flower
1. Binary fission occurs in :
(a) only eukaryotes
(b) only prokaryotes
(c) both prokaryotes and eukaryotes
(d) none of the above
2. Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.
(a) Sexual reproduction always requires two separate individuals.
(b) An organism can reproduce both sexually and asexually in its life span.
(c) Budding and fragmentation are similiar phenomenon.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Structure of Androecium (Male Reproductive Organ)
Unit of androecium or male reproductive organ is called stamen. A typical stamen is differentiated into three parts - a long, thin structure called filament and a swollen spore bearing structure called anther.

## Structure of Anther
Anther has two lobes and each lobe consists of two theca (dithecous), hence appears tetragonal in a cross sectional view. Each theca has a mass of sporogenous tissue which develops into microsporangium or pollen sac. Microsporangium appears roughly circular in outline. It has four layers - outermost layer epidermis, endothecium, middle layer and innermost layer tapetum. The outer three layers provide protection and help in dehiscence of anther, while tapetum nourishes the developing microspores or pollen grains.
Fig. (a) A typical stamen (b) Three dimensional cut-section of an anther (c) Transverse section of a mature anther (d) A dehisced anther

## Microsporogenesis
Each cell of the sporogenous tissue acts as pollen mother cell (PMC). It gives rise to a microspore tetrad, containing four haploid microspores by the process of meiosis. This process of formation of spores from PMC is called as microsporogenesis. As anther matures and dehydrates, microspores dissociate from each other and form pollen grains. Several thousands of pollen grains are released after dehiscence of anther.

## Structure of pollen grain
Pollen grains represent the male gametophyte. They are generally spherical in shape and measure about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></math> micrometers in diameter. It has a two layered wall. The outer hard layer is called the exine which is made up of a resistant organic material known as sporopollenin. It can withstand high temperatures and strong acids, alkali and enzymes. The exine is discontinuous due to the presence of apertures, called as germ pores, where sporopollenin is absent. Pollen grains have been found as fossils because of the presence of sporopollenin. The inner wall of the pollen grain is called the intine. It is thin and continuous layer made up of cellulose and pectin. The cytoplasm of the pollen grain is surrounded by a plasma membrane.
A mature pollen grain contains two cells-the vegetative cell and generative cell.
The vegetative cell is bigger, has abundant food reserve and a large irregularly shaped nucleus. The generative cell is small and floats in the cytoplasm of the vegetative cell. It is spindle shaped with dense cytoplasm and a small nucleus. In most of the angiosperms, pollen grains are shed at the two celled stage. In some species, the generative nucleus divides mitotically to give rise to two male gametes (3-celled) before the pollen grains are shed.
Once the pollen grains shed, they have to land on the stigma (female reproductive part) before they loose viability. The period of viability of pollen grains varies from 30 minutes to few months.
Fig. Stages of Microspore maturation into a Pollen Grain

## Structure of Gynoecium (Female Reproductive Organ)
The unit of gynoecium is called pistil or carpel. It may be monocarpellary (one carpel), bicarpellary (two carpels), tricarpellary (three carpels) or multicarpellary (many carpels). When the number of carpels is more than one, the gynoecium can be syncarpous (all the carpels are fused together) or apocarpous (carpels remain free). Each pistil has three parts; ovary, style and stigma. Placenta is present inside the ovarian cavity (locule) of ovary. It contain ovules, also known as megasporangia. The number of ovules in an ovary may be one as in mango, rice, wheat or many as in orchids, watermelon, papaya, etc.

## Ovule
The ovule arises as a primordium on the placenta. Ovule is attached to the placenta by a short stalk called funiculus or funicle. The body of the ovule fuses with funicle in the region called hilum. Thus hilum represents the junction between ovule and funicle. Each ovule has two protective coverings called integuments. They cover the whole ovule except at the tip, where a small opening called as micropyle is present. Opposite to the micropylar end is chalaza, which is the basal part of the ovule. Enclosed within the integuments is a mass of cells called nucellus. Cells of nucellus are rich in reserve food material. Embedded in the nucellus, is the female gameotophyte called embryo sac.

## Megasporogenesis
The process of formation of megaspore from megaspore mother cell (MMC) is
Fig. : Ovule
called megasporogenesis. A single MMC differentiates in the nucleus towards the micropylar region. It is a large cell containing dense cytoplasm and a prominent nucleus. MMC undergoes meiosis and results in production of four megaspores.
Fig. Part of ovule showing Megasporogenesis

## Embryo Sac
Only one of the four megaspore develops into a functional embryo sac or the female gametophyte while the other three degenerate. The functional embryo sac nucleus divides mitotically into two nuclei which move to the opposite poles, forming a 2 -nucleate embryo sac. Two more sequential mitotic divisions in the 2 -nucleate embryo sac result in the formation of the 4 -nucleate and then 8 -nucleate embryo sac. These mitotic divisions are free nuclear i.e. they are not immediately followed by cell wall formation. After it reaches the 8 -nucleate stage, cell walls are laid down to organise into a typical female gametophyte.
There is characteristic distribution of cells within the embryo sac. Three cells are grouped at the micropylar end and constitute the egg apparatus. The egg apparatus consists of two synergids and one egg cell. The synergids have special thickenings at the micropylar tip called filiform apparatus, which play an important role in guiding the pollen tubes into the synergids. The three cells at the chalazal end are called the antipodal cells. There is one large central cell which has two polar nuclei in the centre of cell. Hence, a typical angiospermic embryo sac, at maturity, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mn mathvariant="bold">8</mn></mrow></mrow></math> nucleated but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mn mathvariant="bold">7</mn></mrow></mrow></math> celled.
Fig. Diagrammatic representation of a mature Embryo sac.

## Pollination
The transfer of pollen grains from anther of the stamen to the receptive stigma of the carpel is called pollination. Pollination is an important mechanism by which the pollen grains produced inside the pollen sacs of anthers, are transported by various agencies to the stigma of female reproductive organ. It is an essential process that facilitates successful fertilisation in plants. The pollen grains protect male gametes from drying out.
Types of Pollination: It is of two types i.e., Self Pollination and Cross Pollination.
Self Pollination: Self pollination involves the transfer of pollen grains from the anther of a flower to the stigma of the same flower or to the stigma of another flower on the same plant. In bisexual flowers, pollen grains are transfered from the anther to the stigma of the same flower. This is called Autogamy. It is generally not dependent on an external agency i.e. wind, water, insects, birds, etc for pollination, but may result in less vigorous offspring.
Cross Pollination: It is the transfer of the pollen grains from the anther of one flower to the stigma of another flower on another plant. It involves two separate plants and depends on external agencies like wind, water, air or insects and etc.
Fig. : Diagrammatic Representation of Self and Cross Pollination
Majority of plants use biotic (animals) agents and only a small portion of plants use abiotic agents (wind and water) for pollination. The contact of pollen grains with stigma depends on a chance factor. So flowers produce large amount of pollen grains to compensate for it.
Observe some flowers in your surroundings (Rose, lemon, Eucalyptus, mustard, Hibiscus, etc). Try to find out which animal visits them and if they could be the pollinators. Also, try to see whether there is any co-relation in the characteristics of a flower to the animal that visits it.

## Fertilisation
Fertilisation involves the fusion of a male gamete with a female gamete. To bring about fertilisation, the pollen grains fall on the stigma and gives rise to the pollen tubes that passes through the style and enter into the ovule via one of the synergids. Here, it releases two gametes; one fuses with the egg cell to form zygote (syngamy) and another fuses with 2 polar nuclei to form a triploid primary endosperm nucleus (triple fusion) inside the embryo sac. Since two types of fusion occur, it is known as double fertilisation.
Fig. Ovary showing the process of fertilisation

## Learn More
There are three paths for the entry of pollen tube inside the embryo sac.
1. Porogamy → Pollen tube enters through the micropyle. It is found in many angiosperms e.g. Capsella
2. Mesogamy → Pollen tube enters through integuments, (e.g. cucurbita) or through funiculus e.g. Pistacia.
3. Chalazogamy → Pollen tube enters through chalaza, e.g. Betula.

## Post Fertilisation Events
This include endosperm and embryo development, maturation of ovules and ovary into seeds and fruit respectively.

## CONNECTING TOPIC
Endosperm: The primary endosperm nucleus undergoes successive nuclear divisions to give rise to free nuclei. This stage of endosperm development is called free-nuclear endosperm. This is followed by cell wall formation and the endosperm becomes cellular. Endosperm is a triploid tissue where cells are filled with reserve food material and is used for the nutrition of the developing embryo. Hence, endosperm development precedes embryo development.
Endosperm may either be completely consumed by the developing embryo (e.g. bean, pea, etc.) before seed maturation called non-endospermic or it may persist in the mature seeds called endospermic seed (e.g. castor, coconut) and it may be used up during seed germination.
Fig. Seed of Castor showing Endosperm

## Embryo
The diploid zygote develops into embryo at the micropylar end. This starts only after formation of a certain amount of endosperm. The zygote gives rise to a proembryo which develops into a globular embryo, is followed by a heart shaped and later into mature embryo.

## Fruit
Fruits develop from the ovary. The formation of seeds and fruits is simultaneous. Wall of the ovary develops into the wall of the fruit called pericarp. In fruits like guava, mango, etc. the fruits are fleshy while in fruits like mustard, groundnut, etc. they are dry. By the time the fruit develops the other floral parts, degenerate and fall off. However, in some fruits such as apple, strawberry, etc. the thalamus contributes to fruit formation. Such fruits are called false fruits. Fruits that develop only from the ovary are called true fruits. Fruits are normally formed as a result of fertilisation but some fruits like banana develop without the act of fertilisation. Such fruits are called parthenocarpic fruits, these fruits are seedless. Such fruits can also be induced by the application of hormones. They have a great commercial value.
Tomato is the world's most popular fruit and is usually termed as a 'vegetable' in most countries.

## SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN HUMAN BEINGS
The human beings are unisexual with two separate sexes - male and female. Both the sexes (male and female) are visibly different from each other. The individuals of the two sexes show distinctive features called the secondary sexual characteristics. This period of secondary growth of sexual characters achieved during adolescence, called puberty. Some of the male secondary sexual characteristics are facial hair, cracking of voice, etc. and female secondary sexual characteristics include breast development,
broadening of hips, etc. Such distinguishing features are present in all the animals. These characteristics serve to identify and attract sex partners.
The reproductive system comprises of two different parts:
(1) Primary reproductive system
(2) Accessory reproductive system
Primary reproductive system includes gamete producing organs, the testes and the ovaries. Accessory reproductive system includes the glands, passages and other such associated structures.

## Male Reproductive System
The male reproductive system consists of a pair of testes, a pair of sperm ducts (Vas deferens), accessory glands, and a penis.

## Testes
A pair of testis are the primary sex organs and are present inside the scrotum. They are contained within the abdominal cavity in the embryonic stage but descend in scrotum and remain here throughout life. It happens because the testes cannot produce sperms at body temperature. A temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></math> degrees lower, is ideal for the production of sperms. The sperms produced in testes are carried by the duct called vas deferens. The vas deferens unites with the urethra coming from the urinary bladder. Thus urethra forms a common passage for both urine and sperms. Prostate and seminal vesicle add their secretions into the vas deferens. Thus the sperm flows in the fluid which also provides the required nutrition.
Fig. Diagrammatic sectional view of the Male Reproductive system
CONNECTING TOPIC

## Structure of Testes
Each testes is covered by a thick connective tissue layer. Internally, the testis is lobed. There are 15-20 lobes, each having a network of seminiferous tubules and the interstitial cells between the tubules. The seminiferous tubules produce sperms that are passed from the tubules to the posterior region of testis. The sperms are produced by a process called the spermatogenesis. The interstitial cells are also know as Leydig cells and they secrete testosterone hormone. In addition, they also form a packing tissue between the seminiferous tubules.

## Duct System
From the seminiferous tubules, the sperms are passed into a network of 10-12 ducts called the efferent ducts or the vasa efferentia. They are then passed into a highly coiled tubular part called the epididymis. Epididymis is an organ that extends from the top of the testis along its side to its back. The sperms from the epididymis pass into a distinct tube called the vas deferens, also called the sperm duct. It rises in the
Fig.: Diagrammatic view of male testis
abdominal cavity and loops over the ureter of that side. The passage that connects the testicular region with the abdomen is called the inguinal canal. Sometimes, the intestine bulges into the scrotum through this canal due to pressure in the abdomen. This causes hernia. Vas deferens is joined by the duct from the seminal vesicle to form the ejaculatory duct. The duct then passes through the prostate and opens into the urethra. The system of tubules consisting of the vasa efferentia, epididymis, vas deferens, ejaculatory tube and the urethra is the duct system associated with the male reproductive system.

## Glands
The various glands associated with the male reproductive system are as follows:
Seminal Vesicle: A pair of glands that are present behind the urinary bladder and secrets a fluid into the common ejaculatory duct. This fluid along with the sperms is called the semen, (a milky fluid).
Prostate: It is a bi-lobed gland near the opening of the urethra. The prostate also pours its secretion into the urethra. It is alkaline and mixes with the semen.
Cowper's Gland: They are a pair of small ovoid glands that secrete lubricating fluid into the urethra just before it enters the penis. The secretions of these glands make the sluggish sperms more active and help in the passage of sperms through the duct system and then in the ejaculation.

## Penis
Penis is a muscular organ containing erectile tissue. The tissue is richly supplied with blood vessels. On sexual stimulation, the penis is gorged, with excess of blood which causes it to become erect. During sexual intercourse, the penis is inserted into vagina of the female for the ejaculation of semen.

## Female Reproductive System
The female reproductive system consists of a pair of ovaries, a pair of oviducts, uterus, vagina and vulva. The main functions of the female reproductive system are to produce eggs, receive the sperms, provide the site for fertilisation, implantation of the growing embryo and development of the foetus. It also produces hormones that control the various stages of ovulation and maintenance of pregnancy.
Fig. Diagrammatic sectional view of the Female Reproductive System

## Ovaries
Ovaries are a pair of oval structures that are present one on either side. The ovaries produce eggs one at a time, every alternate month. The eggs are produced by the germinal epithelial cells of the ovary.

## Oviducts / Fallopian tubes
Oviducts / Fallopian tubes are a pair of tubes of about 12 cm in length. They run from the ovaries of each side to the uterus. At the ovarian end the tube is funnel-shaped with the end of the tube thrown into a number of folds. These folds are ciliated which help to sweep the egg produced by the ovary into the fallopian tube. The fallopian tubes are the sites for fertilisation of the egg by the sperm.

## Uterus
Uterus is pear-shaped structure, broader on the upper end and narrower on the lower end. The upper end is called the body of the uterus and the lower end is called the cervix. At the upper end, it receives the oviducts of either side, whereas at the lower end the cervix opens into the vagina which then opens to the outside.
The uterine wall has three layers. They are the innermost endometrium made up of several glands and blood vessels, the middle myometrium madeup of smooth muscles and the outer perimetrium madeup of connective tissue. The inner surface of the uterus provides a site for the implantation of the embryo. The uterine wall plays an important role during childbirth. Cervix is made of sphincter muscles that control the opening and closing of the uterus.

## Vagina
Vagina is a 9 cm long muscular tube that receives the penis during coitus. It is lined with epithelial cells. The vaginal opening in young females is partially covered with a thin mucous membrane called hymen. This often breaks early in females during strenuous work or sports.

## Vulva
Vulva is the external female genitalia and comprises of mons pubis. The vaginal opening has two pair of folds on either side. The outer fold is thicker with hair, sweat glands and sebaceous glands and is called labia majora. The inner folds are thinner and devoid of hair. They are called labia minora. Covered by the upper part of the folds is the female equivalent of the penis, called the clitoris. It is also an erectile and highly sensitive organ. In this region, there are two openings, the upper urethral meatus or opening and the lower vaginal orifice.

## Gametogenesis
Sperms are produced in seminiferous tubules present in testis. This process is known as spermatogenesis. The precursors of the sperm, the diploid spermatogonium divides mitotically to produce numerous diploid spermatocytes. Spermatocytes undergo meiotic cell division to produce 4 haploid spermatids. Spermatids differentiate and develop into sperm which are single celled male gametes with a head, midpiece, and a tail.
Ovum (egg cell), the female gamete is produced in the ovaries of female reproductive system. This process is known as oogenesis. In contrast to males, the initial steps in egg production occur prior to their birth in females. Diploid oogonia and primary oocytes are produced in foetus and by the time she is born, females have about 1-2 million oocytes. In oocytes, the first meiotic division is initiated and then stopped. No further development occurs until the girl attains puberty. After maturity, the primary oocytes recommence their development, usually one at a time and once a month. The primary oocytes grow further and completes the meiosis I, forming a large secondary oocyte and a small polar body. Only after fertilization, meiosis II is completed. After completion of meiosis II, secondary oocytes gets converted into a fertilized egg or zygote.

## Menstruation
The reproductive cycle in the females is called as menstrual cycle. First menstruation begins at puberty and is called as Menarche. Menstrual cycle has an average time period of about 28 to 29 days. One ovum is released during the middle of each menstrual cycle. Cycle starts with the menstrual phase when menstrual flow occurs and it lasts for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></math> days. Menstrual flow results due to breakdown of endometrial lining of the uterus and its blood vessels that come out through vagina. Menstruation occurs only if released ovum is not fertilised. Pregnancy can be indicated by lack of menstrual cycle. It may also be caused by stress, poor health etc. Menstrual cycle ceases at about 50 years of age and this is termed as menopause. Cyclic menstruation is an indicator of normal reproductive phase and extends between menarche and menopause.

## Structures of Gametes
Each sperm is a small cell of about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>μ</mi><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></math> length and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>2.5</mn><mi>μ</mi><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></math> diameter. It constits of a head, middle piece and a tail region. The head consists of the nucleus and a structure called the acrosome at the tip. The acrosome secretes lytic substance that break down the wall of the egg for fertilisation. The beating of the tail propels the sperms forward at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></math> per minute. Each egg or ovum is a spherical cell much bigger than the sperm. It mainly consists of a single nucleus and some reserve food made of lipid droplets.
Fig. Structure of Sperm
Fig. Structure of Ovum

## Fertilisation
Fertilisaiton is defined as the fusion of sperm with the egg nucleus to form a diploid cell known as zygote. The fertilisation is internal in the human reproductive sytem. It is achieved by the insertion of the male organ, penis into the vagina of the female. The sperms are deposited in the vagina of the females during a process called as copulation or sexual intercourse. The release of sperms is called ejaculation and at one ejaculation there may be upto 600 million sperms. Of these, only a few from thousand make the journey from the vaginal canal to the oviducts. This may take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></math> hours. The life of the sperm is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></math> days after ejaculation.
In the fallopian tubes, many sperms, surround an egg. However, only one enters the egg leaving behind the tail. The enzymes of the acrosome digest the several layers of tissue to reach the egg cytoplasm. Once the sperm is reached inside the egg, the male and female nuclei become lighter and are called pro-nuclei. Both the pro-nuclei fuse to form a zygote. Once a sperm enters inside an egg, the thickening of the outer walls of the egg blocks the entry of other sperms.
Fig. Process of Fertilisation

## Post-Fertilisation Events
Once the egg is fertilised, ovulation and menstruation do not take place. The fertilised structure, now called the zygote, which is diploid. It starts dividing and forms a blastocyst, the first stage of an embryo. The blastocyst gets implanted on the uterine walls. It then grows into a foetus.
The foetus remains attached to the mother through an umbilical cord which is embedded in a tissue called placenta at one end. The placenta in turn is embedded into the uterine wall and is richly supplied with vessels. The nutrients from the mother's blood pass into the umbilical cord and the waste from the foetus pass into the mother's blood through the placenta.
The foetus is fully grown and ready to come out from the womb in 40 weeks. The uterine walls start muscular contractions called the labour that helps in pushing the foetus to the outside through the vaginal canal. The placenta detaches from the uterine wall once the baby is out. The umbilical cord is cut and tied near the baby's body.
Fig. Implantation of embryo
Fig. Reproduction in Human beings

## Reproductive Health
Sexual maturation does not necessarily mean that the body or mind is ready for sexual acts or having and bringing up children. Contraception gives individuals a choice to conceive or have children at their own will. Not only this, contraception also helps in controlling population growth. Goverment has come out with various programmes that encourage smaller families to use various contraceptive methods. Contraceptive devices are used to prevent unwanted pregnancy and STDs (Sexually Transmitted Diseases).

## Contraceptive Method
An ideal contraceptive should be user-friendly, easily available, effective and reversible. It should not have any side-effects and should not interfere with sexual drive, desire and sexual act. The contraceptive methods can be of two types.
1. Natural/Traditional methods
It works on the principle of avoiding chances of fusion of ovum and sperms. It includes :
(i) Periodic abstinence: It involves avoiding of coitus from days 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, when ovulation could be expected. It is called fertile period and the chances of fertilisation in this period are very high.
(ii) Coitus interruptus (withdrawal): It involves withdrawal of penis from the vagina just before ejaculation, in order to avoid insemination inside vagina.
(iii) Lactational amenorrhea: It involves the prevention of conception by breastfeeding the child. Ovulation and the cycle do not occur during the period of lactation. It has no side effects. However, this is effective up to 6 months following parturition (delivery). A chance of failure of this method is high.
2. Artificial methods
(A) Barrier methods
These method also prevent fusion of sperm and ovum. It is available for both male and female partners.
(i) Condoms : Condoms are made of rubber/latex sheath. They are used to cover the penis in male or vagina and cervix in the female. They are used just before coitus so that the semen does not enter into female reproductive tract.
Condoms are very popular because:
- It protects the user from sexually transmitted diseases.
- It is disposable.
- It can be self-used and thereby gives privacy to the user.
(ii) Diaphragms, cervical caps and vaults:
These are barriers made of rubber that are inserted into the female reproductive tract to cover the cervix during coitus. They prevent conception by blocking the entry of sperms through the cervix. They are reusable.
(iii) Spermicidal creams, jellies and foams are used with the barriers to increase contraceptive efficiency.
(B) Intra Uterine Devices (IUDs)
These devices are used only by females. These are inserted by doctors or expert nurses inside uterus. It makes the uterus unsuitable for implantation and cervix hostile to the sperms. IUDs includes :
(i) Non-medicated IUDs (e.g. Lippes loop)
(ii) Copper releasing IUDs (e.g. CuT, Cu7, Multiload 375)
(iii) Hormone releasing IUDs (e.g. Progestasert, LNG-20)
(C) Oral contraceptives
This method is used only by female. It is used in the form of tablets, hence known as pills. They contain hormoneseither progestogens or progestogen-oestrogen combinations. Pills are taken daily for 21 days starting within the first five days of menstrual cycle. After a gap of 7 days (during which menstruation occurs) it has to be repeated in the
same pattern till the female desires to prevent conception. They inhibit ovulation and implantation as well as alter the quality of cervical mucus to prevent the entry of sperms. Pills are very effective with lesser side effects E.g. Saheli which is a non-steroidal preparation used as an oral contraceptive. It is a 'once a week' pill with very few side effects and high contraceptive value.

## (D) Injectables
It involves usage of hormone, either progestogens alone or in combination with oestrogen by females as injections or implants under skin. Their mode of action is similar to that of pills. It is very effective for longer periods.

## (E) Emergency contraceptives
These are used within 72 hours of coitus, rape or casual unprotected intercourse. It involves administration of progestogens or progestogen-oestrogen combinations and IUDs.

theory_sections.topics: (F) Surgical methods (also known as sterilization methods)
theory_sections.markdown:
## (F) Surgical methods (also known as sterilization methods)
These are the permanent or terminal methods to prevent pregnancy. This method blocks the transport of gametes, thereby preventing conception. It is very effective but reversibility is poor. It includes:
(i) Vasectomy: It is the sterilization procedure in males. In this, a small part of the vas deferens is removed or tied up through a small incision on the scrotum.
(ii) Tubectomy: It is the sterilization procedure in females. In this, a small part of the fallopian tube is removed or tied up through a small incision in the abdomen or through vagina.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Infertility
There are many diseases/disorders which may affect the reproductive system in both males and females. One of them is infertility. It is the inability of a sexually active non-contracepting couple to achieve pregnancy in one year. In females, the common causes may include hormonal imbalances, blocked oviducts, damaged uterus or cervix and many others. In males, it may be due to the absence of sperms, low sperm count, abnormal sperms, auto immunity, impotence etc.
Infertility treatments can be either hormonal or modern like in vitro fertilisation (IVF). In IVF, the sperm and ovum are fused in a test tube to form a zygote, which is later implanted in the uterus of female for further development. These babies are called test tube babies. Other methods include gamete intra-fallopian transfer (GIFT). Here, gametes are transferred to the oviducts and fertilisation takes place inside the body itself.

## Column B
(p) Latex sheath
(q) Semen plasma
(r) Protozoan
(s) Corpus spongiosum

## Passage
1. When the branches of a plant growing in the field are pulled towards the ground and a part of them is covered with moist soil (leaving the tips of the branches exposed above the ground), then after sometime new roots develop from the parts of branches buried in the soil. On cutting these branches from the parent plant, new plants are produced from the cut parts of branches which had developed roots.
(a) What is this method of propagation of plants known as?
(b) What type of branches should a plant have to be able to be propagated by this method?
(c) Name any two plants which are grown for their flowers and propagated by this method.
(d) Name any two plants which are grown for their fruits and propagated by this method.
(e) Name one plant which gets propagated by this method naturally by forming runners (soft horizontal stems running above the ground).
2. When an insect sits on the flower of a plant then some particles, present on the top of little stalks in the flower get stick to its body hair. When this insect now sits on the flower of another similar plant, then particles attached to the hair of insect are shifted to top of a flaskshaped organ at the centre of a flower. This particle grows a long tube B from the top of flask-shaped organ through which C moves down and reaches the bottom of the flask-shaped organ. Here C fuses with the nucleus of D, present in structure E.
The fusion of C and D forms a new cell F which grows and develops into a seed of the plant.
(a) What are these particles? Name the process by which these particles are transfered from one flower to other flower of another similar plant.
(b) What is the name of tube B?
(c) What is C which moves down through the tube B ?
(d) Name D and E.
(e) What is F?

## Male Reproduction System
1. Read the following and answer any four questions from 1(i) to 1(v).
The male reproductive system consists of portions which produce the germ-cells and other portions that deliver the germ-cells to the site of fertilisation. The formation of germ-cells or sperms takes place in the testes. These are located outside the abdominal cavity in scrotum because sperm formation requires a lower temperature than the normal body temperature. We have discussed the role of the testes in the secretion of the hormone, testosterone, in the previous chapter. In addition to regulating the formation of sperms, testosterone brings about changes in appearance seen in boys at the time of puberty. The sperms formed are delivered through the vas deferens which unites with a tube coming from the urinary bladder. The urethra thus forms a common passage for both the sperms and urine. Along the path of the vas deferens, glands like the prostate and the seminal vesicles add their secretions so that the sperms are now in a fluid which makes their transport easier and this fluid also provides nutrition. The sperms are tiny bodies that consist of mainly genetic material and a long tail that helps them to move towards the female germ-cell.
(i) The seminiferous tubules of the testes are lined by the germinal epithelium consisting of
(a) spermatids
(b) cells of Sertoli
(c) spermatogonium
(d) spermatocytes
(ii) The seminiferous tubules of the testes are lined by the germinal epithelium consisting of
(a) sertoli cells
(b) cells of germinal epithelium
(c) cells of Leydig or interstitial cells
(d) secondary spermatocytes
(iii) Another name for Bulbourethral gland is
(a) Meibomian gland
(b) Prostate gland
(c) Perineal gland
(d) Cowper's gland
(iv) In man, Cryptorchidism is the condition when
(a) testes do not descent into the scrotum
(b) there are two testes in each scrotum
(c) testis degenerates in the scrotum
(d) testis enlarges in the scrotum
(v) Which of these is an accessory reproductive gland in male mammals
(a) Inguinal gland
(b) Prostate gland
(c) Mushroom-shaped gland
(d) Gastric gland
## (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions)

## Function of testosterone include:
(a) Stimulation of sperm production.
(b) Stimulation of the development of secondary sexual characters in males.
(c) Development, maturation and functioning of accessory sex organs like vas deferens and seminal vesicles.
(ii) Fallopian tubes
(iii) The embryo gets nourishment from the mother's blood with the help of a special tissue called placenta. This tissue is embedded in the uterine wall, containing villi on the embryo's side of the tissue. On the mother's side blood spaces are present which surround the villi. This provides a large surface area for glucose and oxygen to pass from the mother to the embryo.
3. Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther of one flower to the stigma of the same or another flower. It is said to be the first process of sexual fertilization in flowering plants. Pollen grains contain the male gamete and are present in the anthers of the flower.
Types of Pollination- There are two types of pollination:
- Self pollination: It takes place when the pollen of one flower are pollinated to the same flower or other flowers of the same plant.
- Cross pollination: It is also known as allogamy. Cross pollination occurs when pollen grains are transferred from the flower of one plant to the flower of different plant.
Agents of pollination: Wind, water or animals.
Pollination leading to fertilisation: After the pollen lands on a suitable stigma, the male gametes present in it has to reach the female germ cells present in the ovary. For this, a tube grows out of the pollen grain and travels through the style to reach the ovary. Once pollen tube reaches the ovary, it enters inside and bursts to release male gametes. This is followed by union of male and female gametes to produce a zygote. This process is known as fertilisation. After fertilisation, the zygote divides several times to form an embryo within the ovule. This is how a suitable pollination leads to fertilisation.

## (CONCEPTMAP)
[^1]

topics.summary:
## HEREDITY
Transmission of characters (resemblances as well as variations) from parents to the offspring from one generation to another is called heredity.
Although the offsprings inherit characters (or traits) of the parents and resemble them very closely, but the resemblance is not identical in all respects. The offsprings are never a true copy of the parents. In fact, no two individuals are exactly alike and the members of any one species differ from one another in some traits. The differences in the traits shown by the individuals of a species, and also by the offsprings of the same parents, are referred to as variations.
For example, in human beings, few people have hanging earlobes called free earlobe. In others, the earlobe is closely attached to the side of the head and is called closely attached earlobe. So free earlobes and attached earlobes are the two variations found in humans.
Fig. Variation in earlobe
## Accumulation of Variation during Reproduction :
We see different organisms around us. They all look different as species differ from each other. But when you look for differences among individuals of the same species, no two individuals are identical to each other. We acquire a common body design along with subtle changes. For example, a bacterium produces two bacteria by asexual reproduction. One of the offspring bacterium has a variation due to which it has higher tolerance of heat. This characteristic of heat resistance will go on accumulating in the offsprings of successive generations of this bacterium, and this will ultimately give rise to a variant of bacteria which will be highly heat resistant and would be able to survive even at very high temperatures. The advantage of variation to a species is that it increases the chance of its survival in a changing environment.
Only minor differences will be generated due to small inaccuracies in DNA copying mechanism. The diversity is quite high in case of sexually reproducing organisms because of reshuffling of traits during gamete formation and chance fusion of gametes during fertilisation. If the DNA comes from two parents, the differences will be pronounced. These differences will be passed on to the offsprings and would create diversity.
Fig. Creation of diversity over succeeding generations. The original organism at the top will give rise to, say, two individuals, similar in body design, but with subtle differences. Each of them, in turn, will give rise to two individuals in the next generation. Each of the four individuals in the bottom row will be different from each other. While some of these differences will be unique, others will be inherited from their respective parents, who were different from each other. Variations arise either due to an environmental influence or genetic factors like recombination of genes, mutation etc. The environmental factors influence the phenotype of individuals and these are not heritable. Genetic traits affect the genes and are heritable and important from evolutionary perspective.
Few examples of genetic traits in human beings and their variations are-
| S.No. | Genetic Trait | Variations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Earlobes | Free, Attached |
| 2 | Forelock | White, Not white |
| 3 | Dimples | Dimples, No dimples |
| 4 | Thumb | Straight, curved |
| 5 | Pinky finger | Bent, straight |
| 6 | Mid-digit hair | Mid-digit hair, No mid-digit hair |
| 7 | Toes | 2nd toe longer than big toe, 2nd toe shorter than big toe |
| 8 | Tongue rolling | Rolling up edges, Not rolling |
1. Does random fertilization contribute to genetic variation?
2. Why is genetic variation significant?
3. What is the relationship between meiosis and genetic variation?
## Rules for the Inheritance of Traits: Mendel's Contribution
The study of genetics was started by Gregor Johann Mendel, an Austrian Monk, in 1850s. However, his studies were not recognised by the world till after his death. Mendel is known as 'Father of Genetics'.
Before Mendel, although people knew that parents with blue eyes would get blue eyed children, etc., they did not know the exact mechanism of transfer of these traits from the parents to the offspring. They did not know that there is a physical basis of heredity. This was identified by Mendel through his pioneering work in pea plant (Pisum sativum). He blended his knowledge of science and mathematics and was the first one to keep count of individuals exhibiting a particular trait in each generation.
Fig. Gregor Johann Mendel
## Mendel's experimental material.
Mendel picked common garden pea plant for his experiment as they can be grown easily in large numbers and their reproduction can be manipulated. Pea plants have both male and female reproductive organs and they can either self-pollinate themselves or can cross-pollinate with another plant. Mendel was able to selectively cross-pollinate pure bred plants with particular traits and observe the outcome over many generations.
## Learn More
Geneticists today carry out breeding experiments with species that reproduce much more rapidly so that the amount of time and money required is reduced. Fruit flies and bacteria are commonly used for this purpose now. Fruit flies reproduce in about 2 weeks from birth, while bacteria E.coli reproduce in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></math> hours.
Through selective cross-breeding of pea over several generations, Mendel discovered that certain traits show up in offsprings without any blending of parent characteristics. Seven traits were observed by Mendel in pea plant and these could occur in one of the two forms. (dominant or recessive)
Fig. Pisum Sativum
Fig. Pisum Sativum
| S. No. | Character | Dominant | Recessive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stem height | Tall | Dwarf |
| 2 | Flower colour | violet | White |
| 3 | Flower position | Axial | Terminal |
| 4 | Pod shape | Inflated | Constricted |
| 5 | Pod colour | Green | Yellow |
| 6 | Seed shape | Round | Wrinkled |
| 7 | Seed colour | Yellow | Green |
Mendel proposed that something is passed on, unchanged, from parents to offsprings over successive generations, and called it factors. These factors are now known as genes, which are units of inheritance and contain information that is required to express a particular trait. Genes which code for a pair of contrasting traits are called alleles (alternative form of a gene that is located at a specific position on a specific chromosome). When the alleles of a pair are same, they are called homozygous. When the alleles of a pair are different; one masks the expression of other, is termed as heterozygous. The allele which expresses itself even in the presence of another allele is called dominant, and is represented by the first capital letter of the trait. For example; in case of a tall pea plant, dominant allele is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>𝐓</mi></mrow></math>. On the other hand, an allele that fails to express itself in the presence of a dominant allele is called recessive. It is represented by the small letter of the corresponding dominant trait. For example, the dwarf pea plant is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>𝐭</mi></mrow></math>.
Punnett square (devised by R.C. Punnett) is used to predict the outcome of a breeding experiment. It gives the probability for genotypes (genetic makeup) and not the phenotypes (actual expressed traits or morphological characteristics).
## Mendel's Monohybrid cross
Mendel crossed tall and dwarf pea plants (Parental or P generation) to study the inheritance of one gene/single trait. He collected seeds as a result of this cross and grew them to generate plants of first hybrid generation called Filial progeny or F1 generation. Mendel found that all the F1 progeny plants were tall; they all resembled to one of their parents and none were dwarf. He then self-pollinated the F1 progeny and found that some of the plants were dwarf. To his surprise, the trait which was absent in F1 generation reappeared in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="bold">F</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">2</mn></mrow></mrow></math> generation. The proportion of tall plants was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> and the dwarf plants were 1/4. All plants were either tall or dwarf and none in between; there was no blending of characteristics in the progeny. The ratios of tall to dwarf plants for F2 generation were 3:1 (phenotypic ratio) and 1:2:1 (genotypic ratio). Mendel found similar results for other traits of the pea plants.
## Mendel's Dihybrid cross
Mendel also crossed two plants that differed in two traits. For example, he crossed seeds with round shaped and yellow color (RRYY) to the seeds with wrinkled shape and green color (rryy). Yellow color and round shape are dominant traits while green color and wrinkled shape were recessive characters. In F1 generation, both yellow and green seeds as well as round and wrinkled seeds were present in 3:1 ratio. When F1 generation was self-pollinated, the phenotypes round, yellow; wrinkled, yellow; round, green and wrinkled, green appeared in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mn mathvariant="bold">9</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">3</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">3</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">1</mn></mrow></mrow></math>. Such a ratio was also observed for several other pairs of characters that Mendel studied.
Fig. Dihybrid cross
## Think Out of the Box
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation is 7520 . What will be the actual number of the following plants?
(ii) Heterozygous plants with flowers in axial position.
CASE - II : In a particular plant, two genes control leaf shape and color. Round leaves (R) are dominant over jagged leaves (r). Yellow fruits ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>Y</mi></mrow></math> ) are dominant over white fruits (y). A true bred yellow-fruited plant with rounded leaves is crossed with true bred white fruited plant with jagged leaves. What are the genotypes of the plants involved in this cross?
A true bred plant is the one that produces offspring with the same traits, when self-fertilised. True-breeding organisms have identical alleles for specified traits with the parents of opposite phenotypes. So, the genotypes involved in this type of a cross will be RRYY x rryy.
CASE - III : In a dihybrid cross between pod color (yellow and green) and shape (inflated and constricted), what will be the proportion of the progeny will have yellow color and constricted shape of a pod?
The phenotypic ratio of a dihybrid cross is 9:3:3:1. So, the proportion of the progeny having yellow color and constricted shape of a pod will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></math>.
Q. 1 If 400 plants are produced in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation of Mendel's dihybrid cross, what will be the number of non-parental phenotypes among them?
Q. 2 Scientist crossed the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation of true bred parents for flower color (violet and white) and position (axial and terminal). The number of offsprings obtained in
(i) Plants with white flowers in axial position.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Mendel's Laws of Inheritance
Mendel's observations of his experiments led to the principles or laws:
1. Law of Dominance - It states that dominant alleles are always repressed by recessive alleles. Hence, the cross between homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive plants will always result in dominant phenotype with heterozygous genotype.
2. Law of Segregation: According to this law, for any particular trait, the pair of alleles of each parent separate and only one allele passes from each parent to an offspring. Paternal allele is inherited as a matter of chance (we now know that segregation of alleles occurs during the process of meiosis).
3. Law of Independent Assortment: This is also known as 'Inheritance law'. According to this law, different pairs of alleles are passed to offsprings independently of each other. Today, we know this is due to the fact that the genes for independently assorted traits are located on different chromosomes.
## Learn More
Back cross - A cross of F1 hybrid with one of the parents.
Test cross - It is a type of back cross where F1 hybrid is crossed with homozygous recessive parents. It is called a 'test cross' because it is used to make sure whether the dominant character is due to homozygous or heterozygous genotype.
A test cross conducted for a single pair of contrasting traits results in a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>.

## How are Characteristics (or traits) Transmitted to Progeny
Genes are responsibe for the traits of organisms. The characteristics or traits of parents are transmitted to their progeny through genes present on their chromosomes during the process of sexual reproduction. Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid (DNA) carries the genetc information.
## Learn More
- Mendelian trait is controlled by a single locus and shows simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. In such a case, a mutation in a single gene can cause a disease that is inherited according to Mendel's law. e.g. sickle cell anemia.
- According to the latest findings there are nearly 30,000 genes in humans, collectively called the genome.
- Genes are located on chromosomes and they determine the hereditary characteristics in organisms.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA)
Structure of DNA was determined by X-ray diffraction studies. A double helix model of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1953. They suggested that:
(i) Each DNA molecule consists of two polynucleotide chains.
(ii) The chains are helically coiled around a common axis.
(iii) Each chain is complementary to another one.
(iv) There are 10 bases per turn of helix and one complete turn of helix is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3.4</mn><mi>\AA</mi></mrow></math>.
(v) The diameter of the DNA molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mi>\AA</mi></mrow></math>.
The DNA molecule is a polymer consisting of several thousand pairs of nucleotide monomers. Each nucleotide consists of the pentose sugar deoxyribose a phosphate group and a nitrogenous base which may be either purine or pyrimidine. The two purines are Adenine (A) and guanine (G). The two pyrimidines are thymine (T) and Cytosine (C).
Deoxyribose and a nitrogeneous base together form a nucleoside. A nucleoside and a phosphate together form a nucleotide.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">[</mo><mtable><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Nucleoside </mtext><mo>=</mo><mtext> Deoxyribose </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Nitrogenous base </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Nucleotide </mtext><mo>=</mo><mtext> Deoxyribose </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Nitrogenous base </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Phosphate </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">]</mo></mrow></mrow></math>
Deoxyribose is a five carbon sugar molecule, which forms the phosphate backbone of the DNA molecule.
Genes are arranged linearly along long chains of DNA sequence, called chromosomes. The DNA of the chromosome is associated with proteins that organise, compact and control the access to the DNA, forming a material called chromatin. In eukaryotes, chromatin is composed of nucleosomes - segments of DNA wound around histone protein. The complete set of hereditary material in an organism i.e., the combined DNA sequences of all chromosomes is called genome.
Haploid organisms have only one copy of each chromosome while diploid organisms (most animals and many plants) contain two of each chromosome i.e., they have two copies of every gene. The two alleles for a gene are located on identical loci of sister chromatid, each allele inherited from a different parent. Thus each gamete or germ cell has one chromosome from each pair which may be either maternal or paternal. When the two germ cells combine, the normal number of chromosomes is restored in the progeny. This ensures the stability of chromosome number and DNA of a species.
Fig. Structure of Chromosome
## How do genes control traits?
Genes correspond to regions with DNA, a molecule composed of 4 types of nucleotides. The sequence of these nucleotides contains the informations that organisms inherit. DNA is a double stranded structure, each strand acts as a template for creating a new complementary strand.
The sequence of nucleotides in a gene is translated by cells to produce a chain of amino acids, creating proteins. The order of amino acids in a protein corresponds to the order of nucleotides in the gene. This relationship between nucleotide sequence and amino acid sequence is known as genetic code. The amino acids in a protein determine how it folds into a three-dimensional shape. This structure is, in turn, responsible for the protein's function. Proteins carry out almost all the functions needed for cells to live. A change in the DNA can change amino acid it codes and thus, changing its shape and function. This can have a serious effect in the cell and on the organism as a whole e.g., sickle-cell anaemia, which is caused due to change in a single nucleotide.
Fig. DNA
## Sex Determination
## Sex determination is a biological system that determines the development of sexual characters in an organism.
Most sexual organisms have two sexes: Males and females. Genetically, both the sexes have different alleles or even different genes that specify their sexual morphology. In humans, the total chromosome complement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>46</mn><mi>;</mi><mn>44</mn></mrow></math> of which are autosomes while 2 distinct chromosomes are the sex chromosomes, which determine the sex of an organism and various sex-linked characteristics.
In humans, sex is predetermined in the sperm gamete. The egg gamete mother cell is said to be homogametic because all its cells possess the XX sex chromosomes, sperm gametes are said to be heterogametic because around half of them contain the X -chromosome and others possess the Y -chromosome to compliment the first X -chromosome.
There are two possibilities that can occur during fertilisation between male and female gametes, ( XX and XY ).
All offsprings will inherit an X chromosome from their mother regardless of whether they are boys or girls. Thus, the sex of the children will be determined by what they inherit from their father.
Fig. Sex determination in human beings by Checker Board Method
Many sex-determination systems exist. In a number of insects including grasshoppers, cockroaches and crickets, there is a variation where the females have two copies of the sex chromosome (XX), but males have only one (XO). The 'O' denotes the absence of the second sex chromosome. In some species of reptiles like turtles, the sex is determined by the temperature at which the egg is incubated. In tropical clown fish, the dominant individual in a group becomes female while the others are male. Some species have no sex determination system. Certain species of snails and common earthworm are hermaphrodite. A few species of insects, fishes and reptiles reproduce by parthenogenesis and are females only.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Karyotype
It is the entire chromosome complement of an individual or cell. A karyotype includes the detailed morphological structure of each chromosome of the set. In man, 23 pairs of homologous chromosomes are present.
A diagram depicting the arrangement of homologous chromosomes in the decreasing order of their size is called idiogram. Or a pictorial depiction of karyotype is called idiogram.
## EVOLUTION
Evolution is the product of two opposing forces- Processes that constantly introduce variation in traits, and processes that make particular variants become more common or rare. The main cause of variation is mutation, gene flow or reshuffling of genes in sexual reproduction. Natural selection occurs and only few individuals in each generation will survive, as resources are limited and organisms produce more offsprings than their environment can support. Over many generations, mutations produce small random changes in traits which are filtered by Natural Selection and the beneficial changes are retained. This causes adjustment of traits so that they become suited to an organism's environment. These adjustments are called adaptations. However, not every trait is an adaptation.
Evolution: A gradual development of more complex species from pre-existing simpler forms.
## Organic Evolution
The word evolution has been derived from the Latin word evoluere which means 'to unroll'. It can be defined as the gradual unfolding of organisms from pre existing ones through change. The origin of life is inseparably associated with evolution. In the following section, you will study about evolution of the living organisms called organic evolution.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Lamarckism
Jean Baptist Lamarck (1744-1829) tried to explain the principle behind the process of evolution in the book "Philosophie Zoologique". Lamarck proposed the "Theory of inheritance of acquired characters". According to this theory-
(a) Environment keeps on changing and thus, influence the organism by creating new needs. The new needs are met with by producing new structures. The changed environment put certain old organs out of use.
(b) A constant use of an organ results in its better devlopment whereas disuse leads to its ultimate degeneration and loss.
(c) The modifications produced during the whole life of an organism are transferred to its offspring. As the process is continued, after several generations, a new form (species) will be formed.
## Darwin's Theory of Evolution
Charles Darwin (1809-1882) gave this theory in his book entitled. 'The origin of Species'. Darwin's theory presumes "descent with modification" i.e., complex creatures evolve from more simplistic ancestors over time. Genetic mutations occur within an organism's genetic code; the beneficial mutations are preserved because they help in survival, a process known as "Natural Selection". These beneficial mutations are passed on to the next generation. Over the time, beneficial mutations accumulate and the result is an entirely different organism.
Fig. Charles Robert Darwin
Darwin found that some fossils resembled present day forms while others were different, though related. Plants and animals of different places were different, but they had the same type of climate. In Galapagos islands, he fould different species of finches (sparrow-like birds) having varied forms of beaks, size and food habits. All of them were related to finches of mainland. Darwin was fascinated by the creation of large number of breeds of animals by human efforts. He, therefore concluded that variations occur in organisms. Out of these variations, the ones suited to organisms were selected by nature and are inherited. On accumulation, they produce new species. These conclusions were publised in 1859 in his book "Origin of species by Means of Natural Selection. The Preservation of Races in the struggle of Life". These books popularly called 'Origin of Species'.
Theory of Natural Selection (Darwin, 1859): This theory states that organic evolution occurs through natural selection and accumulation of inheritable variations which provide structural and functional superiority to some individuals over others in their survival and reproduction.
The important features of this theory are as follows :
(i) Reproduction. Organisms have a very high reproductive potential, producing a very large number of offsprings.
(ii) Limited food and space. There is a limitation of food and space an earth. Therefore, populations of different species cannot increase beyond a certain limit.
(iii) Struggle for existence. There is a competition amongst organisms to obtain optimum resources. The competition is maximum amongst members of the same species, i.e., intraspecific competition.
(iv) Variations. A large number of variations occur in size, structure, physiology and behaviour of individuals of a species. The variations are of three types-useful, neutral and harmful.
(v) Natural Selection (Survival of the Fittest). In the struggle for existence, only those individuals survive and reproduce which have the most useful variations.
(vi) Inheritance of useful Variations. Individuals with useful variations reproduce and transfer the variations to the next generation. Next generation repeats the process of formation of new variations and natural selection, a process called a continuous selection.
(vii) Formation of new species. Accumulation of variations produces a completely new species.
| | Acquired Traits | Inherited Traits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Development: The traits develop during lifetime of an individual. | The traits are obtained from the parents. |
| 2. | Nature: They are somatic variations. | They are genetic variations. |
| 3. | Cause: Acquired traits develop due to direct effect of an environment. | The traits develop due to mutations and reshuffling of genetic material. |
| 4. | They die with the death of the individual Ex. Muscular body of a wrestler. | They are passed on to the next generation. Ex. Fused and free earlobes. |
Micro-evolution : Refers to small but significant changes that alter the common characteristics of an organism in the same species.
## How does Evolution Occur ?
Darwin's Theory of Natural Selection has been divided into five parts to explain clearly how evolution happens in nature.
1. Organisms produce a large number of offsprings.
2. Every individual must struggle to survive as there are not enough resources.
3. There are variations within a species.
4. Some variations allow members of a species to survive and reproduce better than others.
5. Organisms that survive and reproduce pass their traits to their offsprings and therefore the helpful traits gradually appear in more and more of the population.
This example of how brown hares in polar region became white hares will demonstrate each of the above points.
(a) There were brown hares living in cold, snowy polar region. Many of them were killed and eaten by other animals very few survived.
(b) Hares were competing for life, trying to avoid predators to survive.
(c) As a result, a few albino (white) hares were born due to mutation in the genes.
(d) This variation helped albino hares to hide. Predators had a hard time finding the albino hares in the snow and hence they were able to survive and reproduce.
(e) In the following generations, more white hares survived and reproduced resulting in an entire population of white hare. The albino hares have adapted to fit better in their environment than the brown hares. This is called Natural Selection. Natural selection is the phenomenon where nature selects traits favourable to the species in its environment.
Now, if we examine the fifth point in the example, survival of more albino hare in the population changed the colour characteristic from normal brown to white, over a period of time. This change took place only in a small population of hare living in cold and snowy regions i.e., there is an accidental change or mutation in the genes in a small population, even if this change gives no survival advantage to the possessor. This phenomenon is called Genetic Drift and it is a random genetic change in a population. The elimination of the genes of certain traits from the small population, when a section of the species population dies of natural reasons or imigrates to other region, is called Genetic drift. It alters the gene frequency of the remaining population.
## Speciation
The origin of new species from the existing one due to reproductive isolation of a part of its population is called Speciation. The geographical isolation make sub-populations become more and more different from each other due to geographical barriers. Ultimately, reproductive isolation occurs between individuals of these two groups i.e., they will no longer be able to exchange genetic material between them. At this point, natural selection works on the genetic variation within that population and results in formation of few species.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Four types of speciation occur in nature. They are:
1. Allopatric speciation: Population splits into two geographically isolated regions and each population undergoes genotypic and/or phenotypic divergence. e.g., Darwin's finches
2. Peripatric speciation: New species are formed in isolated small peripheral populations that are prevented from exchanging genes with the main population. Genetic drift plays an important role here e.g., underground mosquito is a variant of Culex pipiens.
3. Parapatric speciation: Two populations are partially seperated geographically, so individuals of each species may come in contact with the other from time to time, but reduced fitness of the heterozygote prevents breeding between the two species. e.g., Ensoltina salamanders.
4. Sympatric speciation refers to the formation of two or more descendant species from a single ancestral species all occupying the same geographical location. e.g., cichlids in East Africa.
| | Allopatric | Peripatric | Parapatric | Sympatric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Original Population | | | | |
| Initial step of speciation | | | | |
| Evolution by reproductive isolation | | | | |
| New distinct species | | | | |
Fig. : Types of speciation
## Artificial Selection
Artificial selection is the process by which man selects traits which are useful to him for improving the qualities of domesticated plants and animals. New species have been created by domesticated Animal Husbandry e.g., reproductive isolation leads to differences in the species of fruit flies after eight generations using different food types.
Hybridisation between two different species sometimes leads to a distinct phenotype. For e.g. Mariana mallard.
The individual having the desired traits are selected and allowed to interbreed among themselves. This process of artificial selection when repeated for many generations produces a new breed. For example many useful plants like wheat, rice, pulses and vegetables are produced through this method. The plants like cabbage, cauliflower, broccoli kholrabi and kale are all formed from a wild cabbage species.
| S. No. | Natural Selection | Artificial Selection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | It is a natural phenomenon | It is an artificial process. |
| 2. | It occurs in nature on a vast scale. | It is controlled by man on a limited scale in a specific environment. |
| 3. | Traits selected for evolution are beneficial to the species. | Traits selected for improvement are beneficial to man. |
| 4. | Results are achieved over a long period of time. | Results are achieved in a short period of time. |
Table: Comparison between Natural and Artificial selection.
CASE - I : Some plants of the grass species (Anthoxanthum odoratum) which live in contaminated soil are tolerant of heavy metals, while others that inhabit the non-polluted soil are not. Which type of speciation has occurred in this species of grass? This is a case of parapatric speciation as two different populations have formed due to an extreme change in their habitat.
CASE-II : Two separate species of squirrel inhabit north and south rims of Arizona's Grand Canyon. This is an example of which type of speciation?
It is an example of Allopatric speciation as in this case, geographical barrier is the driving force for speciation.
## Think Out of the Box
## CASE - IV : Why is sympatric speciation common in bacteria?
Sympatric speciation can result in two different groups that eventually become so genetically different from one another that they can no longer breed with each other. Since, bacteria has the ability to exchange genetic material with organisms other than parents or offspring, they can undergo sympatric speciation.
## Evidences for Evolution
Different organisms show common features which provides evidence in favour of evolution i.e., they can be considered to have been evolved from common ancestor. The more the number of features the organisms/species have in common, the more closely they are related. The more closely they are related, the more recently they would have had a common ancestor. The following sources provide evidence for evolution :
(i) Homologous Organs : These are organs having same structure, but may be similar or different in functions. Example, forelimbs of man (arm or hand), whale (flipper or paddle), bird (wings), lizard and frog. All have the same selection but modified for various functions. This indicates that they all have inherited a common plan from a common ancestor.
Wing of Bird
Fore Limb of House Lizard
(ii) Analogous organs : These are organs of different origin and basic plan, but similar in functions. Example, wings bat and wings of bird. The former are basically skin folds stretched between elongated fingers and the latter are a feathery covering all along the arm. So the designs of two wings are very different and hence analogous.
Fig. : Homologous Organs
Fig. Analogous Organs
| | Homologous Organs | Analogous Organs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Same structure but different functions. | Same function but different underlying structure. |
| 2 | Examples: Wing of a bat, lateral fin of a whale, and the human arm; they all have similar structures, but they carry out different functions. | Examples: Bat's wing and fly's wing; they both are used to help the creature fly, but they have different patterns in bone structure. |
| 3 | Homologous structures mean they had come from a common ancestor, supports divergent evolution. | The similarity of the function also reflects that they are adapted to a similar environment, supports convergent evolution. |
In man, there are about 180 vestigial organs, out of which more than 90 are visible.
