## (iii) Vestigial Organs
These organs occurr as reduced and functionless structures in body. It is considered that vestigial organs were functional in remote ancestors, but became vestigial and functionless due to their disuse in course of evolution.
Fig. Some vestigial structures in Human beings
(iv) Connecting Links : These are animals possessing characters of two different groups. Echidna and Platypus are connecting links between reptiles and mammals. They indicate that mammals have evolved from reptiles. Likewise, Archeopteryx the connecting link between reptile and birds.
Fig. Archaeopteryx
(v) Embryology : The embryology of different vertebrates provides very strong evidences favouring organic evolution. The early embryos of different vertebrates show striking similarities. This indicates common origin and ancestory of the different vertebrates. On this basis, Ernest Haeckel proposed the law named "Biogenetic Law". According to this law the embryonic development of any organism repeats complete evolutionary history
Fig. Comparative Embryology (Early development stages of fish, bird and human)
Molecular phylogeny: Molecular phylogeny states that distantly related organisms accumulate greater number of differences in their DNA. Studies on these differences help in tracing out the evolutionary relationships among the organisms. This relationship among different organisms shown by molecular phylogeny matches the recent classification systems.
Evolution through stages: Evolution is a complex process and hence cannot take place by a single DNA change. It occurs in many stages and over many years of time. During the course of evolution, even the intermediate stages can be useful to some extent. For example the evolution of complex organ 'eyes' took place in stages. One of the intermediate stages; the rudimentary eyes; was also useful for some organisms. The adaptation at different stages was very popular in this case. Eyes are found in insects, octopus as well as vertebrates but vary greatly in structure.

## Fossils
Fossils are organic remains of plants and animals that existed long ago. An entire organism or a part of its body was buried in the sediments of earth resulting in the formation of fossils. The study of fossils indicates the structural features of organisms that are not alive and their comparison with the existing forms which gives an indication as to how evolution has taken place. Fossils are found deeply embedded in sedimentary rocks. Most recent rock layers are on top, the oldest fossils are found in the deepest rocks. In many places, old rocks have become exposed due to erosion and weathering.
Sedimentary rocks are not the only source of fossils. Insects have been found in amber, while the entire woolly mammoths have been found to be preserved in ice.
Fig. Fossil formation
By arranging fossils in the order in which they have appeared on earth many fossil series have been established. Such series demonstrate how various forms of life have changed gradually over a period of millions of years. As a result, the evolution of a number of animals such as horse, camel and elephant, has been clearly understood. Plenty of fossil evidence is now available so as to establish human evolution too.
Learn More
Dating of fossils is the process of finding out the age of fossil. The most effective way of dating is to measure the amount of radioactive material in the rock or soil. Half-life periods are calculated on the basis of the ratio of the radio active material and its products of decay. The most commonly used radioactive materials are uranium and carbon -14 .
## Human Evolution
The evolutionary sequence from earliest known human ancestors is not a straight line but is a 'tree' with many dead ends. As the fossil evidences are limited, the scientists are unable to tell about number of human species that have existed and which was ancestor of the others. The 'tree' of human evolution provides a simple guide to relationship but does not clearly indicate ancestry.
- Paranthropus
- Australopithecus
- Homo habilus
- Homoerectus
- Homo neanderthals
- Homo sapiens
Paranthropus was strongly built "man-apes" that lived in southern and eastern Africa between three and one million years ago. Australopithecus are thought to be the earliest hominids i.e., human-like people. They had a small brain and projecting jaw. They stood upright and walked on two feet. This is known from their leg bones and backbone, and also from the footprints found in Tanzania ( 3.7 million years old).
Homo habilus or the "handyman" is the earliest known species of Homo (genes to which the modern day human belongs). It lived in the woodlands and Savannahs of Africa. It had a brain size of 650-800 ml. It made and used simple stone tools, and was a successful forager and scavenger.
Homo erectus was the first human to leave Africa and move to Europe and Asia. It had a sloping forehead, flattish face and brain size between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>850</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>1100</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow></mrow></math>. These humans exploited more habitats than their ancestors. They were the first ones to use fire. Homo neanderthals were the first humans to have adopted to life in cold climates of Europe and Asia. They had strong physique and large brain. They wore clothes, made a range of tools and used fire to keep warm. They were the first humans to bury their dead. Homo sapiens or the "modern human" first evolved in Africa. They had a large brain, were considerably intelligent and had developed the ability to use language. Modern man took control of their surroundings as they developed agriculture, societies and technology.
Fig. Human Evolutionary Tree
## Origin of life on Earth
Darwin's theory of evolution tells us how life evolved from simple to more complex forms and Mendel's experiments give us the mechanism for the inheritance of traits from one generation to the next. But neither tells us anything about how life began on earth in the first place.
Haldane's Theory - J.B.S. Haldane, a British scientist (who became a citizen of India later), suggested in 1929 that life must have developed from the simple inorganic molecules which were present on earth soon after it was formed. He speculated that the conditions on earth at that time, which were far from the conditions we see today, could have given rise to more complex organic molecules that were necessary for life. The first primitive organisms would arise from further chemical synthesis.
How did these organic molecules arise?
Miller and Urey's Experiment - An answer was suggested by the experiment conducted by Stanley L. Miller and Harold C. Urey in 1953. They assembled an atmosphere similar to that thought to exist on early earth (this had molecules like ammonia, methane and hydrogen sulphide, but no oxygen) over water. This was maintained at a temperature just below <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mn>100</mn><mrow><mo>∘</mo></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></mrow></math> and sparks were passed through the mixture of gases to simulate lightning. At the end of a week, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>15</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the carbon (from methane) had been converted to simple compounds of carbon including amino acids which make up protein molecules.
## Evolution Vs Progress
Is evolution of species a progress to higher, more complex and intelligent forms, as often thought?
If one sees the tree of human evolution, one can see that there are multiple branches at each and every stage of evolution. Speciation does not intend to convey the dying out of an inferior species giving way to superior ones. Species adapt to the conditions available in their environment. Some older and simpler species live on quite effectively. Scorpion is one of the most primitive invertebrate which has lived successfully for millions of years; it can withstand heat and nuclear radiation levels that would easily kill man.
The process of evolution in nature has resulted in every organism reaching a certain stage by various processes. The same process has brought man to the stage of a human being and considers him as a specific kind of species distinct from other ones. In fact, there is no real progress in the idea of evolution. It simply implies the generation and shaping of diverse life forms by environmental selection. The only progressive trend in evolution seems to be that more and more complex body designs have emerged over time.

## Sex Determination
1. Read the following and attempt any four questions from 1(i) to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mi>v</mi><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>.
Most human chromosomes have a maternal and a paternal copy, and we have 22 such pairs. But one pair, called the sex chromosomes, is odd in not always being a perfect pair. Women have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes, both called X . But men have a mismatched pair in which one is a normal-sized X while the other is a short one called Y . So women are XX , while men are XY .
(i) If a normal cell of human body contains 46 pairs of chromosomes then the numbers of chromosomes in a sex cell of a human being is most likely to be:
(a) 60
(b) 23
(c) 22
(d) 40
(ii) Which of the following determines the sex of a child?
(a) The length of the mother's pregnancy
(b) The length of time between ovulation and copulation
(c) The presence of an X chromosome in an ovum
(d) The presence of a Y chromosome in a sperm
(iii) In human males, all the chromosomes are paired perfectly except one. These unpaired chromosomes are:
(a) Large chromosome
(b) Small chromosome
(c) Y chromosome
(d) X chromosome
(iv) The process where characteristics are transmitted from parent to offsprings is called:
(a) Variation
(b) Heredity
(c) Gene
(d) Allele
(e) None of the above
(v) Who have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes?
(a) Girls only
(b) Boys only
(c) Both girls and boys
(d) It depends on many other factors

## Long Answer Questions:
1. Evolution is the sequence of gradual changes which takes place in the primitive organisms over millions of years and new species are produced.
Darwin's theory of Evolution: Charles Robert Darwin gave the theory of evolution in his famous book, 'The Origin of Species'.
It known as 'The Theory of Natural Selection' (Darwinism).
## According to Darwin's theory of evolution :
(i) There is natural variation within any population and some individuals have more favourable variations than others.
(ii) Population remains fairly constant even though all species produce a large number of offsprings.
(iii) This is due to 'competition' or struggle for existence between same and different species.
(iv) The struggle for survival within population eliminates the unfit individuals and those with 'favourable variations' survive and pass on these variations to their progeny to continue. This is called natural selection.
(v) The favourable variations are accumulated over a long time period leading to the origin of a new species.
2. The main causes of evolution are as follows:
(i) Mutations are random, undirected changes in DNA composition. Although most mutations are neutral or harmful to the organism, some prove advantageous in certain environments. Mutations are rare and do not change allele frequencies very much, but they provide the raw material for evolution.
(ii) In gene flow between populations, if the alleles that a migrant carries are different from those in the population from which the migrant comes or to which it migrates, allele frequencies will change.
(iii) In any population, chance events kill or prevent reproduction by some of the individuals. If the population is small, chance events may eliminate a disproportionate number of individuals who bear a particular allele, thereby greatly changing the allele frequency in the population. This change is termed as a genetic drift.
(iv) Many organisms do not mate randomly. If only certain members of a population can mate, then the next generation of organisms in the population will all be offspring of this selected group, whose allele frequencies may differ from those of the population as a whole. Population bottleneck and founder effect, two types of genetic drift, illustrate the consequences of small population size on allele frequency.
3. Dihybrid cross is a cross between two different lines/ genes that differ in two observed traits. In the name "Dihybrid cross", the "di" indicates that there are two traits involved (e.g. R and Y ), the "hybrid" means that each trait has two different alleles (e.g. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></math>, or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>Y</mi></mrow></math> and y), and "cross" means that there are two individuals (usually a mother and father) who are combining or "crossing" their genetic information.
The Dihybrid cross is easy to visualize using a Punnett square of dimensions 16:
| | (RP) | (Ry) | (Y) | (1y) | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (RY) | RRYY Round Yellow | RRYy Round Yellow | RRYy Round Yellow | RrYy Round Yellow | Round Yellow - 9 |
| Ry | RRYy Round Yellow | RRyy Round Green | RrYy Round Yellow | Rryy Round Green | Round Green - 3 Wrinkled Yellow - 3 |
| (Y) | RrYy Round Yellow | RrYy Round Yellow | rrYY Wrinkled Yellow | rr Yy Wrinkled Yellow | Wrinkled Green - 1 |
| (IV) | RrYy Round Yellow | Rryy Round Green | rrYy Wrinkled Yellow | rr yy Wrinkled Green | Recombinants - 6/16 |
For genes on separate chromosomes, each allele pair showed independent segregation. If the first filial generation ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation) produces four identical offspring, the second filial generation, which occurs by crossing the members of the first filial generation, shows a phenotypic (appearance) ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>9</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>.
4. (i) Speciation is the process of formation of a new species from a pre-existing one.
(ii) Four factors that could lead to speciation are:
(a) Genetic drift.
(b) Mutation/Drastic change in the genes of DNA
(c) Natural selection
(d) Geographical isolation
(iii) Geographical isolation cannot be a major factor in the speciation of a self-pollinating plant species.
(iv) Reason is physical barrier cannot be created in self-pollinating plants.
5. (i) Mendel experimented on garden pea plant with selection of seven visible contrasting characters forming laws of inheritance. He selected and crossed homozygous tall pea plant with genotype TT and a homozygous dwarf pea plant with the genotype <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mo>.</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation consists only of tall plants having genotype Tt.
The expressed allele ' T ' for tallness is dominant over the unexpressed allele <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></math> for dwarfness. Therefore, the trait of tallness is dominant while dwarfness is the recessive trait. Thus, Mendel's experiment showed that traits may be dominant or recessive.
(ii) In Mendel's experiment, different traits were tall and dwarf plant, round and wrinkled seeds. In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> (second) generation, some plants were tall with round seeds and others were dwarf with wrinkled seeds. Other combination was dwarf plants having round/wrinkled seed traits, that were independently inherited.
## Reasoning Based Questions:
1. The traits acquired during the life-time of an individual not inherited because certain changes occur in the structure or function of the body parts due to environmental factors which cannot be passed on to the DNA of the germ cells. The traits are only inherited if changes take place in the genetic material. Therefore, the experiences of an individual during its lifetime cannot be passed on to its progeny, and cannot direct evolution.
2. A species is a group of organisms that are capable of interbreeding to produce a fertile offspring. Skin colour, looks, and size are all variety of features present in human beings. These features are genetic but also environmentally controlled. Various human races are formed based on these features. All human races have more than enough similarities to be classified as same species. Therefore, all human beings are a single species as humans of different colour, size, and looks are capable of reproduction and can produce a fertile offspring.
3. The wings of insects is analogous to the wing of bird. They both are analogous because they perform the same function, but are not similar in structural plan and developmental origin.
Yes, the wing of bat is also analogous to the wing of bird. Because the wing of bat and that of bird are built on the same plan, i.e., same in structure and origin.
4. The reason for the appearance of new combinations in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation is given by the law of independent assortment. The law of independent assortment states that,
(i) When two pairs of traits are combined in a hybrid, one pair of character segregates independent of the other pair of character.
(ii) In a dihybrid cross between two plants having round yellow (RRYY) and wrinkled green seeds (rryy), four types of gametes (RY, Ry, rY, ry) are produced. Each of these segregate independent of each other, each having a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the total gametes produced.

## Text - Book Questions:
1. In asexual reproduction, cell division results in the creation of similar cells with the identical copies of DNA. Therefore, in such species, a trait may come into existence due to random mutation induced by various environmental factors. It can be observed that the induced trait occurs in few species which is then inherited by future generations, on replication over a period of time.
Thus, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of a population contains this trait, it means that there members have been replicating themselves for a longer period of time than those in the population where the trait is seen in only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the population. Thus, trait B is likely to have arisen earlier.
2. A variation is the deviation in characters of an individual from the group to which it belongs or deviation in characters of the offspring from those of the parents. The survival of the variation depends upon its nature. Depending on the nature of variations, different individuals would have different kinds of advantage. These variations may lead to the advantages of the individuals due to positive adaptation of the trait. This is how the creation of variations in a species promotes survival of the species.

Analogous organs: Organs similar in functions but different in structure and origin are known as analogous organs.

(b) The correct option is (b) because herbicide resistant weeds, drug resistant eukaryotes and man-created breeds of domesticated animals like dogs are examples of evolution by anthropogenic action. Darwin's Finches of Galapagos islands are example of natural selection, adaptive radiation and founder's effect.
24. (d) The correct option is (d) because flippers of Penguins and Dolphins are an example of analogous organs. Analogous structures are a result of convergent evolution.
25. (d) Embryological support for evolution was disapproved by Karl Ernst von Baer, he noted that embryos never pass through the adult stages of other animals during embryonic development.
26. (c) Experimental verification of the chromosomal theory of inheritance was done by Morgan. Sutton and Boveri proposed chromosomal theory of inheritance but it was experimentally verified by T.H. Morgan.
## (CONCEPT MAP

key_points.text: Does random fertilization contribute to genetic variation?
Why is genetic variation significant?
What is the relationship between meiosis and genetic variation?
Random fertilization promotes random combination of gametes resulting in genetically unique zygote and hence variation.
Genetic variation helps in survival of species to a changing environment or natural selection. It also forms the basis of evolution.
Crossing over occurs during meiosis which may result in genetic variation.
Observe the eye color of all your friends.
Carefully note the eye color of their siblings and parents.
Correlate the eye color of your friends and their siblings to their respective parents.
Based on this observation, try to suggest an inheritance pattern of eye color in humans.
Heterozygous plants with flowers in axial position.
Plants with white flowers in axial position.

Law of Dominance - It states that dominant alleles are always repressed by recessive alleles. Hence, the cross between homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive plants will always result in dominant phenotype with heterozygous genotype.

Law of Segregation: According to this law, for any particular trait, the pair of alleles of each parent separate and only one allele passes from each parent to an offspring. Paternal allele is inherited as a matter of chance (we now know that segregation of alleles occurs during the process of meiosis).

Law of Independent Assortment: This is also known as 'Inheritance law'. According to this law, different pairs of alleles are passed to offsprings independently of each other. Today, we know this is due to the fact that the genes for independently assorted traits are located on different chromosomes.
(Punette square)

Mendelian trait is controlled by a single locus and shows simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. In such a case, a mutation in a single gene can cause a disease that is inherited according to Mendel's law. e.g. sickle cell anemia.
According to the latest findings there are nearly 30,000 genes in humans, collectively called the genome.
Genes are located on chromosomes and they determine the hereditary characteristics in organisms.
Each DNA molecule consists of two polynucleotide chains.

theory_sections.markdown:
## HEREDITY
Transmission of characters (resemblances as well as variations) from parents to the offspring from one generation to another is called heredity.
Although the offsprings inherit characters (or traits) of the parents and resemble them very closely, but the resemblance is not identical in all respects. The offsprings are never a true copy of the parents. In fact, no two individuals are exactly alike and the members of any one species differ from one another in some traits. The differences in the traits shown by the individuals of a species, and also by the offsprings of the same parents, are referred to as variations.
For example, in human beings, few people have hanging earlobes called free earlobe. In others, the earlobe is closely attached to the side of the head and is called closely attached earlobe. So free earlobes and attached earlobes are the two variations found in humans.
Fig. Variation in earlobe

## Accumulation of Variation during Reproduction :
We see different organisms around us. They all look different as species differ from each other. But when you look for differences among individuals of the same species, no two individuals are identical to each other. We acquire a common body design along with subtle changes. For example, a bacterium produces two bacteria by asexual reproduction. One of the offspring bacterium has a variation due to which it has higher tolerance of heat. This characteristic of heat resistance will go on accumulating in the offsprings of successive generations of this bacterium, and this will ultimately give rise to a variant of bacteria which will be highly heat resistant and would be able to survive even at very high temperatures. The advantage of variation to a species is that it increases the chance of its survival in a changing environment.
Only minor differences will be generated due to small inaccuracies in DNA copying mechanism. The diversity is quite high in case of sexually reproducing organisms because of reshuffling of traits during gamete formation and chance fusion of gametes during fertilisation. If the DNA comes from two parents, the differences will be pronounced. These differences will be passed on to the offsprings and would create diversity.
Fig. Creation of diversity over succeeding generations. The original organism at the top will give rise to, say, two individuals, similar in body design, but with subtle differences. Each of them, in turn, will give rise to two individuals in the next generation. Each of the four individuals in the bottom row will be different from each other. While some of these differences will be unique, others will be inherited from their respective parents, who were different from each other. Variations arise either due to an environmental influence or genetic factors like recombination of genes, mutation etc. The environmental factors influence the phenotype of individuals and these are not heritable. Genetic traits affect the genes and are heritable and important from evolutionary perspective.
Few examples of genetic traits in human beings and their variations are-
| S.No. | Genetic Trait | Variations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Earlobes | Free, Attached |
| 2 | Forelock | White, Not white |
| 3 | Dimples | Dimples, No dimples |
| 4 | Thumb | Straight, curved |
| 5 | Pinky finger | Bent, straight |
| 6 | Mid-digit hair | Mid-digit hair, No mid-digit hair |
| 7 | Toes | 2nd toe longer than big toe, 2nd toe shorter than big toe |
| 8 | Tongue rolling | Rolling up edges, Not rolling |
1. Does random fertilization contribute to genetic variation?
2. Why is genetic variation significant?
3. What is the relationship between meiosis and genetic variation?

theory_sections.topics: Rules for the Inheritance of Traits: Mendel's Contribution
theory_sections.markdown:
## Rules for the Inheritance of Traits: Mendel's Contribution
The study of genetics was started by Gregor Johann Mendel, an Austrian Monk, in 1850s. However, his studies were not recognised by the world till after his death. Mendel is known as 'Father of Genetics'.
Before Mendel, although people knew that parents with blue eyes would get blue eyed children, etc., they did not know the exact mechanism of transfer of these traits from the parents to the offspring. They did not know that there is a physical basis of heredity. This was identified by Mendel through his pioneering work in pea plant (Pisum sativum). He blended his knowledge of science and mathematics and was the first one to keep count of individuals exhibiting a particular trait in each generation.
Fig. Gregor Johann Mendel

## Mendel's experimental material.
Mendel picked common garden pea plant for his experiment as they can be grown easily in large numbers and their reproduction can be manipulated. Pea plants have both male and female reproductive organs and they can either self-pollinate themselves or can cross-pollinate with another plant. Mendel was able to selectively cross-pollinate pure bred plants with particular traits and observe the outcome over many generations.

## Learn More
Geneticists today carry out breeding experiments with species that reproduce much more rapidly so that the amount of time and money required is reduced. Fruit flies and bacteria are commonly used for this purpose now. Fruit flies reproduce in about 2 weeks from birth, while bacteria E.coli reproduce in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></math> hours.
Through selective cross-breeding of pea over several generations, Mendel discovered that certain traits show up in offsprings without any blending of parent characteristics. Seven traits were observed by Mendel in pea plant and these could occur in one of the two forms. (dominant or recessive)
Fig. Pisum Sativum
Fig. Pisum Sativum
| S. No. | Character | Dominant | Recessive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stem height | Tall | Dwarf |
| 2 | Flower colour | violet | White |
| 3 | Flower position | Axial | Terminal |
| 4 | Pod shape | Inflated | Constricted |
| 5 | Pod colour | Green | Yellow |
| 6 | Seed shape | Round | Wrinkled |
| 7 | Seed colour | Yellow | Green |
Mendel proposed that something is passed on, unchanged, from parents to offsprings over successive generations, and called it factors. These factors are now known as genes, which are units of inheritance and contain information that is required to express a particular trait. Genes which code for a pair of contrasting traits are called alleles (alternative form of a gene that is located at a specific position on a specific chromosome). When the alleles of a pair are same, they are called homozygous. When the alleles of a pair are different; one masks the expression of other, is termed as heterozygous. The allele which expresses itself even in the presence of another allele is called dominant, and is represented by the first capital letter of the trait. For example; in case of a tall pea plant, dominant allele is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>𝐓</mi></mrow></math>. On the other hand, an allele that fails to express itself in the presence of a dominant allele is called recessive. It is represented by the small letter of the corresponding dominant trait. For example, the dwarf pea plant is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>𝐭</mi></mrow></math>.
Punnett square (devised by R.C. Punnett) is used to predict the outcome of a breeding experiment. It gives the probability for genotypes (genetic makeup) and not the phenotypes (actual expressed traits or morphological characteristics).

## Mendel's Monohybrid cross
Mendel crossed tall and dwarf pea plants (Parental or P generation) to study the inheritance of one gene/single trait. He collected seeds as a result of this cross and grew them to generate plants of first hybrid generation called Filial progeny or F1 generation. Mendel found that all the F1 progeny plants were tall; they all resembled to one of their parents and none were dwarf. He then self-pollinated the F1 progeny and found that some of the plants were dwarf. To his surprise, the trait which was absent in F1 generation reappeared in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="bold">F</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">2</mn></mrow></mrow></math> generation. The proportion of tall plants was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> and the dwarf plants were 1/4. All plants were either tall or dwarf and none in between; there was no blending of characteristics in the progeny. The ratios of tall to dwarf plants for F2 generation were 3:1 (phenotypic ratio) and 1:2:1 (genotypic ratio). Mendel found similar results for other traits of the pea plants.

## Mendel's Dihybrid cross
Mendel also crossed two plants that differed in two traits. For example, he crossed seeds with round shaped and yellow color (RRYY) to the seeds with wrinkled shape and green color (rryy). Yellow color and round shape are dominant traits while green color and wrinkled shape were recessive characters. In F1 generation, both yellow and green seeds as well as round and wrinkled seeds were present in 3:1 ratio. When F1 generation was self-pollinated, the phenotypes round, yellow; wrinkled, yellow; round, green and wrinkled, green appeared in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mn mathvariant="bold">9</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">3</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">3</mn><mi mathvariant="bold">:</mi><mn mathvariant="bold">1</mn></mrow></mrow></math>. Such a ratio was also observed for several other pairs of characters that Mendel studied.
Fig. Dihybrid cross

## Think Out of the Box
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation is 7520 . What will be the actual number of the following plants?
(ii) Heterozygous plants with flowers in axial position.
CASE - II : In a particular plant, two genes control leaf shape and color. Round leaves (R) are dominant over jagged leaves (r). Yellow fruits ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>Y</mi></mrow></math> ) are dominant over white fruits (y). A true bred yellow-fruited plant with rounded leaves is crossed with true bred white fruited plant with jagged leaves. What are the genotypes of the plants involved in this cross?
A true bred plant is the one that produces offspring with the same traits, when self-fertilised. True-breeding organisms have identical alleles for specified traits with the parents of opposite phenotypes. So, the genotypes involved in this type of a cross will be RRYY x rryy.
CASE - III : In a dihybrid cross between pod color (yellow and green) and shape (inflated and constricted), what will be the proportion of the progeny will have yellow color and constricted shape of a pod?
The phenotypic ratio of a dihybrid cross is 9:3:3:1. So, the proportion of the progeny having yellow color and constricted shape of a pod will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></math>.
Q. 1 If 400 plants are produced in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation of Mendel's dihybrid cross, what will be the number of non-parental phenotypes among them?
Q. 2 Scientist crossed the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation of true bred parents for flower color (violet and white) and position (axial and terminal). The number of offsprings obtained in
(i) Plants with white flowers in axial position.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Mendel's Laws of Inheritance
Mendel's observations of his experiments led to the principles or laws:
1. Law of Dominance - It states that dominant alleles are always repressed by recessive alleles. Hence, the cross between homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive plants will always result in dominant phenotype with heterozygous genotype.
2. Law of Segregation: According to this law, for any particular trait, the pair of alleles of each parent separate and only one allele passes from each parent to an offspring. Paternal allele is inherited as a matter of chance (we now know that segregation of alleles occurs during the process of meiosis).
3. Law of Independent Assortment: This is also known as 'Inheritance law'. According to this law, different pairs of alleles are passed to offsprings independently of each other. Today, we know this is due to the fact that the genes for independently assorted traits are located on different chromosomes.

## Learn More
Back cross - A cross of F1 hybrid with one of the parents.
Test cross - It is a type of back cross where F1 hybrid is crossed with homozygous recessive parents. It is called a 'test cross' because it is used to make sure whether the dominant character is due to homozygous or heterozygous genotype.
A test cross conducted for a single pair of contrasting traits results in a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>.
CROSS: Heterozygous tall (male) Homozygous dwarf (female)
F1 generation:
(Punette square)
| | T | t |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| t | Tt | tt |
| t | Tt | tt |
Genotype: Tt : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>
Phenotype: Tall ( Tt ) : Dwarf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>
A test cross conducted for two pairs of contrasting traits results in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
