| Phenotypes | Round Yellow | Round Green | Wrinkled Yellow | Wrinkled Green |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Proportions | <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> | <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> | <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> | <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> |

theory_sections.topics: How are Characteristics (or traits) Transmitted to Progeny
theory_sections.markdown:
## How are Characteristics (or traits) Transmitted to Progeny
Genes are responsibe for the traits of organisms. The characteristics or traits of parents are transmitted to their progeny through genes present on their chromosomes during the process of sexual reproduction. Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid (DNA) carries the genetc information.

## Learn More
- Mendelian trait is controlled by a single locus and shows simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. In such a case, a mutation in a single gene can cause a disease that is inherited according to Mendel's law. e.g. sickle cell anemia.
- According to the latest findings there are nearly 30,000 genes in humans, collectively called the genome.
- Genes are located on chromosomes and they determine the hereditary characteristics in organisms.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA)
Structure of DNA was determined by X-ray diffraction studies. A double helix model of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1953. They suggested that:
(i) Each DNA molecule consists of two polynucleotide chains.
(ii) The chains are helically coiled around a common axis.
(iii) Each chain is complementary to another one.
(iv) There are 10 bases per turn of helix and one complete turn of helix is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3.4</mn><mi>\AA</mi></mrow></math>.
(v) The diameter of the DNA molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mi>\AA</mi></mrow></math>.
The DNA molecule is a polymer consisting of several thousand pairs of nucleotide monomers. Each nucleotide consists of the pentose sugar deoxyribose a phosphate group and a nitrogenous base which may be either purine or pyrimidine. The two purines are Adenine (A) and guanine (G). The two pyrimidines are thymine (T) and Cytosine (C).
Deoxyribose and a nitrogeneous base together form a nucleoside. A nucleoside and a phosphate together form a nucleotide.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">[</mo><mtable><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Nucleoside </mtext><mo>=</mo><mtext> Deoxyribose </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Nitrogenous base </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Nucleotide </mtext><mo>=</mo><mtext> Deoxyribose </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Nitrogenous base </mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext> Phosphate </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">]</mo></mrow></mrow></math>
Deoxyribose is a five carbon sugar molecule, which forms the phosphate backbone of the DNA molecule.
Genes are arranged linearly along long chains of DNA sequence, called chromosomes. The DNA of the chromosome is associated with proteins that organise, compact and control the access to the DNA, forming a material called chromatin. In eukaryotes, chromatin is composed of nucleosomes - segments of DNA wound around histone protein. The complete set of hereditary material in an organism i.e., the combined DNA sequences of all chromosomes is called genome.
Haploid organisms have only one copy of each chromosome while diploid organisms (most animals and many plants) contain two of each chromosome i.e., they have two copies of every gene. The two alleles for a gene are located on identical loci of sister chromatid, each allele inherited from a different parent. Thus each gamete or germ cell has one chromosome from each pair which may be either maternal or paternal. When the two germ cells combine, the normal number of chromosomes is restored in the progeny. This ensures the stability of chromosome number and DNA of a species.
Fig. Structure of Chromosome

## How do genes control traits?
Genes correspond to regions with DNA, a molecule composed of 4 types of nucleotides. The sequence of these nucleotides contains the informations that organisms inherit. DNA is a double stranded structure, each strand acts as a template for creating a new complementary strand.
The sequence of nucleotides in a gene is translated by cells to produce a chain of amino acids, creating proteins. The order of amino acids in a protein corresponds to the order of nucleotides in the gene. This relationship between nucleotide sequence and amino acid sequence is known as genetic code. The amino acids in a protein determine how it folds into a three-dimensional shape. This structure is, in turn, responsible for the protein's function. Proteins carry out almost all the functions needed for cells to live. A change in the DNA can change amino acid it codes and thus, changing its shape and function. This can have a serious effect in the cell and on the organism as a whole e.g., sickle-cell anaemia, which is caused due to change in a single nucleotide.
Fig. DNA
## Sex Determination

theory_sections.topics: Sex determination is a biological system that determines the development of sexual characters in an organism.
theory_sections.markdown:
## Sex determination is a biological system that determines the development of sexual characters in an organism.
Most sexual organisms have two sexes: Males and females. Genetically, both the sexes have different alleles or even different genes that specify their sexual morphology. In humans, the total chromosome complement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>46</mn><mi>;</mi><mn>44</mn></mrow></math> of which are autosomes while 2 distinct chromosomes are the sex chromosomes, which determine the sex of an organism and various sex-linked characteristics.
In humans, sex is predetermined in the sperm gamete. The egg gamete mother cell is said to be homogametic because all its cells possess the XX sex chromosomes, sperm gametes are said to be heterogametic because around half of them contain the X -chromosome and others possess the Y -chromosome to compliment the first X -chromosome.
There are two possibilities that can occur during fertilisation between male and female gametes, ( XX and XY ).
All offsprings will inherit an X chromosome from their mother regardless of whether they are boys or girls. Thus, the sex of the children will be determined by what they inherit from their father.
Fig. Sex determination in human beings by Checker Board Method
Many sex-determination systems exist. In a number of insects including grasshoppers, cockroaches and crickets, there is a variation where the females have two copies of the sex chromosome (XX), but males have only one (XO). The 'O' denotes the absence of the second sex chromosome. In some species of reptiles like turtles, the sex is determined by the temperature at which the egg is incubated. In tropical clown fish, the dominant individual in a group becomes female while the others are male. Some species have no sex determination system. Certain species of snails and common earthworm are hermaphrodite. A few species of insects, fishes and reptiles reproduce by parthenogenesis and are females only.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Karyotype
It is the entire chromosome complement of an individual or cell. A karyotype includes the detailed morphological structure of each chromosome of the set. In man, 23 pairs of homologous chromosomes are present.
A diagram depicting the arrangement of homologous chromosomes in the decreasing order of their size is called idiogram. Or a pictorial depiction of karyotype is called idiogram.

## EVOLUTION
Evolution is the product of two opposing forces- Processes that constantly introduce variation in traits, and processes that make particular variants become more common or rare. The main cause of variation is mutation, gene flow or reshuffling of genes in sexual reproduction. Natural selection occurs and only few individuals in each generation will survive, as resources are limited and organisms produce more offsprings than their environment can support. Over many generations, mutations produce small random changes in traits which are filtered by Natural Selection and the beneficial changes are retained. This causes adjustment of traits so that they become suited to an organism's environment. These adjustments are called adaptations. However, not every trait is an adaptation.
Evolution: A gradual development of more complex species from pre-existing simpler forms.

## Organic Evolution
The word evolution has been derived from the Latin word evoluere which means 'to unroll'. It can be defined as the gradual unfolding of organisms from pre existing ones through change. The origin of life is inseparably associated with evolution. In the following section, you will study about evolution of the living organisms called organic evolution.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Lamarckism
Jean Baptist Lamarck (1744-1829) tried to explain the principle behind the process of evolution in the book "Philosophie Zoologique". Lamarck proposed the "Theory of inheritance of acquired characters". According to this theory-
(a) Environment keeps on changing and thus, influence the organism by creating new needs. The new needs are met with by producing new structures. The changed environment put certain old organs out of use.
(b) A constant use of an organ results in its better devlopment whereas disuse leads to its ultimate degeneration and loss.
(c) The modifications produced during the whole life of an organism are transferred to its offspring. As the process is continued, after several generations, a new form (species) will be formed.

## Darwin's Theory of Evolution
Charles Darwin (1809-1882) gave this theory in his book entitled. 'The origin of Species'. Darwin's theory presumes "descent with modification" i.e., complex creatures evolve from more simplistic ancestors over time. Genetic mutations occur within an organism's genetic code; the beneficial mutations are preserved because they help in survival, a process known as "Natural Selection". These beneficial mutations are passed on to the next generation. Over the time, beneficial mutations accumulate and the result is an entirely different organism.
Fig. Charles Robert Darwin
Darwin found that some fossils resembled present day forms while others were different, though related. Plants and animals of different places were different, but they had the same type of climate. In Galapagos islands, he fould different species of finches (sparrow-like birds) having varied forms of beaks, size and food habits. All of them were related to finches of mainland. Darwin was fascinated by the creation of large number of breeds of animals by human efforts. He, therefore concluded that variations occur in organisms. Out of these variations, the ones suited to organisms were selected by nature and are inherited. On accumulation, they produce new species. These conclusions were publised in 1859 in his book "Origin of species by Means of Natural Selection. The Preservation of Races in the struggle of Life". These books popularly called 'Origin of Species'.
Theory of Natural Selection (Darwin, 1859): This theory states that organic evolution occurs through natural selection and accumulation of inheritable variations which provide structural and functional superiority to some individuals over others in their survival and reproduction.
The important features of this theory are as follows :
(i) Reproduction. Organisms have a very high reproductive potential, producing a very large number of offsprings.
(ii) Limited food and space. There is a limitation of food and space an earth. Therefore, populations of different species cannot increase beyond a certain limit.
(iii) Struggle for existence. There is a competition amongst organisms to obtain optimum resources. The competition is maximum amongst members of the same species, i.e., intraspecific competition.
(iv) Variations. A large number of variations occur in size, structure, physiology and behaviour of individuals of a species. The variations are of three types-useful, neutral and harmful.
(v) Natural Selection (Survival of the Fittest). In the struggle for existence, only those individuals survive and reproduce which have the most useful variations.
(vi) Inheritance of useful Variations. Individuals with useful variations reproduce and transfer the variations to the next generation. Next generation repeats the process of formation of new variations and natural selection, a process called a continuous selection.
(vii) Formation of new species. Accumulation of variations produces a completely new species.
| | Acquired Traits | Inherited Traits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Development: The traits develop during lifetime of an individual. | The traits are obtained from the parents. |
| 2. | Nature: They are somatic variations. | They are genetic variations. |
| 3. | Cause: Acquired traits develop due to direct effect of an environment. | The traits develop due to mutations and reshuffling of genetic material. |
| 4. | They die with the death of the individual Ex. Muscular body of a wrestler. | They are passed on to the next generation. Ex. Fused and free earlobes. |
Micro-evolution : Refers to small but significant changes that alter the common characteristics of an organism in the same species.

## How does Evolution Occur ?
Darwin's Theory of Natural Selection has been divided into five parts to explain clearly how evolution happens in nature.
1. Organisms produce a large number of offsprings.
2. Every individual must struggle to survive as there are not enough resources.
3. There are variations within a species.
4. Some variations allow members of a species to survive and reproduce better than others.
5. Organisms that survive and reproduce pass their traits to their offsprings and therefore the helpful traits gradually appear in more and more of the population.
This example of how brown hares in polar region became white hares will demonstrate each of the above points.
(a) There were brown hares living in cold, snowy polar region. Many of them were killed and eaten by other animals very few survived.
(b) Hares were competing for life, trying to avoid predators to survive.
(c) As a result, a few albino (white) hares were born due to mutation in the genes.
(d) This variation helped albino hares to hide. Predators had a hard time finding the albino hares in the snow and hence they were able to survive and reproduce.
(e) In the following generations, more white hares survived and reproduced resulting in an entire population of white hare. The albino hares have adapted to fit better in their environment than the brown hares. This is called Natural Selection. Natural selection is the phenomenon where nature selects traits favourable to the species in its environment.
Now, if we examine the fifth point in the example, survival of more albino hare in the population changed the colour characteristic from normal brown to white, over a period of time. This change took place only in a small population of hare living in cold and snowy regions i.e., there is an accidental change or mutation in the genes in a small population, even if this change gives no survival advantage to the possessor. This phenomenon is called Genetic Drift and it is a random genetic change in a population. The elimination of the genes of certain traits from the small population, when a section of the species population dies of natural reasons or imigrates to other region, is called Genetic drift. It alters the gene frequency of the remaining population.

## Speciation
The origin of new species from the existing one due to reproductive isolation of a part of its population is called Speciation. The geographical isolation make sub-populations become more and more different from each other due to geographical barriers. Ultimately, reproductive isolation occurs between individuals of these two groups i.e., they will no longer be able to exchange genetic material between them. At this point, natural selection works on the genetic variation within that population and results in formation of few species.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

theory_sections.topics: Four types of speciation occur in nature. They are:
theory_sections.markdown:
## Four types of speciation occur in nature. They are:
1. Allopatric speciation: Population splits into two geographically isolated regions and each population undergoes genotypic and/or phenotypic divergence. e.g., Darwin's finches
2. Peripatric speciation: New species are formed in isolated small peripheral populations that are prevented from exchanging genes with the main population. Genetic drift plays an important role here e.g., underground mosquito is a variant of Culex pipiens.
3. Parapatric speciation: Two populations are partially seperated geographically, so individuals of each species may come in contact with the other from time to time, but reduced fitness of the heterozygote prevents breeding between the two species. e.g., Ensoltina salamanders.
4. Sympatric speciation refers to the formation of two or more descendant species from a single ancestral species all occupying the same geographical location. e.g., cichlids in East Africa.
| | Allopatric | Peripatric | Parapatric | Sympatric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Original Population | | | | |
| Initial step of speciation | | | | |
| Evolution by reproductive isolation | | | | |
| New distinct species | | | | |
Fig. : Types of speciation

## Artificial Selection
Artificial selection is the process by which man selects traits which are useful to him for improving the qualities of domesticated plants and animals. New species have been created by domesticated Animal Husbandry e.g., reproductive isolation leads to differences in the species of fruit flies after eight generations using different food types.
Hybridisation between two different species sometimes leads to a distinct phenotype. For e.g. Mariana mallard.
The individual having the desired traits are selected and allowed to interbreed among themselves. This process of artificial selection when repeated for many generations produces a new breed. For example many useful plants like wheat, rice, pulses and vegetables are produced through this method. The plants like cabbage, cauliflower, broccoli kholrabi and kale are all formed from a wild cabbage species.
| S. No. | Natural Selection | Artificial Selection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | It is a natural phenomenon | It is an artificial process. |
| 2. | It occurs in nature on a vast scale. | It is controlled by man on a limited scale in a specific environment. |
| 3. | Traits selected for evolution are beneficial to the species. | Traits selected for improvement are beneficial to man. |
| 4. | Results are achieved over a long period of time. | Results are achieved in a short period of time. |
Table: Comparison between Natural and Artificial selection.
CASE - I : Some plants of the grass species (Anthoxanthum odoratum) which live in contaminated soil are tolerant of heavy metals, while others that inhabit the non-polluted soil are not. Which type of speciation has occurred in this species of grass? This is a case of parapatric speciation as two different populations have formed due to an extreme change in their habitat.
CASE-II : Two separate species of squirrel inhabit north and south rims of Arizona's Grand Canyon. This is an example of which type of speciation?
It is an example of Allopatric speciation as in this case, geographical barrier is the driving force for speciation.
## Think Out of the Box

theory_sections.topics: CASE - IV : Why is sympatric speciation common in bacteria?
theory_sections.markdown:
## CASE - IV : Why is sympatric speciation common in bacteria?
Sympatric speciation can result in two different groups that eventually become so genetically different from one another that they can no longer breed with each other. Since, bacteria has the ability to exchange genetic material with organisms other than parents or offspring, they can undergo sympatric speciation.

## Evidences for Evolution
Different organisms show common features which provides evidence in favour of evolution i.e., they can be considered to have been evolved from common ancestor. The more the number of features the organisms/species have in common, the more closely they are related. The more closely they are related, the more recently they would have had a common ancestor. The following sources provide evidence for evolution :
(i) Homologous Organs : These are organs having same structure, but may be similar or different in functions. Example, forelimbs of man (arm or hand), whale (flipper or paddle), bird (wings), lizard and frog. All have the same selection but modified for various functions. This indicates that they all have inherited a common plan from a common ancestor.
Wing of Bird
Fore Limb of House Lizard
(ii) Analogous organs : These are organs of different origin and basic plan, but similar in functions. Example, wings bat and wings of bird. The former are basically skin folds stretched between elongated fingers and the latter are a feathery covering all along the arm. So the designs of two wings are very different and hence analogous.
Fig. : Homologous Organs
Fig. Analogous Organs
| | Homologous Organs | Analogous Organs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Same structure but different functions. | Same function but different underlying structure. |
| 2 | Examples: Wing of a bat, lateral fin of a whale, and the human arm; they all have similar structures, but they carry out different functions. | Examples: Bat's wing and fly's wing; they both are used to help the creature fly, but they have different patterns in bone structure. |
| 3 | Homologous structures mean they had come from a common ancestor, supports divergent evolution. | The similarity of the function also reflects that they are adapted to a similar environment, supports convergent evolution. |
In man, there are about 180 vestigial organs, out of which more than 90 are visible.
RKnow?

## (iii) Vestigial Organs
These organs occurr as reduced and functionless structures in body. It is considered that vestigial organs were functional in remote ancestors, but became vestigial and functionless due to their disuse in course of evolution.
Fig. Some vestigial structures in Human beings
(iv) Connecting Links : These are animals possessing characters of two different groups. Echidna and Platypus are connecting links between reptiles and mammals. They indicate that mammals have evolved from reptiles. Likewise, Archeopteryx the connecting link between reptile and birds.
Fig. Archaeopteryx
(v) Embryology : The embryology of different vertebrates provides very strong evidences favouring organic evolution. The early embryos of different vertebrates show striking similarities. This indicates common origin and ancestory of the different vertebrates. On this basis, Ernest Haeckel proposed the law named "Biogenetic Law". According to this law the embryonic development of any organism repeats complete evolutionary history
Fig. Comparative Embryology (Early development stages of fish, bird and human)
Molecular phylogeny: Molecular phylogeny states that distantly related organisms accumulate greater number of differences in their DNA. Studies on these differences help in tracing out the evolutionary relationships among the organisms. This relationship among different organisms shown by molecular phylogeny matches the recent classification systems.
Evolution through stages: Evolution is a complex process and hence cannot take place by a single DNA change. It occurs in many stages and over many years of time. During the course of evolution, even the intermediate stages can be useful to some extent. For example the evolution of complex organ 'eyes' took place in stages. One of the intermediate stages; the rudimentary eyes; was also useful for some organisms. The adaptation at different stages was very popular in this case. Eyes are found in insects, octopus as well as vertebrates but vary greatly in structure.
1. How does migration affect natural selection?
2. Why 'biogenetic law' is also referred as 'recapitulation theory'?
3. What are the main drawbacks of Lamarckism?
4. True and False-
(a) Long neck of giraffe is an example of acquired inheritance.
(b) All organs which are not in use either degenerate or become vestigial with time.
(c) Natural selection is the only factor responsible for evolution.
(d) Darwin's theory of evolution is based on genetic basis of inheritance.

## Fossils
Fossils are organic remains of plants and animals that existed long ago. An entire organism or a part of its body was buried in the sediments of earth resulting in the formation of fossils. The study of fossils indicates the structural features of organisms that are not alive and their comparison with the existing forms which gives an indication as to how evolution has taken place. Fossils are found deeply embedded in sedimentary rocks. Most recent rock layers are on top, the oldest fossils are found in the deepest rocks. In many places, old rocks have become exposed due to erosion and weathering.
Sedimentary rocks are not the only source of fossils. Insects have been found in amber, while the entire woolly mammoths have been found to be preserved in ice.
Fig. Fossil formation
By arranging fossils in the order in which they have appeared on earth many fossil series have been established. Such series demonstrate how various forms of life have changed gradually over a period of millions of years. As a result, the evolution of a number of animals such as horse, camel and elephant, has been clearly understood. Plenty of fossil evidence is now available so as to establish human evolution too.
Learn More
Dating of fossils is the process of finding out the age of fossil. The most effective way of dating is to measure the amount of radioactive material in the rock or soil. Half-life periods are calculated on the basis of the ratio of the radio active material and its products of decay. The most commonly used radioactive materials are uranium and carbon -14 .

## Human Evolution
The evolutionary sequence from earliest known human ancestors is not a straight line but is a 'tree' with many dead ends. As the fossil evidences are limited, the scientists are unable to tell about number of human species that have existed and which was ancestor of the others. The 'tree' of human evolution provides a simple guide to relationship but does not clearly indicate ancestry.
- Paranthropus
- Australopithecus
- Homo habilus
- Homoerectus
- Homo neanderthals
- Homo sapiens
Paranthropus was strongly built "man-apes" that lived in southern and eastern Africa between three and one million years ago. Australopithecus are thought to be the earliest hominids i.e., human-like people. They had a small brain and projecting jaw. They stood upright and walked on two feet. This is known from their leg bones and backbone, and also from the footprints found in Tanzania ( 3.7 million years old).
Homo habilus or the "handyman" is the earliest known species of Homo (genes to which the modern day human belongs). It lived in the woodlands and Savannahs of Africa. It had a brain size of 650-800 ml. It made and used simple stone tools, and was a successful forager and scavenger.
Homo erectus was the first human to leave Africa and move to Europe and Asia. It had a sloping forehead, flattish face and brain size between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>850</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>1100</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow></mrow></math>. These humans exploited more habitats than their ancestors. They were the first ones to use fire. Homo neanderthals were the first humans to have adopted to life in cold climates of Europe and Asia. They had strong physique and large brain. They wore clothes, made a range of tools and used fire to keep warm. They were the first humans to bury their dead. Homo sapiens or the "modern human" first evolved in Africa. They had a large brain, were considerably intelligent and had developed the ability to use language. Modern man took control of their surroundings as they developed agriculture, societies and technology.
Fig. Human Evolutionary Tree

## Origin of life on Earth
Darwin's theory of evolution tells us how life evolved from simple to more complex forms and Mendel's experiments give us the mechanism for the inheritance of traits from one generation to the next. But neither tells us anything about how life began on earth in the first place.
Haldane's Theory - J.B.S. Haldane, a British scientist (who became a citizen of India later), suggested in 1929 that life must have developed from the simple inorganic molecules which were present on earth soon after it was formed. He speculated that the conditions on earth at that time, which were far from the conditions we see today, could have given rise to more complex organic molecules that were necessary for life. The first primitive organisms would arise from further chemical synthesis.
How did these organic molecules arise?
Miller and Urey's Experiment - An answer was suggested by the experiment conducted by Stanley L. Miller and Harold C. Urey in 1953. They assembled an atmosphere similar to that thought to exist on early earth (this had molecules like ammonia, methane and hydrogen sulphide, but no oxygen) over water. This was maintained at a temperature just below <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mn>100</mn><mrow><mo>∘</mo></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></mrow></math> and sparks were passed through the mixture of gases to simulate lightning. At the end of a week, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>15</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the carbon (from methane) had been converted to simple compounds of carbon including amino acids which make up protein molecules.

## Evolution Vs Progress
Is evolution of species a progress to higher, more complex and intelligent forms, as often thought?
If one sees the tree of human evolution, one can see that there are multiple branches at each and every stage of evolution. Speciation does not intend to convey the dying out of an inferior species giving way to superior ones. Species adapt to the conditions available in their environment. Some older and simpler species live on quite effectively. Scorpion is one of the most primitive invertebrate which has lived successfully for millions of years; it can withstand heat and nuclear radiation levels that would easily kill man.
The process of evolution in nature has resulted in every organism reaching a certain stage by various processes. The same process has brought man to the stage of a human being and considers him as a specific kind of species distinct from other ones. In fact, there is no real progress in the idea of evolution. It simply implies the generation and shaping of diverse life forms by environmental selection. The only progressive trend in evolution seems to be that more and more complex body designs have emerged over time.

## Sex Determination
1. Read the following and attempt any four questions from 1(i) to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mi>v</mi><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>.
Most human chromosomes have a maternal and a paternal copy, and we have 22 such pairs. But one pair, called the sex chromosomes, is odd in not always being a perfect pair. Women have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes, both called X . But men have a mismatched pair in which one is a normal-sized X while the other is a short one called Y . So women are XX , while men are XY .
(i) If a normal cell of human body contains 46 pairs of chromosomes then the numbers of chromosomes in a sex cell of a human being is most likely to be:
(a) 60
(b) 23
(c) 22
(d) 40
(ii) Which of the following determines the sex of a child?
(a) The length of the mother's pregnancy
(b) The length of time between ovulation and copulation
(c) The presence of an X chromosome in an ovum
(d) The presence of a Y chromosome in a sperm
(iii) In human males, all the chromosomes are paired perfectly except one. These unpaired chromosomes are:
(a) Large chromosome
(b) Small chromosome
(c) Y chromosome
(d) X chromosome
(iv) The process where characteristics are transmitted from parent to offsprings is called:
(a) Variation
(b) Heredity
(c) Gene
(d) Allele
(e) None of the above
(v) Who have a perfect pair of sex chromosomes?
(a) Girls only
(b) Boys only
(c) Both girls and boys
(d) It depends on many other factors

theory_sections.topics: According to Darwin's theory of evolution :
theory_sections.markdown:
## According to Darwin's theory of evolution :
(i) There is natural variation within any population and some individuals have more favourable variations than others.
(ii) Population remains fairly constant even though all species produce a large number of offsprings.
(iii) This is due to 'competition' or struggle for existence between same and different species.
(iv) The struggle for survival within population eliminates the unfit individuals and those with 'favourable variations' survive and pass on these variations to their progeny to continue. This is called natural selection.
(v) The favourable variations are accumulated over a long time period leading to the origin of a new species.
2. The main causes of evolution are as follows:
(i) Mutations are random, undirected changes in DNA composition. Although most mutations are neutral or harmful to the organism, some prove advantageous in certain environments. Mutations are rare and do not change allele frequencies very much, but they provide the raw material for evolution.
(ii) In gene flow between populations, if the alleles that a migrant carries are different from those in the population from which the migrant comes or to which it migrates, allele frequencies will change.
(iii) In any population, chance events kill or prevent reproduction by some of the individuals. If the population is small, chance events may eliminate a disproportionate number of individuals who bear a particular allele, thereby greatly changing the allele frequency in the population. This change is termed as a genetic drift.
(iv) Many organisms do not mate randomly. If only certain members of a population can mate, then the next generation of organisms in the population will all be offspring of this selected group, whose allele frequencies may differ from those of the population as a whole. Population bottleneck and founder effect, two types of genetic drift, illustrate the consequences of small population size on allele frequency.
3. Dihybrid cross is a cross between two different lines/ genes that differ in two observed traits. In the name "Dihybrid cross", the "di" indicates that there are two traits involved (e.g. R and Y ), the "hybrid" means that each trait has two different alleles (e.g. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></math>, or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>Y</mi></mrow></math> and y), and "cross" means that there are two individuals (usually a mother and father) who are combining or "crossing" their genetic information.
The Dihybrid cross is easy to visualize using a Punnett square of dimensions 16:
| | (RP) | (Ry) | (Y) | (1y) | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (RY) | RRYY Round Yellow | RRYy Round Yellow | RRYy Round Yellow | RrYy Round Yellow | Round Yellow - 9 |
| Ry | RRYy Round Yellow | RRyy Round Green | RrYy Round Yellow | Rryy Round Green | Round Green - 3 Wrinkled Yellow - 3 |
| (Y) | RrYy Round Yellow | RrYy Round Yellow | rrYY Wrinkled Yellow | rr Yy Wrinkled Yellow | Wrinkled Green - 1 |
| (IV) | RrYy Round Yellow | Rryy Round Green | rrYy Wrinkled Yellow | rr yy Wrinkled Green | Recombinants - 6/16 |
For genes on separate chromosomes, each allele pair showed independent segregation. If the first filial generation ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation) produces four identical offspring, the second filial generation, which occurs by crossing the members of the first filial generation, shows a phenotypic (appearance) ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>9</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>.
4. (i) Speciation is the process of formation of a new species from a pre-existing one.
(ii) Four factors that could lead to speciation are:
(a) Genetic drift.
(b) Mutation/Drastic change in the genes of DNA
(c) Natural selection
(d) Geographical isolation
(iii) Geographical isolation cannot be a major factor in the speciation of a self-pollinating plant species.
(iv) Reason is physical barrier cannot be created in self-pollinating plants.
5. (i) Mendel experimented on garden pea plant with selection of seven visible contrasting characters forming laws of inheritance. He selected and crossed homozygous tall pea plant with genotype TT and a homozygous dwarf pea plant with the genotype <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mo>.</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation consists only of tall plants having genotype Tt.
The expressed allele ' T ' for tallness is dominant over the unexpressed allele <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></math> for dwarfness. Therefore, the trait of tallness is dominant while dwarfness is the recessive trait. Thus, Mendel's experiment showed that traits may be dominant or recessive.
(ii) In Mendel's experiment, different traits were tall and dwarf plant, round and wrinkled seeds. In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> (second) generation, some plants were tall with round seeds and others were dwarf with wrinkled seeds. Other combination was dwarf plants having round/wrinkled seed traits, that were independently inherited.
## (CONCEPT MAP

topics.summary:
## ECOSYSTEM
The term Ecosystem was coined by Sir Arthur Tansley in 1935. Ecosystem is essentially a technical term for nature. Ecosystem can be defined as a self-sustaining structural and functional unit of the biosphere. This system depends upon the sun for its energy.
## Components of Ecosystem
An ecosystem has two basic components :-
(i) Abiotic components include all non-living components that affect the distribution, number, metabolism and behaviour of organisms in an ecosystem. These include
(a) Inorganic substances like carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, water, etc. These occur either in the form of compounds dissolved in water, in the soil or in free state in the air.
(b) Organic compounds like carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, nucleic acid, etc. These are present in living organisms and dead organic matter. Dead organic matter is broken down by the action of decomposers into inorganic substances for recycling.
(c) Climatic factors include light, temperature, humidity, wind, rainfall, water, sunlight and edaphic factors such as soil texture or topography.
(ii) Biotic components include the living components of the ecosystem.
The biotic components usually have
(a) Producers that convert light energy into chemical energy and then into food, e.g., plants.
(b) Consumers that depend upon producers for food e.g., animals and few plants.
(c) Decomposers that take the chemicals from producers and consumers and break them into simpler form so that they can be reused. e.g., fungi, bacteria, etc.
An ecosystem is a self-contained unit of living organisms (plants, animals and decomposers), and their non-living environment (soil, air and water). The examples of ecosystems are: a grassland (meadow); a forest; a desert; a mountain; a pond; a lake; a river; and sea. When we say that a forest is an ecosystem then it means the physical environment of the forest like soil, air and water alongwith all the plants and animals that occur in the forest.
Different ecosystems are in contact with one another. Though they are distinct, but exchange of materials can occur amongst them.
Ecosystem can be small (rotting piece of wood) or large (e.g., forest, ocean), temporary (e.g. is red pond) or permanent (e.g. lake), natural and artificial.
The study of an ecosystem is called Ecology. The term Ecology was introduced by a German biologist, Hannus Reiter (1868). Ecology (Oekologie) is derived from Greek words; Oikos meaning 'house' and logos meaning 'study'. Ecology is defined as a scientific study of plants and animals in relation to each other and to their environment. It is one of the disciplines of environmental science.
## Types of Ecosystem
Depending on the human interference and degree of the impact of authropogenic activities, ecosystem can be classified as-
1. Natural Ecosystem : These are self-regulating ecosystems without any direct human interference.
Natural ecosystem are of two types-
- Aquatic Ecosystem This ecosystem is found in water bodies which may be either fresh water or sea water. e.g., ponds, lakes, rivers (fresh water), estuarine, marine (salt water)
- Terrestrial ecosystems - This ecosystem occurs an land. They are of three types desert, grass land and forest.
2. Artifical ecosystem : These ecosystems are created and maintained by human beings. Agroecosystem is the largest manmade ecosystem and garden is a common artificial ecosystem maintained by man. Aquarium is also an example of an artificial ecosystem.
## Food Chain
Food in an ecosystem can be transferred from one organism to the other through food chain. The sequence of living organism in an ecosystem in which one organism consumes another organism to transfer food energy is called food chain.
Food chains are attempts to demonstrate the fate of organisms in an ecosystem.
Let us have a look at the ecosystem operating in a grassland. Here, is a lot of grass (producer) is eaten by small insects (consumer), and the insects are killed and eaten by a toad, which in turn is caught by a snake. The snake is then eaten by hawk. Traditionally, food chains are drawn with the producer at the bottom. Successive trophic levels are connected by arrows which lead from the organism being eaten to the organisms eating it. Although, decomposers play an important role in an ecosystem, they are usually not included. It is rare for a food chain to have more than five levels. Herbivores (plant eaters) are known as primary consumers; first-level carnivores (animal eaters) as secondary consumers; second-level carnivores as tertiary consumers; thirdlevel carnivores as quaternary consumers; and so on. Omnivores (both plant and animal eaters) can also be present at any trophic level of food chain after producers.
Grass
Producer
Secondary
Consumer
Snake
Tertiary
Consumer
Following are some more examples of food chain.
(i) grains → sparrows → cats
(ii) Algae, moss and food crumbs in the drains → cockroach → lizard → birds
(iii) grass → rabbit → hawks
(iv) bark of a tree → wood house → spider → shrew → fox
(v) cacti → ants/locusts → spiders → lizards → snakes
(vi) thorns → bushes → camel
1. Why are there more herbivores than carnivores in a food chain?
2. What is the 5th trophic level in a food chain?
3. What travels through a food chain?
## Trophic Level
The trophic level of an organism is the position it occupies in a food chain. Food chains start at trophic level 1 with primary producers, move to herbivores or omnivores at level 2, carnivores at level 3 and finish with apex predaters at level 4 or 5 . The food chain occurs on land as well as in water. Few examples are-
In the aquatic food chain, the phytoplankton is eaten by zooplankton. The zooplankton is eaten by the fish and the fishes are eaten by the seal. The predator seals are then eaten by the shark.
The interdependence of the populations within a food chain helps to maintain the balance of plant and animal populations within a community. For example, where there are too many giraffes, there will be insufficient trees and shrubs for them to eat. Many giraffes will starve and die. If there are fewer giraffes then food is available for the lions to eat and some lions will starve to death. When there are fewer lions, the giraffe population will explode. So, the predatory-prey population balance each other.
## Characteristics of Food Chain
1. It shows nutritive interaction between living organisms.
2. It is always straight.
3. There is a unidirectional flow of energy. (sun → producers → consumers)
4. There are usually 3-4 trophic levels in a food chain.
5. Organisms can occupy different positions in different food chains.
6. As per second law of thermodynamics, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>80</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>90</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of energy is lost as heat at each trophic level.
## Importance of Food Chain
1. It provides pathways for passage of food and its energy in the ecosystem.
2. It provides information about the living components of the ecosystem.
3. Food chains indicate the interactions and interdependence among the different organisms.
4. It helps in identifying the effect of decrease or increase in the population at a trophic level.
5. If a non-degradable chemical happens to enter a food chain at one level, it will undergo increase in concentration with rise of trophic levels (Biomagnification).
## Food Web
Food web is a network of food chains operating in an ecosystem which connects at various trophic levels to form a number of feeding connections amongst different organisms of the biotic community. Here, each organism is eaten by two to many other kinds of organisms which in turn are eaten by several other organisms. For example, deer is prey for jackal, wolf and tiger. Rabbit is prey for fox, jackal, hawk and a number of carnivores. Mouse is food for hawk, snake, fox and wild cat. Likewise, wolf can feed on fox, jackal, wild cats, deer, goat, sheep, etc. A rabbit can feed on a avariety of plants and does not starve if its favourite food becomes scarce. It begins to feed on alternate plants. Similarly, if the population of rabbit decreases, its predators begin to eat mice, shrews and squirrels. Meanwhile, rabbits increase in number and the balance of nature is restored. There are many food chains in a food web.
Fig. A simple terrestrial food web
Food web is further classified into two broad categories :
(i) Grazing web : This food web has plants or photosynthetic organisms at its base followed by herbivores/omnivores and carnivores.
(ii) Detrital web : This food web has decomposers e.g., bacteria or fungi at its base that feed on dead organic matter. These two food webs are often connected to each other. For e.g., mushrooms produced by decomposers in the detrital web become a food source for deer, squirrels, and mice in the grazing web.
## Characteristics of Food Web
1. Food webs are never straight as they are formed by interlinking of food chains.
2. Food web provides alternative pathways of food availability. For examples, if a particular species of producer is destroyed, the herbivore can feed on an alternative species.
3. Greater alternatives available in a food web makes the ecosystem more stable.
4. Food webs Reeps a check on overpopulation of species.
5. Food webs help in ecosystem development.

## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Ecological Pyramids
Charles Elton (1927) developed the concept of ecological pyramids. They are also called 'Eltonian pyramids'.
Ecological pyramids can be defined as graphical representation of ecological parameters like number of individuals or the amount of biomass or the amount of energy present at various trophic levels of a food chain, with producer forming the base and top carnivore at the apex.
An ecological pyramid may be upright (tapering towards the tip), or inverted (wide at the tip) or spindle shaped (broad in the middle and narrow above and below). On the basis of the parameters used, ecological pyramids are of three types:
(i) Pyramid of Numbers is a graphical representation of number of individuals at different trophic levels in a food chain in an ecosystem. The pyramid is upright in case of grassland and pond ecosystem where the number of organisms decrease from producer to top carnivore level. An inverted pyramid is seen in parasitic food chain where a single oak tree (producer) supports a large number of herbivorous birds followed by larger number of parasites and then even greater number of hyperparasites.
Fig. Pyramid of Numbers
(ii) Pyramid of Biomass is a graphical representation of biomass (total amount of organic matter in an ecosystem at any time) per unit area at different trophic levels. In a terrestrial ecosystem, the maximum biomass occurs in producers, and there is a progressive decrease in biomass from lower to higher trophic levels. Thus, the pyramid of biomass in a terrestrial ecosystem is upright. In an aquatic ecosystem, the pyramid of biomass is inverted or spindle shaped where the biomass of phytoplanktons (producers) is less as compared to the higher trophic levels.
Fig. Pyramid of Biomass
(iii) Pyramid of Energy is a graphical representation of the amount of energy trapped per unit time and area at different trophic levels of a food chain with producers forming the base and the top carnivores at the tip. Pyramid of energy is always upright as at each transfer, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>80</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>90</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the energy is used up and only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the energy is available to the next trophic level.
Fig. Pyramid of Energy
## Energy Transfer in an Ecosystem
Linderman (1942) put forth the 'Ten percent Law' for the transfer of energy from one trophic level to the next. According to this law, during the transfer of organic food from one trophic level to the next, only about ten percent of the organic matter is stored as flesh and the remaining is lost during transfer or used up in respiration.
Plants utilise sun energy for primary production and can store only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the utilised energy for the herbivores. When the plants are consumed by animals, about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the energy in the food is fixed and available for next trophic level i.e. the carnivores. When a carnivore consumes that animal, only about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of energy is fixed for the higher level. So, at each transfer 80-90\% of potential energy is dissipated as heat and only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of energy is available for the next trophic level.
Fig. Progressive loss of Energy in Food Chain

## Learn More
Biome - It is defined as the total assemblage of plant and animal species interacting within specific conditions. In simpler terms, it is basically a set of ecosystems sharing similar characteristics and occupying a major habitat. For example, forest, grassland, etc.
Biosphere - It includes all the living components of the earth. It consists of all plants, animals and micro organisms inhabiting the earth and their interactions with the environment.
Biogeochemical cycles - The cyclic movements of chemical elements of the biosphere between the organism and the environment are referred as biogeochemical cycles. The cycle starts by absorbing the chemical elements by the organism and is returned to the air, water and soil through decomposition. For example, gaseous (the main reservoirs of nutrients are atmosphere and ocean) and sedimentary cycles (reservoirs of nutrients are soil and rocks).
## IMPACT OF HUMAN ACTIVITIES
Human beings are an important part of environment. Our activities change the environment around us. And the change in the environment affects us. Let us see how?
## Pollution
Pollution is derived from a Latin word 'polluere' which means contamination. Pollution is defined as the introduction of substances (contaminants) into the natural environment that causes adverse changes. These substances that cause pollution are called as pollutants. Pollution can be broadly classified into following categories -
Air pollution - The contamination of the earth's atmosphere by the release of various harmful substances like chemicals, harmful gases or fumes etc. is called air pollution. The various sources of air pollution are burning of fossils fuels, release of harmful fumes/chemicals from the industries/factories and mining operations. It can cause various respiratory, cardiovascular and skin diseases in humans.
Water pollution - The contamination of water bodies by water pollutants (contaminants) is termed as water pollution. The various sources of water pollution are human and animal wastes, untreated industrial sewage, agricultural runoff containing pesticides and fertilizers etc. Sometimes it can be natural as in case of eutrophication (nutrient enrichment of water body resulting in oxygen depletion).
Soil Pollution - The contamination or degradation of soil by the presence of chemicals or manmade substances is termed as soil pollution. The various sources include acid rain, industrial waste disposal, agrochemicals (pesticides and fertilizers), oil spills etc. Noise Pollution - It is usually man made and is caused by the excessive amount of noise thereby disrupting natural balance. The various sources are social events, household noises, heavy machines in industries, noises from construction sites etc.
## CONNECTING TOPIC
## Global Warming
Scientist have determined that a number of human activities are contributing to global warming by adding excessive amounts of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>N</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow></mrow></math>. Carbon dioxide accumulate in the atmosphere and trap heat that normally would exit into outer space.
While many greenhouse gases occur naturally and are needed to create the greenhouse effect. They keep the earth warm enough to support life. The human use of fossil fuels is the main source of greenhouse gas emission. By driving cars, using electricity from coal-fired power plants, or heating our homes with oil or natural gas, we release carbon dioxide and other heat trapping gases into the atmosphere. Deforestation is another significant source of greenhouse gases as fewer trees convert less carbon
dioxide to oxygen.
During the 150 years of the industrial age, the atmospheric concentration of carbon dioxide has increased by 31 percent. Over the same period, the level of atmospheric methane has risen by 151 percent, mostly from agricultural activities such as raising cattle and growing rice.
As the concentration of greenhouse gases increases, more heat is trapped in the atmosphere and less escapes back into space. This increase in trapped heat changes the climate and alters weather patterns. This may affect species extinction, the length of seasons, and can cause natural disasters (floods, storms etc.).
## Few actions one can take to Reduce Global Warming
1. Reduce, Reuse, Recycle - Reduce waste by choosing reusable products instead of disposables. Recycle paper, plastic, newspaper, glass and aluminium cans. By recycling half of your household waste, you can save 2,400 pounds of carbon dioxide.
2. Use less heat and air conditioning - Proper insulation of the room can lower the heating or airconditioning cost by more than 25 percent. Setting the thermostat just 2 degrees lower in winter and higher in summer could save about 2,000 pounds of carbon dioxide each year.
3. Change a light bulb - Replace regular light bulbs with Compact Fluorescent Light (CFL) bulbs. CFLs last 10 times longer than incandescent bulbs, use two-third less energy, and give off 70 percent less heat.
4. Drive less and Drive smart - Lesser driving means fewer emissions. Explore the community mass transit system and check out options for car-pooling to work or school.
When you do drive, make sure your car is running efficiently. Every gallon of gas you save not only helps your budget but also keeps 20 pounds of carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere.
5. Buy energy-efficient products - Buy appliances which are energy efficient. Avoid products that come with excess packaging, especially plastic molded and other packaging that can't be recycled.
6. Use less hot water - Set the water heater at 120 degrees to save energy and make sure that it is well insulated. Wash clothes in warm or cold water to reduce the use of hot water and the energy required to produce it. This change alone can save atleast 500 pounds of carbon dioxide annually in most households. Use energy saving settings in your dishwasher and let the dishes air-dry.
7. Use the 'Off' switch - Save electricity and reduce global warming by turning off lights when you leave a room, and using only as much light as you need. Remember to turn off the television, music system and computer when you are not using them.
It is good idea to turn off water when you are not using it especially, while brushing your teeth, washing your vehicle, etc. In this way you'll reduce the water bill and also save energy needed to pump water to your house.
8. Plant a tree - Trees absorb carbon dioxide and give off oxygen during photosynthesis. Trees are an integral part of the natural atmospheric cycle, but very few are left to counter the increase in carbon dioxide caused by human activities. A single tree can absorb about one ton of carbon dioxide during its lifetime.
These steps will take you a long way towards reducing energy use and also your monthly budget. And less energy use means less dependence on the fossil fuels that create greenhouse gases and contribute to global warming.
## OZONE DEPLETION
About <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>90</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of earth's ozone is concentrated in the stratosphere. Stratospheric ozone protects the earth from potentially damaging doses of ultraviolet radiations. Its concentration is maximum at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>23</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>25</mn><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></math> above equator and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>11</mn><mo>−</mo><mn>18</mn><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mrow></math> above poles. This rich zone of ozone in the stratosphere is called ozone layer or ozonosphere. It is also called ozone shield because this region intercepts the ultraviolet radiations.
## Ozone layer
In the stratosphere, ozone is being photodissociated and generated simultaneously by the absorption of harmful ultraviolet radiations coming from the sun.
These two reactions are in equilibrium, hence maintaining a steady concentration of ozone in the stratosphere.
Thinning of ozone layer is called ozone depletion. With the technological advancement in the past century various man made substances have caused depletion of ozone from the stratosphere. Thinning of ozone was noticed in 1985 above the Antartica, by Farman and coworkers. In 1987, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in reaching an agreement control CFC production. The widely known substances which cause ozone depletion are :
- Refrigerants (CFCs-chloroflurocarbons and HCFs- hydrofluorocarbons)
- Insulating foams
- Solvents
- Aerosols
Primary health issues caused by ozone depletion are because of the easy penetration of ultraviolet rays from the sun. It has harmful effects on man, animals and plants:
- Sunburn
- Skin Aging
- Various forms of skin cancer.
- Eye problems like cataract, cornea damages, etc.
- Attacks immune system and makes humans prone to infections/diseases.
This is a global issue and environmentalists are working towards developing an effective strategy to check the thinning of ozone layer. The Helsinki Declaration in 1989 had majority of the nations pledge to phase out CFC by the year 2000. Efforts are still on and each one of us has to contribute to this.

## WASTE
Accumulation of wastes due to its improper disposal is a major problem in a country. Population in our country has been growing at a rapid rate. With this increase, there has been an increase in the amount of wastes being produced, especially in the cities. Every person on an average generates about 400 to 500 grams of wastes per day. At this rate, in a city of about 10 lakh people, around 500 tones of wastes is being produced per day.
In order to understand the problem and work towards a solution, one must know the types of wastes being generated. Waste can be classified into two categories :
(1) Biodegradable Wastes : These are the wastes which are broken down and disposed off naturally by saprophytes or decomposers. Common saprophytes are microscopic organisms or microbes like bacteria and fungi. They secrete enzymes which bring about the solubilisation of wastes. Biodegradable wastes are generally organic wastes, e.g., garbage, sewage, livestock waste, used tea leaves, waste paper, waste cotton, wool, leather, wood and jute articles, food leftovers, etc. Besides natural degradation by microbes, they can also be disposed off through waste treatment plants or converted into an agricultural resource of manure and compost. Biodegradable wastes should not be allowed to pile up anywhere as they attract wildlife.
(2) Non-biodegradable Wastes : These are the wastes which cannot be broken down by decomposers because they do not have enzymes for the same. We cannot eat coal to obtain energy because we do not possess enzymes for the same. Most of the non-biodegradable wastes are human made, e.g., plastic, crockery, metallic cans, glass articles, polythene bags, synthetic fibres, silver foil, many pesticides etc. Industries also produce a number of non-biodegradable wastes consisting of heavy metals, synthetic chemicals, toxins, etc., Some of these wastes can be broken down by heat, pressure and oxidation under specific conditions.
Differences between Biodegradable and Non-biodegradable Waste
| Biodegradable Waste | Non-biodegradable Waste |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. They decompose into the soil. 2. They are degraded by microorganisms such as bacteria and fungi. 3. They don't undergo biomagnification. 4. They are converted into soil or some useful end product. Examples : Sewage, household garbage, etc. | 1. Take long time or may never decompose. 2. They are not degraded by microorganisms. They can be broken down into smaller elements by few natural processes. 3. They enter the food chain and can undergo biomagnification. 4. These require recycling depending on the material. Examples : Plastic, glass, pesticides, heavy metals, radioactive elements, etc. |
（1）Take a piece of paper，a piece of old cotton cloth and a plastic bag．Dig these three items 15 cm deep in the ground， separately．Cover with soil and leave it for about a month．Then dig out the buried material and observe them．
We will find that the piece of paper and the piece of cloth have been partially decomposed but the plastic bag has remained unaffected．This means the paper and cloth have been decomposed by the micro－organisms present in the soil i．e．they are bio－degradable．The plastic bag has not been decomposed by micro－organisms present in the soil，therefore，plastic is non－biodegradable．
（2）Dig pit of 40－50 cm depth in one corner of your school garden or any other vacant area．Place some domestic waste articles in it．Moisten them and cover the articles with atleast 15 cm thick soil．Moisten the soil at intervals of 2－3 days．Observe at 15 days interval by removing the soil temporarily．
We will see that some articles have degenerated after 30－45 days into an amorphous mass．The latter disappears within 2－3 months．They are biodegradable articles．For e．g．，staple food，food left－overs，fruit and vegetable peels， used tea leaves，waste paper，cartons，etc．Others remain identifiable and intact even after six months．They are non－biodegradable wastes．For e．g．，polythene bags，milk pouches，bottles，strips，etc．
## Sources of Waste
Domestic waste includes wastes generated in houses e．g．paper，plastic，vegetable waste，glass，textile，etc：
Commercial waste includes waste generated in commercial establishments like shops，printers，offices，godowns，etc．It includes packing material，polythene，paper，spoiled goods，vegetable and meat remnants，etc．Ashes come from burning solid fossil fuels like coal，wood and coke．Many houses and road side eateries use these fuels．
Animal wastes include dung of stray．
Biomedical wastes are wastes generated from hospitals which include expired drugs，plastic syringes，surgical dressings，etc． They can be infectious．
Construction waste is contributed by the construction activity going on in the urban areas．This includes metal rods，cement， concrete，etc．This type of waste is also generated by departments like electricity，drainage，telephone，etc．
Sewer removed from the sewerage during cleaning is left on the roadside which poses several health hazards to the public．
Hazardous wastes are those which catch fire easily，react explosively with air or water，corrode other materials and are toxic or radioactive．

## E－waste
Discarded electronic devices like television，personal computers，floppies，audio－video CDs，batteries，electric switches， telephones，cellphones，refrigerators，washing machines，air conditioners，kitchen gadgets，aircrafts，etc．contribute to the e－waste．These products contain components that have toxic substances like lead，cadmium，mercury，barium and carcinogens like carbon black and heavy metals．This deadly mix can cause severe health problems for those handling the waste．It can even prove fatal．
## Harmful Effects of Wastes
In the absence of proper waste management，the waste lies on the streets，road corners and improperly disposed off on vacant land．It has some serious health hazards．They attract wildlife like flies，mosquitoes，etc．，scavangers such as stray dogs，pigs， rats which can spread diseases．It also generates bad odour and causes pollution．
Some non－biodegradable substances such as industrial chemicals，pesticides，radioactive substances，heavy metals are very harmful to the living beings．These substances enter the food chain at a lower trophic level and their concentration increases from one trophic level to the next．As human beings occupy the top most level in the food chain，maximum concentration of these elements get accumulated in our bodies and produce harmful effects．This increase in concentration of harmful chemicals from one tophic level to the next is termed as biomagnification．Excessive use of pesticides and fertilizers may reduce the soil fertility and crop yield．
## Bioplastics or Organic Plastics
It is a form of plastic derived from renewable biomass sources such as vegetable oil，corn starch，pea starch，or microbiota rather than fossil－fuel plastics which are derived from petroleum．Some，but not all，are designed to biodegrade．
These plastics are used for organic waste bags where they can be composted along with the food or green waste．Some trays for fruits，vegetables，eggs and meat，bottles for soft drink and daily products are manufactured from bioplastics．
## WASTE MANAGEMENT
Improvement in our life- style has resulted in greater amount of waste material generation. Waste management has become a serious issue in urban areas. Disposing of garbage at the outskirts of a city is a common sight. Empty cans, food wrappers, etc which are non- biodegradable are littered in crowded places and tourist centres.
The disposal of the waste we generate is causing serious environmental problems. There are a number of concepts about waste management, which vary in their usage between countries or regions. The waste hierarchy refers to the ' 3 Rs' reduce, reuse and recycle, which classify waste management strategies according to their desirability in terms of waste minimisation.
For leading a healthy life, society has to efficiently manage the waste produced by it. Waste management is concerned
Least favoured option
Fig. Waste Hierarchy
with the activity of collecting, transporting, processing, recycling, disposing and monitoring the waste dumped by the people in a society and around the world as a whole.
## Methods of Waste Management
1. Preventing and reducing the amount of waste being disposed of : - People should be made aware of their requirements for waste management, and efforts should be made to reduce the amount of waste. They should be encouraged to use recycled products.
2. Recovering energy from waste material:- Various kinds of machines have been developed to transform waste materials into some form of energy which can be used for cooking, heating and for providing power to various other machines.
3. Biological reprocessing: - Biodegradable waste can be converted into campost by burying in a pit dug into ground and used as manures.
4. Sanitary Landfill : - The disposal of waste by dumping it in low- lying areas of ground and covering it with earth is called landfill. This requires proper planning, design and good amount of man power. Land once filled can be reused, although it asks for maintenance.
5. Incineration:- 'Incineration' means 'reducing to ashes' i.e. burning of substance at very high temperature (above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mn>1000</mn><mrow><mo>∘</mo></mrow></msup></mrow></math> C) to form ash. This reduces the volume of the waste. Incineration is carried out in a furnace called incinerator. All organic matter present in the waste is removed as carbon dioxide and water. The ash left behind is removed from time to time and used in landfill.
6. Sewage treatment:- The ultimate return of used water to the environment is done by treating sewage. Sewage treatment involves three stages-primary, secondary and tertiary. The solids are first separated from the waste water. The dissolved biological matter is slowly converted into a solid mass by using water-borne bacteria. Finally, the biological solids are neutralized and then disposed off or reduced. The treated water is disinfected chemically. The final effluent is either discharged into a stream or water body or used for irrigation.
7. Bioremediation:- This is a rapidly developing clean up technology. Cleaning the environment with biological options such as microbes and plants is called bioremediation. Some naturally occuring bacteria and other micro- organisms have the capability to degrade or absorb or detoxify the wastes such as heavy metals.

## Ozone Depletion
1. Read the following and answer any four questions from 1 (i) to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mi>v</mi><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>.
Ozone ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ) is a molecule formed by three atoms of oxygen. While <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>, which we normally refer to as oxygen,
is essential for all arobic forms of life. Ozone is a deadly poison. However, at the higher levels of the atomsphere, ozone performs an essential function. It sheilds the surface of the earth from ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun. This radiation is highly damaging to organisms, for example , it is known to cause skin cancer in human beings. Ozone at the higher levels of the atmosphere is a product of UV radiation acting on oxgen ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ) molecule. The higher energy UV radiations split apart some moleculer oxygen ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ) into free oxygen ( O ) atoms. The amount of ozone in the atmoshphere began to drop sharply in the 1980s. This decrease has been linked to synthetic chemicals like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) which are used as refrigerants and in fire extinguishers. In 1987, the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in forging an agreemennt to freeze CFC production at 1986 levels. It is now mandatory for all the manufacturing companies to make CFC-free referigerators throughout the world.
(i) Ozone molecule is made up of
(a) 3 oxygen atoms
(b) 2 oxygen atoms
(c) 4 oxygen atoms
(d) 1 oxygen atoms
(ii) Ozone shields the surface of the earth from which radiation?
(a) IR
(b) U.V.
(c) Microwave
(d) X-ray
(iii) The amount of ozone in the atmosphere began to drop sharply in which decade of the twentieth century?
(a) 1979
(b) 1981
(c) 1980
(d) None of these
(iv) The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) succeeded in forging an agreement to freeze CFC production at which levels?
(a) 1987
(b) 1986
(c) 1980
(d) 1985
(v) UV radiations are known to cause <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> in humans.
(a) Skin cancer
(b) Lung cancer
(c) Asthama
(d) Eye defect

## Very Short Answer Questions:
1. A food web is the natural interconnection of food chains and a graphical representation (usually an image) of what-eats-what) in an ecological community.
2. Zooplanktons are free floating small animals which usually feed on phytoplanktons, e.g., Daphnia, Paramaecium.
3. The trophic level of an organism is the position it occupies in a food chain.
4. Biomass is amount of organic matter present in organisms.
5. It is the reduction in the concentration of ozone in the ozone layer or ozonosphere.
