key_points.text: Inexhaustible resources are those which are present in unlimited quantity in nature and are not likely to be exhausted by human activity. For example, sunlight, air, rain etc.
Exhaustible resources are those which are limited and can be exhausted by human activity in the long run. For example, petroleum, natural gas etc.

Renewable resources are those that can be replenished or reproduced easily. Some of them like wind, air, sunlight, etc. are continuously available and their quantity is not affected by man's consumption. Some renewable resources can be depleted by human use, but is also replenished, thus maintaining a flow. Agricultural crops take a short time for renewal; water takes a comparatively longer time, while forest takes even longer time.

Non-renewable resources are those which are formed over very long geological periods. Minerals and fossil fuels are included in this category. As the rate of formation of fossil fuels is very slow, they can't be replenished once they get depleted.
On the basis of origin:

Biotic resources are those which are obtained from the biosphere such as forests, animals, plants and their products. Mineral fuels such as coal and petroleum are also included in this category because, they are formed from decayed organic matter.
Abiotic resources include non-living things like land, water, air and mineral ores.
On the basis of their stage of development:

Potential resources are those that exist in a region and may be used in the future. For example, petroleum exists in many parts of the country, but it remains a potential resource till the time it is drilled out and put to use.
Actual resources are those that have been surveyed, their quantity and quality determined and are being used in present times e.g. wood.
On the basis of their distribution:
Natural resources are confined to national boundries e.g. minerals, forests, etc.
Multinational resources are shared by more than one nation e.g. certain rivers, migratory birds, etc.
International resources are shared uniformly by all nations e.g. sunlight, air, etc.
Natural resources like land, forest and water provide livelihood to millions.
Natural resources help in raising food crops and other natural food items
Export of minerals helps in economic growth of the country.
Natural resources like forests help to prevent soil erosion.
Developing countries depend a lot on the natural resources for their economic development.
Avoid single-use containers whenever possible.

theory_sections.markdown: ## 6. Management of Our Natural Resources

## Foundation
[^2]

## NATURAL RESOURCES
Natural resources are obtained from earth and its environment, and are also know as earth resources. Resources can be natural, human or human-made.
On the basis of their distribution, natural resources can be classified into:
(i) Inexhaustible resources are those which are present in unlimited quantity in nature and are not likely to be exhausted by human activity. For example, sunlight, air, rain etc.
(ii) Exhaustible resources are those which are limited and can be exhausted by human activity in the long run. For example, petroleum, natural gas etc.
(a) Renewable resources are those that can be replenished or reproduced easily. Some of them like wind, air, sunlight, etc. are continuously available and their quantity is not affected by man's consumption. Some renewable resources can be depleted by human use, but is also replenished, thus maintaining a flow. Agricultural crops take a short time for renewal; water takes a comparatively longer time, while forest takes even longer time.
(b) Non-renewable resources are those which are formed over very long geological periods. Minerals and fossil fuels are included in this category. As the rate of formation of fossil fuels is very slow, they can't be replenished once they get depleted.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

theory_sections.topics: Natural Resources can also be classified in three other ways:
theory_sections.markdown:
## Natural Resources can also be classified in three other ways:
1. On the basis of origin:
(i) Biotic resources are those which are obtained from the biosphere such as forests, animals, plants and their products. Mineral fuels such as coal and petroleum are also included in this category because, they are formed from decayed organic matter.
(ii) Abiotic resources include non-living things like land, water, air and mineral ores.
2. On the basis of their stage of development:
(i) Potential resources are those that exist in a region and may be used in the future. For example, petroleum exists in many parts of the country, but it remains a potential resource till the time it is drilled out and put to use.
(ii) Actual resources are those that have been surveyed, their quantity and quality determined and are being used in present times e.g. wood.
3. On the basis of their distribution:
(i) Natural resources are confined to national boundries e.g. minerals, forests, etc.
(ii) Multinational resources are shared by more than one nation e.g. certain rivers, migratory birds, etc.
(iii) International resources are shared uniformly by all nations e.g. sunlight, air, etc.
Why do we need to manage our resources?
Natural resources especially the non-renewable ones are depleting day by day. If proper attention is not given to this, the day is not far when we will be left with no more coal and oil.
- Natural resources like land, forest and water provide livelihood to millions.
- Natural resources help in raising food crops and other natural food items
- Export of minerals helps in economic growth of the country.
- Natural resources like forests help to prevent soil erosion.
- Developing countries depend a lot on the natural resources for their economic development.
As the population grows, the demands for all these resources also increase, but it is upto us to utilise it wisely and not waste it or misuse it.
Renewable resources such as water, air, plants, etc are considered to be plenty and one might even think that it will not perish. But the fact is that they might not perish away but will definitely become scarce. The capacity at which these items renew themselves is at a much slower pace than the demand. For example, we might think that there is plenty of water and we might never go short of it, but the truth is that there is only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of fresh water on earth for human need and the rest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>97</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> is saline water. If water is not utilised properly there will be scarcity of water in the near future.
These natural resources should be consumed to protect our environment. The management of natural resources is called as Natural Resource Management. It is to improve the quality of life of the present and the future generations.
Mother earth rich with its natural resources should be managed and preserved without which the existence of all life forms would be impossible. Healthy atmosphere would keep us healthy and increase our life span.
According to the U.S. Energy Information on Administration's (EIA) International. Energy Outlook (IEO 2019), the global supply of crude oil, other liquid hydrocarbons, and biofuels is expected to meet the world's demand for liquid fuels only through 2050.

## SAVE NATURE
As we read in chapter V, there are three R's of waste management: Reduce, reuse, and recycle. Practicing all three of these activities every day is important for a healthy environment.
3 R's

## Reduce
Reduce/Reduction: to make something smaller or use less, resulting in a smaller amount of waste. "Source reduction" is reducing waste before you purchase it, or by purchasing products that are not wasteful in their packaging or use. A key part of waste "reduction" is "conservation" - using natural resources wisely, and using less than usual in order to avoid waste.

## Tips to practice reduction:
1. Avoid single-use containers whenever possible.
2. Purchase less products and in materials/packaging that can be readily recycled.
3. Buy concentrates rather than diluted products- the result is less waste for disposal when it is empty.
4. Refuse store bags.
5. Use durable items rather than disposable items whenever possible.

## Reuse
Reuse materials in their original form instead of throwing them away, or pass those materials to others who could use them too.

## Tips for reuse :
Carry washable cups or travel mugs instead of disposables; a lot of restaurants and convenient stores will be glad to fill or refill your own mug. When you do use disposables like plastic cups, plates, utensils, and plastic food storage bags; don't throw them away.
Bicycles or other durable goods like washers, dryers, etc. are being repaired rather than being replaced when they break. This is another form of "reuse". New is not always better, nor it is always necessary. When you do decide to replace something large and "reusable", be sure to donate the old one to charitable outlets. Use cloth gift bags and stop ripping the paper off gifts. If you remove the wrapping paper carefully, you can use it again, and there's nothing wrong with doing just that. And don't forget to use canvas or cloth bags when shopping so you don't need to make the choice between "paper or plastic." Use washable table napkins instead of paper napkins. Cloth napkins are usually much larger and they absorb more than paper products.

## Recycle
Recycling occurs when you save and take reusable materials to places where they can be remade into either the same product or new products, instead of throwing them.
Making new items from recycled ones also takes fewer energy and other resources than making products from brand new materials. Buy toilet paper, paper towels and stationery made from recycled paper, tile from crushed light bulbs, garden hoses from used tires and yard furniture from recycled plastic bottles.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Environmental Laws
One of the fundamental rights, according to the Indian constitution is the 'Right to life' which means right to a healthy environment. Various laws have been enacted by the Government of India at different times. Some of the important ones are given below :
- Forest Act, 1972, 1980
- Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (amended in 1991)
- The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977 (amended in 1991)
- The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1981 (amended in 1987)
- The Environment Protection Act, 1986
- Prevention of Cruelty of Animals Act, 1960
- The Motor Vehicle Act, 1938 (amended in 1988)

## FORESTS AND WILDLIFE
The biotic component of our environment on land i.e. plants and animals are found in forests. Forests form a buffer for the earth to protect life forms. It comprises an area of diverse and complex ecosystems consisting mainly of trees, shrubs and wild plants which house an extraordinary variety of communities of living organisms. For example, the Indian forest includes a wide variety of plants and animals. India has over 45,000 plant species, 800 species of mammals, 2000 species of birds, 420 species of reptiles, 2000 species of fish, 5000 species of insects and 4000 species of molluscs etc. Due to the wide range of life forms and genetic diversity of life forms, a good forest is therefore called the 'biodiversity hot spots'.

## Forest
In last chapter, we studied the ecological web of life, how all life forms are interconnected and also how the well being of one organism is dependent on the other. One of the main aims of conservation is to try and preserve the inherited biodiversity. A loss of diversity would lead to an ecological imbalance. There is a clear and mutual relation between the forests, rainfall concentration, the amount of water in rivers and conservation of wild animals.

## Role of Forest
Forests play a vital role in boosting the welfare of people and our environment. They produce many valuable things besides providing ecological balance of the land area. Some important products of the forests are :
1. Wood is needed as timber used for making furniture and houses, wooden crates for packing, paper, sports goods, boats, railway sleepers, as fire wood in developing countries, etc.
2. Food and spices like dry fruits (almond, walnut, cashew, etc.) and spices (cloves, cinnamon, cardamon, etc.).
3. Tannins, Gums, Resins and Dyes. Tannins are used in making ink and leather polish, glues are used in making adhesives, resins are used in paints, dyes like hematoxylin are used in staining tissues in anatomy studies.
4. Drugs e.g. quinine, aconite, belladona, etc.
5. Other products like rubber, cork, honey, camphor, etc.

## Stakeholders
Modern approaches in forest conservation have found that enforcement of forest laws or policies which do not recognise the legitimate needs of different groups that depend on the forest, often fail in their objectives when we consider the conservation of forests. We need to look at different stakeholders whose dependency on forest resources vary.
1. Local communities that stay next to or in the forests Tribals staying within the forest areas and villages bordering them need the five basic ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ' i.e., Food, Fodder, Fibre, Fuel and Fertilizer for surviving. This is obtained from the forest.
2. The forest department of the Government which owns the land undertakes protection and controls the resources from forests. They earn revenue from the sale of timber to construction and furniture markets. They also earn revenue from commercial mono-plantations grown from trees like popular, eucalyptus, teak, bamboo etc. to access the specific products for industries such as paper, plyboard, etc.
3. Industries that use various forest produce, but are not dependent on the forest produce in any one area. These include timber, paper, resin and gum, lac, natural oils, minor food products, medicines, sports equipment industries. They are neither interested in loss of valuable biodiversity nor in the sustainability of forests in particular.
4. Environment Conservationists and Nature Enthusiasts who are not in any way dependent on the forests. NGOs like the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF), Centre for Science and Environment (CSE), International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) often exercise a considerable say in policy and management of forests. Historically, conservationists took up causes of protecting large animals like tigers (Project Tiger), lions, elephants and rhinoceros. They now recognise the need to preserve biodiversity as a whole, regulate industrial over-exploitation of forest resources and involve local people in the conservation of forests. Along with the Ministry of Environment and Forests, they have designated protected forest areas, wildlife sanctuaries, biodiversity hotspots for special attention and management.

## SUSTAINABLE MANAGEMENT
India has about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>63.5</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> million hectares of forest which forms <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the geographical area of the country. For a healthy environment, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>33</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of land should be under forest cover. It is extremely difficult for the forest department to safeguard the forest with its flora and fauna along with sustainable harvest of forest produce, solely by its own efforts. There have been enough instances of local communities working traditionally for conservation of forests with trees. Magsaysay Award recipient Sunderlal Bahuguna gave momentum to the 'Chipko Andolan' in Tehri Gharwal district in Uttrakhand. This was a movement started by the villagers especially the women at Reni village who tried to stop the commercial wood contractors from cutting the tree by embracing the trunk of the trees (Chipko). Earlier in 1731, the Bishnoi community in Rajasthan for whom conservation of forest and wildlife is a religious duty, sacrificed 363 lives along with Amrita Devi Bishnoi for the protection of Khejri trees in Khejrali village near Jodhpur in Rajasthan. The Government of India has recently instituted an 'Amrita Devi Bishnoi National Award for Wildlife
Conservation' in her memory.
While the government and conservation enthusiasts have a major role to play in protection and conservation

## Chipko Andolan
of forest, the acceptance of locals who live in harmony with natural resources is vital for forest conservation measures. This has been examplified by the West Bengal Forest Department who recognised their failure in reviving the Sal forests in the south western district of the state in 1972. Traditional method of surveillance and policing had led to a complete alienation of the people from the administration resulting in frequent clashes between forest officials and villagers. This was also a major factor in fuelling the militant peasant movements led by the Naxalites. The department was forced to change its strategy and made a beginning of a farsighted forest officer A.K. Banerjee. In the Arabari forest range of Midnapore district, he involved the villagers in the protection of 1,272 hectares of badly degraded Sal forest. In return, for help in protection, villagers were allowed fuel wood and fodder collection on payment of a nominal fee and given employment in both silviculture and harvesting operations. Also 25 percent of the final harvest was given to the village community. The result was an active and willing participation of the local community. The Sal forest of Arabari underwent a remarkable recovery by 1983-a previously worthless forest was valued ₹ 12.5 crores. Such instances have now been repeated and clearly pointed to a model in which decentralized economic growth and ecological conservation go hand-in-hand.

## Conservation of Forest
Conservation is protection, augmentation and scientific mangement of resources so as to maintain them at their optimum level provide benefit to the present as well as future generations. Forests must be conserved because of their immense economic, protective and regulative properties. It is carried out by the following methods.
1. Afforestation. Growing of forests on uprotected barren lands. Van Mahotsava is a tree plantation movement carried out twice a year (February and July) by both government and voluntary agencies.
2. Reforestation. Developing forest cover in the area which has been damaged or cleared during exploitation.
3. Separation of Commercial Forestry. Useful plants required by industry should be planted separately preferably on waste land. Growing industry required plants is called production plantation.
4. Grazing. Grazing should be regulated according to the availability of pasturage.
5. Sustained Yield Block Cutting. A forest is divided into a number of blocks depending upon the period required by forest trees to mature. In one year, trees of one block are felled. The block is reforested immediately. In this way, annual deforestation is compensated by annual reforestation. The forest is conserved indefinitely and provides sustained yield.
6. Social Forestry. It is growing of useful multipurpose trees and shrubs on common village lands, roadsides, railsides, etc, by cooperative efforts of people of the area. The plants provide fodder, small timber and fire wood to villagers.
7. Agroforestry. Multipurpose trees and shrubs are planted on the sides of crop fields to provide fodder, fuel and timber.
NOTE: Both social forestry and agroforestry reduce pressure on the forests. Trees are also planted at all vacant places and along roads in urban areas (urban forestry) for reducing air and noise pllution.
## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Red Data Book
The International Union for the Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) maintains an international list, published as the Red Data Book. The aim of the list is to impart information to the public and policy makers about the urgency of conservation problems. The list recognises 8 Red list categories of species.
(i) Data deficient (DD) - appropriate data on abundance and distribution of the species is unavailable.
(ii) Not evaluated (NE) - species not evaluated against the criteria of IUCN
(iii) Low risk/Least concern (LC) - species is characterized by IUCH but not being a focus of species conservation.
(iv) Vulnerable (VU) - species susceptible to become endangered unless the circumstances affecting its reproduction and survival improve.
(v) Endangered (EN) - species is at risk of becoming extinct, as it is few in number and threatened by various parameters like predation, deforestation etc.
(vi) Critically Endangered (CR) - species that face a high risk of extinction in the wild.
(vii) Extinct in the wild (EW) - species that are not known to have any living specimens in the wild and are only zoos or artificial environments.
(viii) Extinct (EX) - when the last existing member of the species dies.
The 2000 Red list contains assessment of more than 18,000 species, 11,000 of which are threatened.
According to the Red list, in India
44 plant species - Critically endangered e.g. Berberies nilghirensis
113 plant species - Endangered e.g. Bentinckiet nicobarica
87 plant species - Vulunerable e.g. Cupressus cashmeriana
18 animal species - Critically endangered e.g. Pigmy hog
54 animal species - Endangered e.g. Red Panda
143 animal species - Vuunerable e.g. Black buck
The list also provides information to international agreements such as the Convention on Biological diversity and the convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora.

## Biodiversity Conservation
Earth's rich biodiversity is vital for the very survival of mankind. Biodiversity conservation is mainly aimed at protection, enhancement and scientific management of biodiversity.
There are two basic strategies of biodiversity conservation -
(i) In-situ conservation: The conservation of plants/animals in their natural habitats. National parks, wildlife sanctuaries and biosphere reserves are the examples of protected areas.
(ii) Ex-situ conservation: The conservation of plants/animals outside their natural habitats. It includes offsite collections (botanical gardens, zoos, arboreta etc.) and gene banks (preserves genetic material like freezing cuttings from plants or seeds, freezing of sperm and eggs etc.).
The famous bird dodo (Raphus cucullatus) that primarily lived in the island nation of Mauritius was hunted to extinction, known as human driven extinction. This bird was popularized by its appearance in Lewis Carroll's book "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland." Dodos were first mentioned by Dutch sailors in the late 16th century. They ate the beast to extinction after finding that the bird was incredibly easy to catch due to the fact it had no fear of humans.

## WATER FOR ALL
Water is an essential component of all forms of life. After air, it is also the most abundant substance present in the earth's biosphere. Oceans, seas, and other saline inland water bodies make <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>97.3</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the total global supply of water which is not available for human consumption due to high salt content. Fresh water accounts for only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>2.7</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the total estimated global water supply. Of this percentage, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>75</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the fresh water is frozen in polar ice caps and glaciers. Some fresh water is also present underground. The fraction of fresh water available for human beings is less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>0.003</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the total global water available.
There are three main sources of fresh water available. The first is rainwater resources of India. India receives an overall rainfall of about 105 to 117 cm annually resulting in 3 million <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>m</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math> of water. Most of this water is collected in few months during monsoon. The second source is surface water, there are 14 major river systems which include Ganga, Brahmaputra, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri, etc. Other surface water sources include ponds, lakes and streams. The third source is ground water which is the water that percolates down the surface soil into pore spaces of rocks. The total volume of ground water found in the aquifer is estimated to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>42.3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math>. Ground water provides soil moisture for plants and supplements for streams and lakes. Water table indicates the level at which ground water is found. The agricultural sector and urban sectors use this water for various proposes which has resulted a decline in its level.
Study the rainfall pattern in India and identify the regions where water is abudant and where it is scarce.
Rainfall pattern in India is -
Scanty rainfall - parts of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Punjab and Haryana
Moderate rainfall - Indo-Gangetic plains and Southeastern parts of the country.
Heavy rainfall - Kerala, North eastern states and Western Ghats.
Regions in India with water scarcity - Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Chattisgarh, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu.
In India, most of the water supply and regeneration is dependant on the monsoon rains. There are some worrying facts about the water situation. The effects of world climate change are being felt all over the planet. For a country like India, where agricultural activities are still dependent on regular monsoon rainfall, it is a cause of worry for adequate and reliable water supply. Erratic rainfall like heavy rains in areas receiving minimal rainfall (Rajasthan floods of 2006 in Barmer), scanty rainfall in areas receiving good rainfall (Karnataka and A.P. drought of 2006), melting of glaciers are causing problems in surface water supply, ground water recharge and agriculture.
Major diversion of water from the rivers has resulted in many downstream areas having reduced ground water recharge. This includes urban areas which have huge requirements of water and depend on dams and reservoir supply. Cities have an additional problem of clearing vegetation from land to build houses, roads, etc. Large concreting of surface areas does not allow water to seep into the ground to be stored in aquifiers leading to water shortages. Because of this, there is water run off or water remains on the surface which increases the surface run off and floods in cities causing heavy damage.

## Measurement of Water Pollution
The contamination of river water usually occurrs due to the presence of coliform bacteria. Coliform is a group of bacteria found in human intestines. The presence of coliform in the river water indicates its contamination by disease-causing micro-organisms. Although coliform itself is harmless, but its presence in river water indicates that other harmful intestinal bacteria might also be present. The pH of river water is measured by using universal indicator paper. If the pH of river water is below 7 , then the river water will be acidic and hence polluted.

## Ganga Action Plan
A multicrore 'Ganga Action Plan' project was launched in 1985 to clean the river Ganga and make pollution free.
Ganga Action Plan (GAP) was formulated to reduce pollution load of river Ganga by more than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>75</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math>. The plan has been carried out in two phases since 1985. The water quality has been tested from time to time by checking coliform (group of harmless bacteria in human intestine) number/ 100 ml . The important aspect of GAP has been (i) Diversion of sewers away from river (ii) Treatment of sewage and changing it into an energy source (iii) Construction of community management system (vi) Enforcement of setting up of effluent treatment plants by the industry. (iii) Development of Ghats and interceptions.
Fig.: Varanasi (the holiest place on the Ganga River)
Did You
Till 2014, over ₹ 4,000 crore had been spent to clean Ganga, but the river has remained dirty. The National Democratic Alliance (NDA) government had launched the Namami Gange in mid-May 2015. It was being implemented by the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG), and its state counterparts-State Programme Management Groups. The National Ganga Council (NGC) was later created and to give it utmost importance, the Prime Minister was made the head of it. It was the biggest-ever initiative. Around ₹ 20,000 crore was allotted and a deadline was set: "Ganga will be clean by 2019" which has now been extended to 2020.

## Learn More
Water pollution is a serious health hazard in India. The principal sources of water pollution and water pollutants are domestic sewage and industrial effluents. The domestic sewage contains human faeces, urine, kitchen/cloth washings, organic wastes, industrial wastes, etc. Sewage provides food for decomposers, so the population of microorganisms increases.
The microorganisms/decomposers cause decomposition of sewage and takes up most of the oxygen dissolved in water. So, the BOD (Biological or Biochemical oxygen demand) of water increases. BOD of pure drinking water is less than 1ppm (parts per million) or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mi>l</mi></mrow></math>. A weak organic waste will have a BOD below <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1500</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mi>l</mi></mrow></math>, medium organic waste between 1500-4000 <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mi>l</mi></mrow></math>, while it is above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>4000</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mi>l</mi></mrow></math> in strong organic waste. The degree of water pollution is directly proportional to BOD.COD (Chemical oxygen demand) is the amount of oxygen required to oxidize all pollutant materials in one litre of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mn>20</mn><mrow><mo>∘</mo></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></mrow></math> in five days. The value of COD is much higher than BOD.
In sewage, phosphorus and nitrogen compounds are present which are necessary for the growth of algae. In polluted water, these accumulate and result into excessive growth of algae on water surface. Excessive growth of algae is called water bloom. Presence of extra nutrients brings about dense growth of plankton algae (termed as eutrophication) which increases organic loading and brings about reduction in the oxygen content of water causing death of aquatic animals.
Industrial effluents (or industrial discharge) contain heavy metals (mercury, lead, copper, arsenic, and calcium), inorganic pollutants (acids, alkalies and bleaching liquors) and organic pollutants (phenol, naptha, proteins, aromatic compounds, cellulose fibres, etc.). The non biodegradable pollutants like DDT, pesticides, phenolic compounds etc. are not decomposed by microorganisms. These get accumulated in tissue in increasing concentration along the food chain and the phenomenon is called biological magnification.

## Dams
Dams are large water-storing bodies across the river to regulate the flow of water. Many dams have been constructed across the major rivers which serve as storehouse of river water. The stored water is allowed to flow dowstream in order to generate electricity and carry water to long distances for the purpose of irrigation. Some examples of famous dams and canals in India are :
1. Bhakra Dam built on the river Sutlej in Punjab.
2. Sardar Sarovar Dam built on river Narmada in 1940 in Gujarat.
3. Tehri Dam on river Ganga in Tehri, Uttranchal (highest dam).
4. Tawa Dam on the Tawa river in Hoshangabad (MP).
5. Mettur Dam in Kaveri river in Tamil Nadu (oldest dam).
Fig. Tehri Dam : The highest dam in India

## Benefits of Dams
(i) Canal system originating from the dams can transfer large amounts of water to great distances which helps to raise agricultural production in distant places. For example, Indira Gandhi Canal originating from Bhakra dam has brought greenery in deserts of Rajasthan.
(ii) Dams ensure continuous water supply in the surrounding areas.
(iii) Flowing water from a height is used for generating electricity.

theory_sections.topics: Problems associated with construction of Dams
theory_sections.markdown:
## Problems associated with construction of Dams
(i) Construction of dams creates displacement of a large number of peasants and tribals without adequate compensation or rehabilitation. These social problems result in protests like Narmada Bachao Andolan. The people who have been displaced by the construction of Tawa Dam in 1970s are still fighting for the benefits they were promised. The other social problems are created by the mismanagement of the officials. It has been observed that people close to the source of water grow water intensive crops like sugarcane and rice whereas people further downstream do not get sufficient water to grow these crops. This causes social problems due to the unequal distribution of water.
(ii) Construction of dam is an expensive project. Some people think that such expenditure does not generate the proportionate benefits. Others believe that no progress can happen without constructing dams as it is the most efficient way to use water resources.
(iii) Construction of dams result in deforestation and loss of biodiversity.

## Water Management
In our country, there is no shortage of water, but proper management is lacking. Some of the methods for managing and conserving water resources are as follows :
(i) Rain water harvesting : The government and various organisations have been trying to find an alternative of dams. Several states in the country have been using the age-old practices of water harvesting which are still in use today and are as listed below:
| S.No. | State | Water Harvesting Practices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | Rajasthan | Khadins, tanks and Nadis |
| 2. | Maharashtra | Bandharas and Tals |
| 3. | Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh | Bundhis |
| 4. | Bihar | Ahars and Pynes |
| 5. | Himachal Pradesh | Kulhs |
| 6. | Jammu and Kashmir | Ponds |
| 7. | Tamil Nadu | Eris |
| 8. | Kerala | Surangams |
| 9. | Karnataka | Kattas |
Khadins in Rajasthan consist of a long (100-300 m long) earthen embankment built across the lower edge of the sloping farmland. The area enclosed by the embankment is called the 'bund' which collects huge amount of rain water that flows down the slopes. The overflowing water is carried through the pathways which fills the shallow well dug behind the bund.
Fig. The Khadin System in Rajasthan
Kulhs was a canal irrigation system developed in Himachal Pradesh about 400 years ago. Stream waters were channelled into the downhill villages. Villages had made a common agreement about water use. These kulhs were under looked by few people who were being paid by the villagers. The water from these kulhs was used for irrigation as well as percolated into the soil to feed springs. Ever since these kulhs were taken over by the irrigation department, they became defunct.
In the present day, rain water harvesting comprises of water storage and ground water recharging. The rain water collected from the roof top of a house or a building is then filtered and stored in proper tanks. This stored water is either used for human consumption, irrigation or can be channelised through a drain pipe into a well to raise the water table.
Fig.: Modern method of rain water harvesting
(ii) Treatment of waste water : Waste water usually contains organic and inorganic impurities which must be removed by primary and secondary treatments. This water can then be used for industrial and irrigational purposes. The benefits of using treated waste water are :
(a) Conservation of water resources.
(b) Increases crop yield by conserving nutrients and reducing the need for artificial fertilizers.
(c) Reduces pollution of surface water resources.
(d) Low cost method for sanitary disposal of municipal waste water.
(iii) Storage of water : Rain water should be stored in huge tanks for use in agriculture. Dams and reservoirs also store water which can be supplied to canals and rivers throughout the year.
CASE STUDY : Water Harvesting
(A) Kulhs
(B) Ahar Pyne
(C) Khadin
CASE - I : In which state, Kulhs is used and why did it become defunct?
KulhsisacanalirrigationsystempracticedinhillyareasofHimachalPradesh.Earlieritwasmanagedbythepeoplein villages,but later when it was taken over by the irrigation department,it became defunct.
CASE - II : Ahar pyne irrigation system is practiced in which part of the country and why it still exists?
AharpyneisatraditionalirrigationsystemandispracticedinSouthplainsofBiharforcenturies.ItsavestheplainsofSouth Bihar from recurrent floods and utilizes water; hence it is still in use.
CASE - III : In which areas, Khadin is used for water harvesting and why?
It is used in Western Rajasthan to retain monsoon water and soak plots to raise crops.
CASE - IV : Why is Khadin better than dams?
In comparison to Khadin, a lot of space is required to build dams and they can also cause floods.

## Think Out of the Box
Q. 1 What is the major concern regarding use of Khadin in Rajasthan?
Q2. The neglected use of Kuhls has affected the crop pattern in Himachal Pradesh. Explain with an example?

## COAL AND PETROLEUM
Fossil fuels consist of deposits of dead organisms. The organic matter takes centuries to form. Fossil fuels principally consist of carbon and hydrogen bonds. There are three types of fossil fuels which can all be used for energy production; coal, oil and natural gas. They are a non-renewable source of energy formed from plants and animals that lived up to 300 million years ago and are found in deposits beneath the earth. The fuels are burnt to release the chemical energy that is essential to modern society. Over <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>85</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of our energy demands are met by the combustion of fossil fuels.

## Coal
Coal is a hard, black colored rock-like substance. It is made up of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and varying amounts of sulphur. There are three main types of coal - Anthracite, Bituminous and Lignite. Anthracite coal is the hardest and has
Fig.: Fossil fuels-synthesis and combustion
more carbon, which gives it a higher energy content. Lignite is softest and is low in carbon but high in hydrogen and oxygen content. Bituminous is in between. Today, the precursor to coal peat - is still found in many countries and is also used as an energy source.

## Petroleum
(comes from the Latin word petra, "rock," and oleum, "oil.") :
Oil is a liquid fossil fuel that is formed from the remains of marine microorganisms deposited on the sea floor. After millions of years, these deposits end up in rock and sediment where oil is trapped in small spaces. It can be extracted by large drilling platforms. Oil is the most widely used fossil fuel. Crude oil consists of many different organic compounds which are transformed to products in a refining process. It is applied in cars, jets, roads, roofs etc. Oil cannot be found everywhere on earth. Some scientists say that tiny diatoms are the source of oil. Diatoms are sea creatures of the size of a pin head. They do one thing just like plants; they can convert sunlight directly into energy. The various steps in this process are-
(1) Diatoms died and fell on the sea floor, (2) Here, they were buried under sediment and other rock, (3) The rock squeezed the diatoms and carbon eventually turned into oil under great pressure and heat. As the earth changed, moved and folded, pockets where oil and natural gas can be found, were formed.
Oil has been used for more than 5,000-6,000 years. We are currently in an energy crisis. Fossil fuels are the lifeblood of our modern society but our supply has a finite end. Fossil fuels take millions of years to make. We are using up the fuels that were made more than 300 million years ago before the time of the dinosaurs. Once they are gone it is almost impossible to get them back once again. So, it's best not to waste fossil fuels. They are not renewable; they can't really be made again. We can save fossil fuels by conserving energy.

theory_sections.topics: Pollution caused by Burning Coal and Petroleum based Fuels
theory_sections.markdown:
## Pollution caused by Burning Coal and Petroleum based Fuels
When coal and petroleum based fuels are burnt, the products are carbon dioxide, water, sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides. If incomplete combustion takes place in an insufficient supply of oxygen then carbon monoxide is also produced . Of all the products of combustion, only water is harmless while others pollute the environment.

## Few examples are-
(i) Sulphur dioxide causes respiratory disorders. It dissolves in rain water making it acidic and causing damage to plant life, buildings, monuments and metal structures.
(ii) Nitrogen oxide also has similar effects like sulphur dioxide.
(iii) Carbon dioxide has green house effect and leads to global warming.
(iv) Carbon monoxide is a poisonous gas which gets into our blood stream, binds with RBCs and causes suffocation. Excess carbon monoxide can also cause death.

theory_sections.topics: Methods of conservation of fossil fuels
theory_sections.markdown:
## Methods of conservation of fossil fuels
(i) Burning coal causes pollution. Coal can be converted to liquid fuel and Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) through coal gasification.
(ii) Wastage during extraction and transportation of fossil fuel should be avoided by using modern technology.
(iii) Oil wells and coal mines should be protected from fire to avoid wastage, pollution and loss of life or property.
(iv) Alternative sources of energy such as hydroelectricity, nuclear, solar, wind power and biogas plants should be used.
1. Calculate the amount of petrol or diesel used by the vehicle/s at your home in a month.
2. Observe the number of vehicles in your area and calculate how much of fuel is used by the people in your community.
3. Get an estimate of fuel consumption in your community.
4. Can you think of ways to reduce it?

## (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions) \section*{Selected Questions)}

## Advantages:
(i) The main aim is to develop primary resources of land and water.
(ii) To produce secondary resources of plants and animals for use in a manner which will hot cause ecological imbalance.
6. We need to use our resources carefully as:
(a) our resources are limited and the demand for all resources is increasing at an exponential rate.
(b) sustainable management of natural resources is required so that these resources last for the generations to come.
(c) we require equitable distribution of these resources.
7. Fossil-fuel contains hydrogen, nitrogen and sulphur in addition to carbon. When fossil-fuels are burnt carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, water, oxide of nitrogen and sulphur are produced. Products like carbon monoxide, oxide of nitrogen and sulphur are poisonous in high concentration. At the same time excessive production of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> causes global warming as it is one of the green house gases.
8. Stakeholders in respect of forest are those people who utilise various forest products. These are :
(i) The people who live in and around the forest.
(ii) Industrialist engaged in manufacture of furniture, biris, plywood and sports goods.
(iii) Forest department of government.
(iv) Wild-life and nature enthusiasts, the industrialists cause maximum damage to forests as they consider the forest as merely a source of raw material for their factories.
9. The three problems that arise due to construction of large dams are :
(i) Social problem : Displacement of large number of peasants and tribals.
(ii) Economic problem : These projects swallow up large amounts of public money without the generation of proportionate benefits.
(iii) Environmental problem : As they contribute to deforestation and the loss of biodiversity.
10. Ground water is more advantageous as compared to surface water due to following reasons:
(i) Ground water generally does not get polluted, if it is away (at least 20 mtr .) from the sanitary works. The water gets filtered while percolating through sand \& stones. Therefore groundwater remains pure and clean.
(ii) Since bore well is closed, no risk of getting contaminated.
(iii) Since it is closed no danger of children or animals falling into it.
(iv) Temperature of deep water remains stable. It feels cool in summer and warm in winter.
(v) Since bore are deep, chances of water remaining available in summer are more.
11. Without the regular grazing of grass by cattles like sheep and goats the grass first grows taller and taller and then falls over. Due to this growth, fresh grass is prevented resulting in loss of green cover.
12. Forest,plants are important as they serve as the primary products on which other living beings depend and serve as basic food, raw materials for growth in the country. Forests are the great source of many herbs and material having medicinal value. This is how it play a productive role.
Forest play a protective role by acting as a natural habitat for Flora and fauna and also protect soil from erosion as well as by enhancing the quality of environment. Forest play an important role in regulation as it modify the local climate, thus maintaining the ecological balance by providing oxygen and absorbing carbon dioxide.
13. There are many examples which suggest that involvement of local communities is necessary for any conservation effort. The Bishnoi community of Rajasthan is one such example. Amrita Devi Bishnoi is still remembered with reverence for the way she fought for protecting the khejri trees in Khejrali village. She; along with 363 other people; sacrificed her life for the protection of khejri trees in 1731. The 'Amrita Devi Bishnoi National Award for Wildlife Conservation' has been named in her honour.
14. Biodiversity is an estimate of the number of different life forms available in an area. Various species are available in a forestwhich include bacteria, fungi, ferns, plants, nematodes, insects, birds, reptiles and mammals. Forests are therefore, known as biodiversity hot spots. An individual can contribute in the management of forest and wildlife by
(i) Avoiding of clearing of forests and killing of wildlfe.
(ii) Educating people about necessity of forest and wildlife in our life.
15. (i) Building of rest house for tourist in park causes disturbance in natural habitat. Restriction in movement waste disposal poaching/hunting.
(ii) Allowing animals to graze in national parks causes ecological imbalance, less food for herbivores, overgrazing could result in the loss of habitat.
16. (i) Reuse implies to the use of the same material over and over again.
(ii) Resources are saved and no energy is consumed in reuse of materials.
(iii) Certain used materials are converted into other useful materials in recycling.
(iv) In recycling of materials, thus energy is consumed and the resources may get wasted.
17. Management of forest and wildlife resources are consider as a challenging task because of :
(i) Various stakeholders of forest, Management of forest and wildlife has to consider the needs of all these stakeholders which becomes a challenging task.
(ii) Sources of raw material industries would consider the forest as or its factories and huge interest group demand the government for access to these raw materials at extremely low rates. This further poses a challenge to manage the forest and wildlife reasources.
18. Two main advantages of water harvesting at the community level include:
(i) Water evaporation does not occur, rather it percolates into the ground. Therefore, it results in the recharging of wells.
(ii) Water storage provides moisture to the vegetation within the area.
19. (a) Two ways to bring awareness on "how to save water" are:-
(i) By organising a demonstration of the households practices to minimize the wastage of water.
(ii) By organising poster competitions on "how to save water" every week every year to create awareness.
(b) Rainwater harvesting is better a way by which the underground water table does not go down further. In this we can collect rainwater and store it in the form of pits and lakes ensuring that the water seeps through the soil and recharges the aquifers.
20. A dam is built to control water through placement of a blockage of earth, rock and/or concrete across a stream or river. Large dams can ensure the storage of adequate water not just for irrigation, but also for generating electricity. Criticisms about large dams address three problems in particular are:
(i) Social problems because they displace large number of peasants and tribals without adequate compensation or rehabilitation.
(ii) Economic problems because they swallow up huge amounts of public money without the generation of proportionate benefits.
(iii) Environmental problems because they contribute enormously to deforestation and the loss of biological diversity.
21. Water harvesting - Rainwater harvesting (RWH) is a simple method by which rainfall is collected for future usage. The collected rainwater may be stored, utilised in different ways or directly used for recharge purposes. The two main advantages associated with water harvesting at the community level are :
- Over-exploitation of water resources will be reduced.
- Helps to recharge the natural wells and provides moisture for vegetation over a wide area.
The two main causes for the failure of sustained availibility of water are :
- Increase in Industries lead to extra requirement of water for manufacturing goods and products.
- Increasing effects of global warming.
22. Role of forests in conservation :
(a) It prevents soil erosion and helps in maintaining fertility of soil.
(b) It provides shelter to wild animals.
(c) It reduces atmospheric pollution.
(d) It increases humidity and frequency of rainfall.
The forests get depleted due to forest fires, urbanisation, industrialization, overgrazing by animals, felling of trees etc. The consequences of deforestation will be :
(a) Change in climate and global warming
(b) Soil erosion and floods
(c) Extinction of wildlife, etc.
