rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q8
rows.question: Diffusion is insufficient to meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> requirement of multicellular organisms like humans.
rows.answer:
(a) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> from the respiratory tissues to the lungs is transported by the blood in 3 ways :
(i) In dissolved state or a a solution : Very small amount dissolved in plasma ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> i.e. 0.3 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> by each 100 ml of blood).
(ii) Bicarbonate ions : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>70</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 2.5 ml per 100 ml of blood) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> diffuses in plasma \& then into RBCs where it (in the presence of carbonic anhydrase) combines with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow></math> to form carbonic acid which is almost spontaneously dissociated into hydrogen ion \& bicarbonate ions.
(iii) Carbaminohaemoglobin : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>23</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 1 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> per 100 ml of blood) combines with haemoglobin forming an unstable compound.

rows.question: What is osmoregulation?
rows.answer: Maintenance of a fixed osmotic concentration of body fluids by controlling the amount of water and salts is called osmoregulation.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Define lenticels.
rows.answer: Lenticels are the aerating pores present in cork or bark of the plants that allows gaseous exchange between atmosphere and internal tissues.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What would happen if all the green plants disappear from the earth?
rows.answer: Green plants are the source of food for all the organisms. If all the green plants disappear from the earth, then all the organisms (herbivores, carnivores and omnivores) will die due to starvation.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Explain why a land plant may die if its roots remain waterlogged for a long time.
rows.answer: A land plant may die if its roots remain water logged for a long time because too much water expels all the air from in-between the soil particles. Due to this, oxygen is not available to the roots for aerobic respiration. Under these conditions the roots will respire anaerobically producing alcohol which may kill the plant.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Explain how it would benefit deep sea divers if humans also had gills.
rows.answer: The deep sea divers could remain under sea water even without carrying oxygen cylinders for breathing because they could then extract dissolved oxygen from water for breathing purpose just like fish.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Expain why, plants have low energy needs as compared to animals.
rows.answer:
Plants are stationary. They are autotrophic and do not have to move from one place to another in search of their food. Hence, their energy requirement is less and therefore, most of the plant tissues are dead with no living protoplasm.
On the other hand, animals are heterotrophic and have to move in search of food, mate, and shelter. Hence, they require more energy and therefore, most of the animal tissues are living.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Why do the walls of trachea not collapse when there is less air in it?
rows.answer: Trachea is supported by c-shaped rings of cartilage which hold them in place when there is less air inside it. So, the walls of trachea do not collapse even when there is a little air.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Why do some people need to use a dialysis machine? what does the machine do?
rows.answer: Some people need to use dialysis machines because they have lost function of their own kidneys. The dialysis machine essentially removes excess fluid from the body, cleans the blood of urea (toxic build-up), and balances electrolytes.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why bile juice is necessary for digestion of food however it does not contain enzymes ?
rows.answer: It neutralizes the acidic nature of chyme and provides strong alkaline medium for the action of pancreatic juice and intestinal juice.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Why is sino-atrial node also called pace maker ?
rows.answer: Sino-atrial node also called pace maker because it determines the rate of heart beat by determining the rate of discharge of cardiac impulse.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Explain why constriction of skin blood vessels on a cold winter day is beneficial.
rows.answer: Constriction of blood vessels to the skin reduces blood flow to the skin, which cools as a result. The benefit is that less heat is lost through the skin to the environment and the internal body temperature is maintained. As the difference in temperature between the skin and the environment decreases, less heat is lost. If the skin temperature decreases too much, however, dilation of blood vessels to the skin occurs, which functions to prevent the skin from becoming so cold that it is damaged.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Predict what would happen if vitamins were broken down during the process of digestion rather than being absorbed intact into the circulation.
rows.answer: If vitamins are broken down during the process of digestion, their structures are destroyed, and as a result, their ability to function as vitamins is lost. Because vitamins cannot be synthesized in adequate amounts, vitamin deficiency diseases would occur.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Explain what happen to your throat when you sleep with your mouth open, especially when your nasal passages are plugged as a result of having a cold.
rows.answer: When you sleep with your mouth open, less air passes through the nasal passages. This is especially true when nasal passages are plugged because you have a cold. As a consequence, air is not humidified and warmed. Thus, dry air dries the throat and the trachea.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Predict the effect on the heart if blood flow through the anterior inter-ventricular artery is restricted or completely blocked.
rows.answer: The anterior interventricular artery supplies blood to the anterior wall of the heart and to much of the left ventricle. A blocked anterior interventricular artery reduces the oxygen supply to the portion of the heart that is supplied by that artery, and the cardiac muscle in that area is not able to contract effectively. Thus, the left ventricle on the anterior surface of the heart does not contract normally.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q7
rows.question: If blood supply is reduced in a small area of the heart through which the left bundle branch passed, predict the effect on ventricular contractions.
rows.answer: If the normal blood supply is reduced in a small area of the heart through which the left bundle branch passes, conduction of action potentials through that side of the heart is reduced or blocked. As a consequence, the left side of the heart contracts more slowly. The right side of the heart contracts more normally. The reduced rate of contraction of the left ventricle reduces the pumping effectiveness of the left ventricle.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What are differences between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition?
rows.answer:
Difference between antotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition
| S.No. | Autotrophic nutrition | Heterotrophic nutrition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is the nutrition in which organic food is manufactured from inorganic raw materials. | It is the nutrition in which organic food is obtained from various sources. |
| (ii) | It is a type of nutrition in which organism synthesise their own food by converting simple inorganic substances into complex organic substances. | It is a type of nutrition in which an organism can not synthesise their own food but obtain its nutrition or food from the autotrophs directly or indirectly. |
| (iii) | An external source of energy (Sun) is required for synthesis of organic substances (photosynthesis). | An external source of energy is not required. It is obtained by the oxidisation of food. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (iv) | Inorganic substances constitute the raw materials for synthesis of food. | Inorganic substances are not much required. |
| (v) | Chlorophyll is present for trapping light energy. | Chlorophyll is absent. |
| (vi) | Organisms performing autotrophic nutrition function as producers. | Organisms performing heterotrophic nutrition function as consumers. |
| (vi) | Examples: Green plants, some bacteria, some protists. | Examples: Animals, many protists and monerans. |

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q2
rows.question: What is the role of acid in our stomach ?
rows.answer:
The hydrochloric acid in our stomach facilitates the following:
(i) It creates acidic medium by which inactive enzyme pepsinogen is converted to active pepsin, which is a protein digesting enzyme.
(ii) It give signals to pancreas for producing digestive juices and enzymes to further breakdown food.
(iii) It gets mixed with food and kills bacteria present in food.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Where do plants get such raw materials required for photosynthesis?
rows.answer:
Photosynthesis requires carbon dioxide, sunlight, water and chlorophyll as its basic raw materials:
(i) Carbon dioxide: It is a gas present in the atmosphere. Plants absorb carbon dioxide through small pores (called stomata) on the surface of their leaves. This is a source of carbon for the plants.
(ii) Water: It is a liquid form, absorbed by the roots, transported through the stem to leaves by a special structures called as xylem tissues.
(iii) Chlorophyll: It is a green coloured pigment in the chloroplasts of the cells. It traps energy from the sunlight.
(iv) Sunlight: It is an important factor for the photosynthesis, which is absorbed by the chlorophyll and other green parts of the plants.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Why is diffusion insufficient to meet the oxygen requirements of multicellular organisms like humans?
rows.answer:
Diffusion is insufficient to meet the oxygen requirements of multicellular organisms like humans because of the following reasons:
- The body design is very complex and the oxygen demand is quite high. The oxygen requirements of an animal cannot be met by the process of diffusion as it is a very slow process.
- All the cells of the body are not in direct contact with the surrounding environment.
- Intake of oxygen in multicellular organism takes place by one organ and used in another organ.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q5
rows.question: How are the lungs designed in human beings to maximise the area for exchange of gases ?
rows.answer:
In the lungs, trachea divides into smaller tubes, called 'bronchi' which inturn forms 'bronchioles'. The bronchioles terminate in balloon - like structures, called
as 'alveoli'. Each lung contains 300-350 million alveoli. The alveoli provide maximum surface for exchange of gases. The alveoli have very thin walls and contain an extensive network of blood vessels to facilitate exchange of gases.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q6
rows.question: What are the different ways in which glucose is oxidised do provide energy in various organisms ?
rows.answer:
Every living organism respires for getting oxygen which is essential for releasing energy from food. Glucose is the nutrient of food which provides energy on decomposition. Glucose decomposes in the following ways:
(i) Aerobic respiration: During this respiration, breakdown of pyruvate takes place in the presence of oxygen to give rise to three molecules of carbon dioxide along with the release of energy and water. The release of energy takes place in the form of ATP.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (in mitochondria) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><munder><mrow><mtext> Glucose </mtext></mrow><mrow><mtext> (1 molecule) </mtext></mrow></munder><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (in cytoplasm) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> Glycolysis </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munder><mrow><mtext> Pyruvate </mtext></mrow><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Pyruvic acid) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mtext> (2 molecules) </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></munder><munder><mrow><mn>6</mn><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext> Carbon dioxide </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>6</mn><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow><mrow><mtext> Water </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>38</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></mrow><mrow><mtext> Energy </mtext></mrow></munder></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math>
(ii) Anaerobic respiration: It takes place in the absence of oxygen during which pyruvate is either converted into ethanol and carbon dioxide as in yeast or lactic acid as in human muscles during vigorous exercise. Very little amount of energy (ATP) is produced during anaerobic respiration.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Yeast) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Fermentation) </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> Glucose </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (1 molecule) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> In absence of oxygen </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munder><mrow><mtext> Glycolysis </mtext></mrow><mrow><mtext> (in cytoplasm) </mtext></mrow></munder><munder><mrow><mtext> Pyruvate </mtext></mrow><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Pyruvic acid) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mn>2</mn><mtext> molecules </mtext><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></munder><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext> Carbon dioxide </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></mrow><mrow><mtext> Energy </mtext></mrow></munder></mrow></math>

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q7
rows.question: What advantage over an aquatic organism does a terrestrial organism have with regard to obtaining oxygen for respiration?
rows.answer: Terrestrial organism obtains oxygen for respiration from the atmosphere where the oxygen content is high, whereas, aquatic organisms obtain oxygen dissolved in the water which is very low when compared to the amount in atmosphere. For this reason, aquatic animals breathe faster to obtain as much oxygen as possible, on the other hand, terrestrial animal does not have to breathe faster and thus spends less energy than aquatic organism. So, terrestrial organisms have more advantage over the aquatic animals to obtain oxygen for respiration.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q8
rows.question: How are water and minerals transported in plants ?
rows.answer:
The water and minerals are transported by xylem tissue in the plants. Xylem vessels and tracheids form a continuous system of water conducting channels reaching all parts of the plant. The process of transpiration also plays an important role in transport of water and minerals in the plants.
Hence, the transport of water and minerals takes place in the following ways:
(i) Transport of water and minerals by root pressure: The xylem tissue is present in stems, roots, leaves of the plant. The root cells that are in close contact with the soil actively uptake ions, which create a difference between in the concentration of soil and roots cells, due to this difference water and minerals moves into the root cells and transported in various parts of the plant.
(ii) Transport of water and minerals by transpiration: There is a continuous loss of water through stomata in the form of water vapour, this process is known as transpiration. Transpiration creates a suction which helps in absorption and upward movement of water and minerals from roots to leaves of the plant.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q9
rows.question: What are the methods used by plants to get rid of excretory products?
rows.answer:
Like animals, plants also pass out the excretory wastes from the body which are as follows :
(i) During photosynthesis, oxygen is produced as a by-product which is passed out from the plant body through stomata.
(ii) The plants take in excess amount of water from the soil for getting required amount of mineral salts. The excess amount of water is released through transpiration.
(iii) Many of tissues consist of dead cells, lose some parts such as leaves.
(iv) Waste products may stored in leaves.
(v) Other waste products are stored as resins and gums, especially in old xylem.
(vi) Plants also excrete some waste substances into the soil around them.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q10
rows.question: Describe the structure and functioning of nephrons.
rows.answer:
Structure of Nephron:
Fig. Structure of a nephron
Nephron is the functional unit of kidney. Each kidney contains about one million nephrons.
Each nephron consists of the following structures :
(i) Malpighian Capsulae: It is a funnel shaped structure situated at one terminal end of the nephron. It receives the glomerulus capillaries.
(a) Bowman's capsule: It is a funnel shaped structure situated at one terminal end of the nephron. It receives the glomerulus capillaries.
(b) Glomerulus: It is a tuft of blood capillaries situated in the Bowman's capsule. This tuft is formed by the incoming arteriole (afferent arterioles) and the out going arteriole (efferent arterioles).
(ii) Renal tuble: It consists of three main parts:
(a) Proximal tubular part: It is comprising of a convoluted part i.e. coiled part and a thick straight descending part of Henle's loop which is permeable to water.
(b) 'U'-Shaped Henle's Loop: Distal tubular part is comprising of thick straight ascending limb of Henle's loop which is impermeable to water. It is convoluted part.
(iii) Collecting tubule: The distal convoluted tubules open into the collecting tubule.
Collecting tubules unite to make common ducts that pass through the medulla, reach the renal papilla and enter the renal pelvis. Pelvis is an expanded region which opens into the ureter.
- Functions of nephron:
(i) Filtration: Filtration of blood takes place in Bowman's capsule from the capillaries of glomerulus. The filtrate passes into the tubular part of the nephron. This filtrate contains glucose, amino acids, urea, uric acid, salts and a major amount of water.
(ii) Reabsorption: As the filtrate flows along the tubule, useful substances such as glucose, amino acids, salts and water are selectively reabsorbed into the blood by capillaries surrounding the nephron tubule. The amount of water reabsorbed depends on the need of the body and also on the amount of wastes to be excreted.
(iii) Urine formation: The filtrate which remains after reabsorption is called urine. Urine contains dissolved nitrogenous waste, i.e. urea and uric acid, excess salts and water. Urine is collected from nephrons by the collecting duct to carry it to the ureter.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q11
rows.question: Why is it necessary to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood in mammals and birds?
rows.answer: It is necessary to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood in mammals and birds because if both type of blood gets mixed up in the body the amount of oxygen reaching the organs would be insufficient for their efficient working. As both birds and mammals are warm-blooded animals so they maintain their constant body temperature by cooling themselves when they are in a hotter environment and by warming their bodies when they are in a cooler environment. Hence, these animals require more oxygen ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ) for more cellular respiration so that they can produce more energy to maintain their body temperature. Thus, it is necessary for them to separate oxygenated and de-oxygenated blood, so that their circulatory system is more efficient and can maintain their constant body temperature.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q1
rows.question:
The autotrophic mode of nutrition requires
(a) carbon dioxide and water
(b) chlorophyll
(c) sunlight
(d) all of the above
rows.answer: (d) All the green plants are called autotrophs. This is due to the fact that the green plants make their own food from very simple substances like carbon dioxide and water that is present in the surroundings. They do this by the process of photosynthesis. These green plants contain a green pigment called chlorophyll which further helps in making food by absorbing energy received from the sunlight. Thus, the autotrophic mode of nutrition requires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow></math>, chlorophyll and sunlight.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q2
rows.question:
The breakdown of pyruvate to give carbon dioxide, water and energy takes place in
(a) cytoplasm
(b) mitochondria
(c) chloroplast
(d) nucleus
rows.answer: (b) Breakdown of pyruvate using oxygen takes place in the mitochondria which break up the three-carbon pyrurate molecule to give three molecules of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q3
rows.question: What is the role of saliva in the digestion of food ?
rows.answer:
Saliva is secreted by salivary glands. It has following important roles in the digestion of food:
(i) The primary role of saliva is to mix with the food, moisten it, and enable it to form bolus (small balls) that are easy to swallow and pass through the oesophagus (food pipe) into the stomach.
(ii) Saliva also contains an enzyme called salivary amylase or ptyalin which breaks down complex carbohydrates like starch into simpler carbohydrates like maltose and dextrin which are then further broken down in the stomach.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q4
rows.question: How are fats digested in our bodies ? Where does this process take place?
rows.answer:
The small intestine is the site of complete digestion of fats. Bile juice makes the acidic food into alkaline form. Bile juice converts the fat into fine globules. This process is called emulsification and converted form of fat is called emulsified fat. Lipase, fat digesting enzyme of pancreatic juice converts fat globules into fatty acid and glycerol.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mtext> Fats </mtext><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> by bile salts </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> emulsified </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><mtext> Emulsified fats </mtext><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> juice </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> pancreatic </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math>
Breakdown of fats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> juice lipase </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> intestinal </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math> Fatty acids + Glycerol

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q5
rows.question: What are the differences between aerobic and anaerobic respiration? Name some organisms that use the anaerobic mode of respiration.
rows.answer:
- Difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration
| S.No. | Aerobic respiration | Anaerobic respiration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | It is a type of respiration that takes place in presence of oxygen. | It is a type of respiration that takes place in absence of oxygen. |
| 2. | It occurs in cytoplasm and mitochondria. | It takes place only in cytoplasm. |
| 3. | Glucose breaks down into carbon dioxide and water. | Glucose breaks down into ethyl alcohol, carbon dioxide, and energy. |
| 4. | 38 molecules of ATP are formed. | Only 2 molecules of ATP are formed. |
| 5. | All higher organisms such as mammals have this type of respiration. | Lower organisms such as bacteria and yeast use this type. In other organisms, it occurs during heavy activities. |
- Anaerobic respiration occurs in yeast, certain bacteria and internal parasites like tapeworm.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q6
rows.question: How are the alveoli designed to maximize the exchange of gases?
rows.answer: The alveoli are the small balloon-like structures present in the lungs. The walls of the alveoli consist of extensive network of blood vessels. Each lung contains 300-350 million alveoli, making it a total of approximately 700 million in both the lungs. The thin, moist, permeable walls of alveoli facilitate quick diffusion and exchange of gases. Moreover it is richly supplied with blood capillaries. When the air is inhaled, it gets filled in the alveoli due to which they expand and provide maximum space for the exchange of gases.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q7
rows.question: Describe double circulation in human beings. Why is it necessary?
rows.answer:
Double circulation is a process in which blood flows twice through the heart. Process of double circulation can be done in the following ways:
(i) First of all the blood is collected from various organs into the right atrium. The right atrium then contracts and passes the deoxygenated blood to the right ventricle, through an auriculo-ventricular aperture.
(ii) Then the right ventricle contracts and passes the deoxygenated blood into the two pulmonary arteries, which pumps it to the lungs where the blood becomes oxygenated.
(iii) From the lungs, the pulmonary veins transport the oxygenated blood to the left atrium of the heart.
(iv) Then the left atrium contracts and through the auriculo-ventricular aperture, the oxygenated blood enters the left ventricle.
(v) The blood passes to aorta from the left ventricle. The aorta gives rise to many arteries that distribute the oxygenated blood to all the regions of the body.
Fig.: Schematic representation of transport and exchange of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
- Double circulation is necessary in human being to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood because this makes their circulatory system more efficient and helps in maintaining constant body temperature.

rows.question:

rows.question: What are the differences between the transport of materials in xylem and phloem ?
rows.answer: (c) Carbon refixation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants occurs in chloroplasts of bundle sheath cells.

rows.title: Check Your Knowledge
rows.question:
Time to Check Your Knowledge
1. A black smoker, a hydrothermal vent found on the seabed, contains higher levels of sulphur. Which type of organisms found here?
2. What are the end products of light reaction of photosynthesis?
3. Arrange the following parts in the order in which food passes after it is released from stomach. rectum, colon, jejunum, caecum, duodenum, ileum
rows.answer:
1. Since sunlight cannot penetrate to such depth in water, chemosynthetic bacteria can be found near the vent. These bacteria, instead of light, harvest chemical energy from the minerals and chemical compounds such as hydrogen sulfide, hydrogen gas, ferrous iron and ammonia releasing from vent to make their food.
2. ATP and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
3. From stomach, food passes into small intestine and then into large intestine. The correct order of parts in which food passes through them during digestion is:
Duodenum ⟶ jejunum ⟶ ileum ⟶ caecum ⟶ colon ⟶ rectum

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q1
rows.question: Ninety percent of the water lost by the plants during transpiration is through the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> of the leaf.

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q2
rows.question: The semi-liquid mixture of partially digested food found in the stomach is called <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> .
rows.answer: chyme

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q3
rows.question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> prevents the entry of food into the respiratory tract.
rows.answer: Dup/Dubb, semilunar

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q4
rows.question:
Second heart sound heard as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> is due to closure of
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> valves at the beginning of ventricular diastole.
rows.answer: ATP

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q5
rows.question: Kidney eliminate the excretory waste materials as their aqueous solution, called <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> .
rows.answer: oxygen

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q6
rows.question: Energy rich compound generated during photosynthesis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> .
rows.answer: (d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants are more efficient in photosynthesis than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants due to absence of photorespiration. Photorespiration is an inhibitory process that decreases the rate of photosynthesis. It is absent in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants and present only in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants.

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q7
rows.question: Pressure in the arteries during ventricular relaxation is called <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> pressure.

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q8
rows.question: Diffusion is insufficient to meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> requirement of multicellular organisms like humans.
rows.answer:
(a) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> from the respiratory tissues to the lungs is transported by the blood in 3 ways :
(i) In dissolved state or a a solution : Very small amount dissolved in plasma ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> i.e. 0.3 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> by each 100 ml of blood).
(ii) Bicarbonate ions : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>70</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 2.5 ml per 100 ml of blood) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> diffuses in plasma \& then into RBCs where it (in the presence of carbonic anhydrase) combines with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow></math> to form carbonic acid which is almost spontaneously dissociated into hydrogen ion \& bicarbonate ions.
(iii) Carbaminohaemoglobin : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>23</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 1 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> per 100 ml of blood) combines with haemoglobin forming an unstable compound.

rows.answer: True

rows.answer: True

rows.answer: False

rows.answer: True

rows.answer: True

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — True / False: — Q6

rows.answer: False

rows.answer: False

rows.answer: True

rows.answer: True

rows.question: Define translocation.
rows.answer: Translocation is the movement of materials in solution form within an organism especially in phloem of plants.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q2
rows.question: What is glomerulus?
rows.answer: Glomerulus is a bunch of fine blood vessels or capillaries present in the depression of Bowman's capsule where ultrafiltration occurs.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q3
rows.question: What is osmoregulation?
rows.answer: Maintenance of a fixed osmotic concentration of body fluids by controlling the amount of water and salts is called osmoregulation.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Define lenticels.
rows.answer: Lenticels are the aerating pores present in cork or bark of the plants that allows gaseous exchange between atmosphere and internal tissues.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q5
rows.question: What is holozoic nutrition?
rows.answer: Holozoic nutrition is a method of nutrition that involves the ingestion of liquid or solid organic material, digestion, absorption and assimilation of it to utilise it.

Write two different ways in which glucose is oxidized to provide energy in human body. Write the products formed in each case.
[2019]
rows.answer:
(a) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> from the respiratory tissues to the lungs is transported by the blood in 3 ways :
(i) In dissolved state or a a solution : Very small amount dissolved in plasma ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> i.e. 0.3 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> by each 100 ml of blood).
(ii) Bicarbonate ions : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>70</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 2.5 ml per 100 ml of blood) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> diffuses in plasma \& then into RBCs where it (in the presence of carbonic anhydrase) combines with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow></math> to form carbonic acid which is almost spontaneously dissociated into hydrogen ion \& bicarbonate ions.
(iii) Carbaminohaemoglobin : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>23</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> (i.e. 1 ml of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> per 100 ml of blood) combines with haemoglobin forming an unstable compound.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What would happen if all the green plants disappear from the earth?
rows.answer: Green plants are the source of food for all the organisms. If all the green plants disappear from the earth, then all the organisms (herbivores, carnivores and omnivores) will die due to starvation.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Explain why a land plant may die if its roots remain waterlogged for a long time.
rows.answer: A land plant may die if its roots remain water logged for a long time because too much water expels all the air from in-between the soil particles. Due to this, oxygen is not available to the roots for aerobic respiration. Under these conditions the roots will respire anaerobically producing alcohol which may kill the plant.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Explain how it would benefit deep sea divers if humans also had gills.
rows.answer: The deep sea divers could remain under sea water even without carrying oxygen cylinders for breathing because they could then extract dissolved oxygen from water for breathing purpose just like fish.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Expain why, plants have low energy needs as compared to animals.
rows.answer:
Plants are stationary. They are autotrophic and do not have to move from one place to another in search of their food. Hence, their energy requirement is less and therefore, most of the plant tissues are dead with no living protoplasm.
On the other hand, animals are heterotrophic and have to move in search of food, mate, and shelter. Hence, they require more energy and therefore, most of the animal tissues are living.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Why do the walls of trachea not collapse when there is less air in it?
rows.answer: Trachea is supported by c-shaped rings of cartilage which hold them in place when there is less air inside it. So, the walls of trachea do not collapse even when there is a little air.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Reasoning based Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Why do some people need to use a dialysis machine? what does the machine do?
rows.answer: Some people need to use dialysis machines because they have lost function of their own kidneys. The dialysis machine essentially removes excess fluid from the body, cleans the blood of urea (toxic build-up), and balances electrolytes.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why bile juice is necessary for digestion of food however it does not contain enzymes ?
rows.answer: It neutralizes the acidic nature of chyme and provides strong alkaline medium for the action of pancreatic juice and intestinal juice.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Why is sino-atrial node also called pace maker ?
rows.answer: Sino-atrial node also called pace maker because it determines the rate of heart beat by determining the rate of discharge of cardiac impulse.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Explain why constriction of skin blood vessels on a cold winter day is beneficial.
rows.answer: Constriction of blood vessels to the skin reduces blood flow to the skin, which cools as a result. The benefit is that less heat is lost through the skin to the environment and the internal body temperature is maintained. As the difference in temperature between the skin and the environment decreases, less heat is lost. If the skin temperature decreases too much, however, dilation of blood vessels to the skin occurs, which functions to prevent the skin from becoming so cold that it is damaged.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Predict what would happen if vitamins were broken down during the process of digestion rather than being absorbed intact into the circulation.
rows.answer: If vitamins are broken down during the process of digestion, their structures are destroyed, and as a result, their ability to function as vitamins is lost. Because vitamins cannot be synthesized in adequate amounts, vitamin deficiency diseases would occur.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Explain what happen to your throat when you sleep with your mouth open, especially when your nasal passages are plugged as a result of having a cold.
rows.answer: When you sleep with your mouth open, less air passes through the nasal passages. This is especially true when nasal passages are plugged because you have a cold. As a consequence, air is not humidified and warmed. Thus, dry air dries the throat and the trachea.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Predict the effect on the heart if blood flow through the anterior inter-ventricular artery is restricted or completely blocked.
rows.answer: The anterior interventricular artery supplies blood to the anterior wall of the heart and to much of the left ventricle. A blocked anterior interventricular artery reduces the oxygen supply to the portion of the heart that is supplied by that artery, and the cardiac muscle in that area is not able to contract effectively. Thus, the left ventricle on the anterior surface of the heart does not contract normally.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q7
rows.question: If blood supply is reduced in a small area of the heart through which the left bundle branch passed, predict the effect on ventricular contractions.
rows.answer: If the normal blood supply is reduced in a small area of the heart through which the left bundle branch passes, conduction of action potentials through that side of the heart is reduced or blocked. As a consequence, the left side of the heart contracts more slowly. The right side of the heart contracts more normally. The reduced rate of contraction of the left ventricle reduces the pumping effectiveness of the left ventricle.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q8
rows.question:
Please read the following data of a patient and then answer the questions. Also a box diagram is given where the four chambers of heart are indicated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></math>, C, D.
Cardiac output = 4 litre/minute
Pulse rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo>/</mo></mrow></math> minute
(i) Label the correct names of heart chambers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi></mrow></math>, D.
(ii) How much amount of blood is transferred from chamber C to chamber D in a single heart beat ?
(iii) How much amount of blood (approximately) is present in the Arteries and veins respectively of this person at any moment of time ?
(iv) What events take place in the auricles and ventricles in the cardiac cycle of this person after end of second heart sound and before onset of first heart sound ?
rows.answer:
(i) A : Right ventricle
B : Left auricle
C : Left ventricle
D : Right auricle
(ii) 50 ml
(iii) Veins 3200 ml , Arteries - 650 ml
(iv) Auricular diastole, ventricular diastole and auricular systole

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What are differences between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition?
rows.answer:
Difference between antotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition
| S.No. | Autotrophic nutrition | Heterotrophic nutrition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is the nutrition in which organic food is manufactured from inorganic raw materials. | It is the nutrition in which organic food is obtained from various sources. |
| (ii) | It is a type of nutrition in which organism synthesise their own food by converting simple inorganic substances into complex organic substances. | It is a type of nutrition in which an organism can not synthesise their own food but obtain its nutrition or food from the autotrophs directly or indirectly. |
| (iii) | An external source of energy (Sun) is required for synthesis of organic substances (photosynthesis). | An external source of energy is not required. It is obtained by the oxidisation of food. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (iv) | Inorganic substances constitute the raw materials for synthesis of food. | Inorganic substances are not much required. |
| (v) | Chlorophyll is present for trapping light energy. | Chlorophyll is absent. |
| (vi) | Organisms performing autotrophic nutrition function as producers. | Organisms performing heterotrophic nutrition function as consumers. |
| (vi) | Examples: Green plants, some bacteria, some protists. | Examples: Animals, many protists and monerans. |

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q2
rows.question: What is the role of acid in our stomach ?
rows.answer:
The hydrochloric acid in our stomach facilitates the following:
(i) It creates acidic medium by which inactive enzyme pepsinogen is converted to active pepsin, which is a protein digesting enzyme.
(ii) It give signals to pancreas for producing digestive juices and enzymes to further breakdown food.
(iii) It gets mixed with food and kills bacteria present in food.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Where do plants get such raw materials required for photosynthesis?
rows.answer:
Photosynthesis requires carbon dioxide, sunlight, water and chlorophyll as its basic raw materials:
(i) Carbon dioxide: It is a gas present in the atmosphere. Plants absorb carbon dioxide through small pores (called stomata) on the surface of their leaves. This is a source of carbon for the plants.
(ii) Water: It is a liquid form, absorbed by the roots, transported through the stem to leaves by a special structures called as xylem tissues.
(iii) Chlorophyll: It is a green coloured pigment in the chloroplasts of the cells. It traps energy from the sunlight.
(iv) Sunlight: It is an important factor for the photosynthesis, which is absorbed by the chlorophyll and other green parts of the plants.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Why is diffusion insufficient to meet the oxygen requirements of multicellular organisms like humans?
rows.answer:
Diffusion is insufficient to meet the oxygen requirements of multicellular organisms like humans because of the following reasons:
- The body design is very complex and the oxygen demand is quite high. The oxygen requirements of an animal cannot be met by the process of diffusion as it is a very slow process.
- All the cells of the body are not in direct contact with the surrounding environment.
- Intake of oxygen in multicellular organism takes place by one organ and used in another organ.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q5
rows.question: How are the lungs designed in human beings to maximise the area for exchange of gases ?
rows.answer:
In the lungs, trachea divides into smaller tubes, called 'bronchi' which inturn forms 'bronchioles'. The bronchioles terminate in balloon - like structures, called
as 'alveoli'. Each lung contains 300-350 million alveoli. The alveoli provide maximum surface for exchange of gases. The alveoli have very thin walls and contain an extensive network of blood vessels to facilitate exchange of gases.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q6
rows.question: What are the different ways in which glucose is oxidised do provide energy in various organisms ?
rows.answer:
Every living organism respires for getting oxygen which is essential for releasing energy from food. Glucose is the nutrient of food which provides energy on decomposition. Glucose decomposes in the following ways:
(i) Aerobic respiration: During this respiration, breakdown of pyruvate takes place in the presence of oxygen to give rise to three molecules of carbon dioxide along with the release of energy and water. The release of energy takes place in the form of ATP.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (in mitochondria) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><munder><mrow><mtext> Glucose </mtext></mrow><mrow><mtext> (1 molecule) </mtext></mrow></munder><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (in cytoplasm) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> Glycolysis </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munder><mrow><mtext> Pyruvate </mtext></mrow><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Pyruvic acid) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mtext> (2 molecules) </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></munder><munder><mrow><mn>6</mn><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext> Carbon dioxide </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>6</mn><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow><mrow><mtext> Water </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>38</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></mrow><mrow><mtext> Energy </mtext></mrow></munder></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math>
(ii) Anaerobic respiration: It takes place in the absence of oxygen during which pyruvate is either converted into ethanol and carbon dioxide as in yeast or lactic acid as in human muscles during vigorous exercise. Very little amount of energy (ATP) is produced during anaerobic respiration.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Yeast) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Fermentation) </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> Glucose </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> (1 molecule) </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> In absence of oxygen </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><munder><mrow><mtext> Glycolysis </mtext></mrow><mrow><mtext> (in cytoplasm) </mtext></mrow></munder><munder><mrow><mtext> Pyruvate </mtext></mrow><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mtext> (Pyruvic acid) </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mn>2</mn><mtext> molecules </mtext><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></munder><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mtext> Carbon dioxide </mtext></mrow></munder><mo>+</mo><munder><mrow><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></mrow><mrow><mtext> Energy </mtext></mrow></munder></mrow></math>

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q7
rows.question: What advantage over an aquatic organism does a terrestrial organism have with regard to obtaining oxygen for respiration?
rows.answer: Terrestrial organism obtains oxygen for respiration from the atmosphere where the oxygen content is high, whereas, aquatic organisms obtain oxygen dissolved in the water which is very low when compared to the amount in atmosphere. For this reason, aquatic animals breathe faster to obtain as much oxygen as possible, on the other hand, terrestrial animal does not have to breathe faster and thus spends less energy than aquatic organism. So, terrestrial organisms have more advantage over the aquatic animals to obtain oxygen for respiration.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q8
rows.question: How are water and minerals transported in plants ?
rows.answer:
The water and minerals are transported by xylem tissue in the plants. Xylem vessels and tracheids form a continuous system of water conducting channels reaching all parts of the plant. The process of transpiration also plays an important role in transport of water and minerals in the plants.
Hence, the transport of water and minerals takes place in the following ways:
(i) Transport of water and minerals by root pressure: The xylem tissue is present in stems, roots, leaves of the plant. The root cells that are in close contact with the soil actively uptake ions, which create a difference between in the concentration of soil and roots cells, due to this difference water and minerals moves into the root cells and transported in various parts of the plant.
(ii) Transport of water and minerals by transpiration: There is a continuous loss of water through stomata in the form of water vapour, this process is known as transpiration. Transpiration creates a suction which helps in absorption and upward movement of water and minerals from roots to leaves of the plant.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q9
rows.question: What are the methods used by plants to get rid of excretory products?
rows.answer:
Like animals, plants also pass out the excretory wastes from the body which are as follows :
(i) During photosynthesis, oxygen is produced as a by-product which is passed out from the plant body through stomata.
(ii) The plants take in excess amount of water from the soil for getting required amount of mineral salts. The excess amount of water is released through transpiration.
(iii) Many of tissues consist of dead cells, lose some parts such as leaves.
(iv) Waste products may stored in leaves.
(v) Other waste products are stored as resins and gums, especially in old xylem.
(vi) Plants also excrete some waste substances into the soil around them.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q10
rows.question: Describe the structure and functioning of nephrons.
rows.answer:
Structure of Nephron:
Fig. Structure of a nephron
Nephron is the functional unit of kidney. Each kidney contains about one million nephrons.
Each nephron consists of the following structures :
(i) Malpighian Capsulae: It is a funnel shaped structure situated at one terminal end of the nephron. It receives the glomerulus capillaries.
(a) Bowman's capsule: It is a funnel shaped structure situated at one terminal end of the nephron. It receives the glomerulus capillaries.
(b) Glomerulus: It is a tuft of blood capillaries situated in the Bowman's capsule. This tuft is formed by the incoming arteriole (afferent arterioles) and the out going arteriole (efferent arterioles).
(ii) Renal tuble: It consists of three main parts:
(a) Proximal tubular part: It is comprising of a convoluted part i.e. coiled part and a thick straight descending part of Henle's loop which is permeable to water.
(b) 'U'-Shaped Henle's Loop: Distal tubular part is comprising of thick straight ascending limb of Henle's loop which is impermeable to water. It is convoluted part.
(iii) Collecting tubule: The distal convoluted tubules open into the collecting tubule.
Collecting tubules unite to make common ducts that pass through the medulla, reach the renal papilla and enter the renal pelvis. Pelvis is an expanded region which opens into the ureter.
- Functions of nephron:
(i) Filtration: Filtration of blood takes place in Bowman's capsule from the capillaries of glomerulus. The filtrate passes into the tubular part of the nephron. This filtrate contains glucose, amino acids, urea, uric acid, salts and a major amount of water.
(ii) Reabsorption: As the filtrate flows along the tubule, useful substances such as glucose, amino acids, salts and water are selectively reabsorbed into the blood by capillaries surrounding the nephron tubule. The amount of water reabsorbed depends on the need of the body and also on the amount of wastes to be excreted.
(iii) Urine formation: The filtrate which remains after reabsorption is called urine. Urine contains dissolved nitrogenous waste, i.e. urea and uric acid, excess salts and water. Urine is collected from nephrons by the collecting duct to carry it to the ureter.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q11
rows.question: Why is it necessary to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood in mammals and birds?
rows.answer: It is necessary to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood in mammals and birds because if both type of blood gets mixed up in the body the amount of oxygen reaching the organs would be insufficient for their efficient working. As both birds and mammals are warm-blooded animals so they maintain their constant body temperature by cooling themselves when they are in a hotter environment and by warming their bodies when they are in a cooler environment. Hence, these animals require more oxygen ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> ) for more cellular respiration so that they can produce more energy to maintain their body temperature. Thus, it is necessary for them to separate oxygenated and de-oxygenated blood, so that their circulatory system is more efficient and can maintain their constant body temperature.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q1
rows.question:
The autotrophic mode of nutrition requires
(a) carbon dioxide and water
(b) chlorophyll
(c) sunlight
(d) all of the above
rows.answer: (d) All the green plants are called autotrophs. This is due to the fact that the green plants make their own food from very simple substances like carbon dioxide and water that is present in the surroundings. They do this by the process of photosynthesis. These green plants contain a green pigment called chlorophyll which further helps in making food by absorbing energy received from the sunlight. Thus, the autotrophic mode of nutrition requires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>H</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi></mrow></math>, chlorophyll and sunlight.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q2
rows.question:
The breakdown of pyruvate to give carbon dioxide, water and energy takes place in
(a) cytoplasm
(b) mitochondria
(c) chloroplast
(d) nucleus
rows.answer: (b) Breakdown of pyruvate using oxygen takes place in the mitochondria which break up the three-carbon pyrurate molecule to give three molecules of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q3
rows.question: What is the role of saliva in the digestion of food ?
rows.answer:
Saliva is secreted by salivary glands. It has following important roles in the digestion of food:
(i) The primary role of saliva is to mix with the food, moisten it, and enable it to form bolus (small balls) that are easy to swallow and pass through the oesophagus (food pipe) into the stomach.
(ii) Saliva also contains an enzyme called salivary amylase or ptyalin which breaks down complex carbohydrates like starch into simpler carbohydrates like maltose and dextrin which are then further broken down in the stomach.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q4
rows.question: How are fats digested in our bodies ? Where does this process take place?
rows.answer:
The small intestine is the site of complete digestion of fats. Bile juice makes the acidic food into alkaline form. Bile juice converts the fat into fine globules. This process is called emulsification and converted form of fat is called emulsified fat. Lipase, fat digesting enzyme of pancreatic juice converts fat globules into fatty acid and glycerol.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mtext> Fats </mtext><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> by bile salts </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> emulsified </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover><mtext> Emulsified fats </mtext><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> juice </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> pancreatic </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math>
Breakdown of fats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><munderover><mstyle scriptlevel="0"><mo>→</mo></mstyle><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> juice lipase </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded><mpadded width="+0.833em" lspace="0.556em" voffset="-.2em" height="-.2em"><mrow><mtext> intestinal </mtext></mrow><mspace depth=".25em" /></mpadded></munderover></mrow></math> Fatty acids + Glycerol

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q5
rows.question: What are the differences between aerobic and anaerobic respiration? Name some organisms that use the anaerobic mode of respiration.
rows.answer:
- Difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration
| S.No. | Aerobic respiration | Anaerobic respiration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. | It is a type of respiration that takes place in presence of oxygen. | It is a type of respiration that takes place in absence of oxygen. |
| 2. | It occurs in cytoplasm and mitochondria. | It takes place only in cytoplasm. |
| 3. | Glucose breaks down into carbon dioxide and water. | Glucose breaks down into ethyl alcohol, carbon dioxide, and energy. |
| 4. | 38 molecules of ATP are formed. | Only 2 molecules of ATP are formed. |
| 5. | All higher organisms such as mammals have this type of respiration. | Lower organisms such as bacteria and yeast use this type. In other organisms, it occurs during heavy activities. |
- Anaerobic respiration occurs in yeast, certain bacteria and internal parasites like tapeworm.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q6
rows.question: How are the alveoli designed to maximize the exchange of gases?
rows.answer: The alveoli are the small balloon-like structures present in the lungs. The walls of the alveoli consist of extensive network of blood vessels. Each lung contains 300-350 million alveoli, making it a total of approximately 700 million in both the lungs. The thin, moist, permeable walls of alveoli facilitate quick diffusion and exchange of gases. Moreover it is richly supplied with blood capillaries. When the air is inhaled, it gets filled in the alveoli due to which they expand and provide maximum space for the exchange of gases.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q7
rows.question: Describe double circulation in human beings. Why is it necessary?
rows.answer:
Double circulation is a process in which blood flows twice through the heart. Process of double circulation can be done in the following ways:
(i) First of all the blood is collected from various organs into the right atrium. The right atrium then contracts and passes the deoxygenated blood to the right ventricle, through an auriculo-ventricular aperture.
(ii) Then the right ventricle contracts and passes the deoxygenated blood into the two pulmonary arteries, which pumps it to the lungs where the blood becomes oxygenated.
(iii) From the lungs, the pulmonary veins transport the oxygenated blood to the left atrium of the heart.
(iv) Then the left atrium contracts and through the auriculo-ventricular aperture, the oxygenated blood enters the left ventricle.
(v) The blood passes to aorta from the left ventricle. The aorta gives rise to many arteries that distribute the oxygenated blood to all the regions of the body.
Fig.: Schematic representation of transport and exchange of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
- Double circulation is necessary in human being to separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood because this makes their circulatory system more efficient and helps in maintaining constant body temperature.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q1
rows.question:
Major function of contractile vacuole is
(a) Excretion
(b) Circulation
(c) Osmoregulation
(d) All of these

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q2
rows.question: What are the differences between the transport of materials in xylem and phloem ?
rows.answer: (c) Carbon refixation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants occurs in chloroplasts of bundle sheath cells.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q3
rows.question: Compare the functioning of alveoli in the lungs and nephrons in the kidneys with respect to their structure and functioning.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q1
rows.question:
If salivary amylase is lacking in the saliva, which of the following events in the mouth cavity will be affected ?
(a) Proteins breaking down into amino acids.
(b) Starch breaking down into sugars.
(c) Fats breaking down into fatty acids and glycerol.
(d) Absorption of vitamins.
rows.answer:
(b) The human saliva contains an enzyme called salivary amylase, which breaks down the starch
present in food into suger. The digestion of starch(carbohydrates) begins in the mouth. In case, saliva is lacking, it will affect the break down of starch.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q2
rows.question:
The inner lining of stomach is protected by one of the following from hydrochloric acid. Choose the correct one.
(a) Pepsin
(b) Mucus
(c) Salivary amylase
(d) Bile

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q3
rows.question:
Which part of alimentary canal receives bile from the liver?
(a) Stomach
(b) Small intestine
(c) Large intestine
(d) Oesophagus
rows.answer: (b) Bile is secreted by liver and is stored in gall bladder from where it enters the small intestine via common duct.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q4
rows.question:
A few drops of iodine solution were added to rice water. The solution turned blue-black in colour. This indicates that rice water contains
(a) complex proteins
(b) simple proteins
(c) fats
(d) starch
rows.answer: (d) When a few drops of iodine are added to the food containing starch, it turns blue-black in colour showing the presence of starch. If starch amylase is not present, then the color will stay orange or yellow.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q5
rows.question:
In which of the following vertebrate group/groups, heart does not pump oxygenated blood to different parts of the body?
(a) Pisces and amphibians
(b) Amphibians and reptiles
(c) Amphibians only
(d) Pisces only
rows.answer:
(d) In pisces (fish) heart does not pump oxygenated blood to different parts of the body. The oxygenated blood is supplied from the gills to the body by dorsal aorta. Thus, in fish, heart receives only deoxygenated blood.
(i) Protein
(ii) Starch
(iii) Protein
(iv) Fats

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q6
rows.question:
Name the correct substrates for the following enzymes:
(i) Trypsin
(ii) Amylase
(iii) Pepsin
(iv) Lipase
rows.answer: All are parasites as they derive nutrition from plants or animals without killing them.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q7
rows.question: What is common for cuscuta, ticks and leeches?
rows.answer: The effect of the platelet response is to stop the bleeding and to form the site of the developing blood clot, or thrombus. If platelets are absent, this important defense reaction cannot occur, and protracted bleeding from small wounds (prolonged bleeding time) results.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q8
rows.question: What will happen if platelets were absent in the blood?
rows.answer: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) produced during respiration in living organisms and also during photosynthesis in plants.

rows.title: Passage based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q9
rows.question: Name the energy currency in the living organisms. When and where is it produced?
rows.answer:
This plant will not remain healthy for a long time because
(a) It will not get oxygen for respiration.
(b) It will not get carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.

Glucose is the prime source of energy in our body. However, it is stored in the form of glycogen in the muscle and liver of animals and in the form of starch in plants. As a result, everytime a cell requires glucose, it must hydrolyze glycogen which is an energy consuming process. Why does the cell store glycogen instead of glucose in free form?
[NTSE]
(a) Glycogen is more compact and more hydrophilic.
(b) Storage of glucose in free form will consume more ATP.
(c) Glucose in the free form creates more osmotic pressure.
(d) Glucose is highly reactive molecule hence storing in the free form can result in unwanted reactions in the cells.
rows.answer:
Glucose is the prime source of energy in our body. However, it is stored in the form of glycogen in the muscle and liver of animals and in the form of starch in plants. As a result, everytime a cell requires glucose, it must hydrolyze glycogen which is an energy consuming process. Why does the cell store glycogen instead of glucose in free form?
[NTSE]
(a) Glycogen is more compact and more hydrophilic.
(b) Storage of glucose in free form will consume more ATP.
(c) Glucose in the free form creates more osmotic pressure.
(d) Glucose is highly reactive molecule hence storing in the free form can result in unwanted reactions in the cells.
