rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q4
rows.question: What constitutes the central and peripheral nervous systems? How are the components of central nervous system protected?
rows.answer:
Central Nervous System (CNS) - It is hollow part of nervous system that lies along the mid-dorsal part of the the body. It has two parts, brain (located in the head) and spinal cord (located in the trunk),
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) - It is solid lateral part of the nervous system that develops from CNS and connects different parts of the body with CNS. It has two components, voluntary and involuntary. Voluntary peripheral nervous system is under the control of will. It consists of cranial nerves from brain and spinal nerves from spinal cord. Involuntary peripheral nervous system works independent of will. It develops from some cranial and spinal nerves. It is also called autonomic nervous system. It has two parts, sympathetic (for emergency) and parasympathetic (for moderation). They control the functioning of various internal body parts.
The components of central nervous system is protected by cranium (brain) and by vertebral column (spinal cord).

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Name the various plant harmones. Also give their physiological effects on plant growth and development.
rows.answer:
Five types of plant hormones are - auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene and abscisic acid•
Auxin - Cell enlargement, root formation, apical dominance, inhibition of abscission, fruit growth.
Gibberellin - Growth in stem and leaves, higher fruit yield, overcoming dormancy.
Cytokinin - Essential for cell division, differentiation, prevention of senescene and overcoming apical dominance.
Ethylene - Promotes transverse growth, fruit ripening and overcoming dormancy of some parts.
Abscisic Acid (ABA) - Induces dormancy, senescence and abscission, checking excessive activity of growth promoting hormones, closure of stomata under water stress.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q6
rows.question: What are the major parts of the brain? Mention the function of different parts.
rows.answer:
Brain is the most important coordinating centre in the body. It has three major parts or regions namely the forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain.
| Parts | Functions |
| :--- | :--- |
| Forebrain | |
| Cerebrum or Cerebral hemispheres Olfactory lobes Diencephalon | Main thinking part of the brain. Intelligence and voluntary actions. Centre of smell Has centre of hunger, thirstm etc. |
| Midbrain | |
| Optic lobe | Controls reflex movements of the neck, head and trunk in response to visual and auditory stimuli. Also controls the reflex movements of the eye muscles, changes in pupil size and shape of the eye lens. |
| Hindbrain | |
| Cerebellum | Regulates respiration, Relays information between the cerebellum and the cerebrum. Maintains posture and balance of the body. Enables us to make precise and accurate movements. |
| (a) Thyroxine | Regulates metabolism of |
carbohydrates, fats and proteins, release energy for body activity.
(b) Insulin - Regulates blood glucose by its absorption in liver (for formation of glycogen) and individual cells (for metabolic activiry).
(c) Adrenaline - For meeting an emergency by increasing heart rate and supply of blood to various organs.
(d) Growth Hormone (GH) or Somatotrophic Hormone - Regulates growth and development of the body through anabolic activity for growth of bones, muscles and visceral organs.
(e) Testosterone (Male Sex Hormone) - Controls changes in body associated with puberty in males (beard moustache, low pitch voice, increased growth of bones and muscles)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q1
rows.question: How do we detect the smell of an agarabatti (incense stick)?
rows.answer: Smell of an agarbatti (incense stick) is sensed by olfactory receptor present in the nose and then information is transmitted to olfactory lobe located in the forebrain which interpret the information and give us a perception of smell.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Why do you need iodine in your diet?
rows.answer: Iodine is a mineral found in some foods. The body needs iodine to make thyroid hormones. These hormones control the body's metabolism and many other important functions. The body also needs thyroid hormones for proper bone and brain development during pregnancy and infancy.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Why are some patients of diabetes treated by giving injections of insulin ?
rows.answer: Insulin is a hormone secreted by pancreas that regulates the sugar level in the blood. When this hormone is not synthesised in proper amount then the sugar level in the blood rises and the persons suffers from a disease called diabetes. This is the reason why diabetic patients are treated by giving injections of insulin.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q4
rows.question: A person walks across a room in barefeet and puts his foot on a drawing pin lying in the floor. He lets outside a cry. Explain what happens in his nervous system in bringing about this response.
rows.answer: It is an example of a reflex action. The stimulus in this example is pain which is felt by the foot on touching the drawing pin. This pain is sensed by a receptor in the foot. The receptor triggers an impulse to a sensory neuron which transmits the message to the spinal cord. Here, the impulse is passed on to a relay neuron which, in turn, passes it to a motor neuron. The motor neuron passes the impulse to a muscle in the foot. The muscle, then, contracts and pulls the foot away from the drawing pin.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q5
rows.question: The tongue may be considered to be a receptor and an effector organ. Why?
rows.answer: Tongue is considered as a receptor because it has taste buds which act as receptors for tastes; tongue can be considered to be an effector because it is a muscular organ having muscles which can respond to a stimulus.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Why is the closing of a Dandelion flower at dusk ( when it gets darker) not a tropism?
rows.answer: The closing of a Dandelion flower at dusk is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement in response to the intensity of light. On the contrary, tropic movement is the movement of plant parts towards the light. It does not depend upon the intensity of light.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q7
rows.question: Why is the folding up of the leaves of a sensitive plant on touching with a finger not a tropism?
rows.answer: The folding up of leaves in a sensitive plant on touching is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement. Tropism is a directional movement where the direction of the movement of the plant part is towards the stimulus.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why is oxytocin called as 'birth hormone'?
rows.answer: Oxytocin is called as 'birth hormone' because it stimulates the contractions of the smooth muscles of uterus at the end of pregnancy and helps in child birth.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q2
rows.question: How do you support the statement that 'pancreas' is the overall controller of the blood glucose level ?
rows.answer: The statement that "Pancreas is the overall controller of the blood glucose level" is correct. Pancreas secrete insulin which helps the cells to absorb glucose, reducing blood sugar and providing the cells with glucose for energy. When blood sugar levels are too low, the pancreas releases glucagon. Glucagon instructs the liver to release stored glucose, which causes blood sugar to rise.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q3
rows.question: An alcoholic person when drunk, generally walk clumisly. Why?
rows.answer: Alcohol consumption affects cerebellum, which is the area of the brain responsible for coordinating movement and balancing. So, after drinking, the person looses his/ her balance and walks clumsily.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q4
rows.question: A farmer is having a big guava farm, he wants big and healthy fruits to improve his economy. What hormones should be used and why ?
rows.answer:
To promote the growth, the farmer should use the following hormonese:
(i) Auxins : It is responsible for the growth of fruits and plants because it promotes cell enlargement and cell differentiation.
(ii) Cytokinins : It is responsible for cell division.
(iii) Gibberellins : It is responsible for the growth of stem.
(iv) Ethylene : It is responsible for ripening of fruits.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What happens at the synapse between two neurons?
rows.answer: Synapse is a very small region that occurs between the last portion of axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the other neuron. It acts as a one-way passage in which transmission of nerve impulse can takes place in one direction only. At synapse, the nerve ending of a nerve cell converts the nerve impulse into some chemical substance that travels through the gap towards the dendrite of succeeding neuron. The axon terminal of a neuron releases specialised chemicals called neurotransmitters. These chemicals travel through the synapse and reach the dendrites of the successive neuron. These chemical messengers cross the synapse and start an electrical impulse.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Which part of the brain maintains posture and equilibrium of the body?
rows.answer: Cerebellum, a part of hind brain maintains the posture and equilibrium of the body.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q3
rows.question: What is the role of the brain in reflex action ?
rows.answer:
- Reflex actions are sudden involuntary responses which does not involve thinking. For example, when we touch a hot object, we withdraw our hand immediately without thinking. Though, spinal reflexes are produced in the spinal cord, but the message of reflex action taken also goes on to reach the brain. The sensory nerves that detect the heat are connected to the nerves that move the muscles of the hand. Such a connection of detecting the signal from the nerves (input) and responding to it quickly (output) is known as reflex arc.
- Reflex arcs are formed in the spinal cord but the information is still sent to the brain. The brain doesn't have a significant response to it.
Fig.: Reflex arc

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q4
rows.question: How is the movement of leaves of the sensitive plant different from the movement of a shoot towards light?
rows.answer:
| S.No. | Movement of leaves of sensitive plants | Movement of shoot towards light |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is a nastic movement (n o n - d ire c tio n a 1) which does not depend on the direction of stimulus applied. | It is a tropic movement (directional) which depends on the direction of stimulus applied. |
| (ii) | It is sensitive to touch. | It is sensitive to light. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (iii) | It is caused by the sudden loss of water from the swellings at the base of the leaves. | It is caused by the unequal growth on two sides of the shoots |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q5
rows.question: How do auxins promote the growth of a tendril around a support?
rows.answer:
- Tendril is a thread-like growth on the stems and leaves of the climbing plants. These tendrils are sensitive to touch.
- Auxin, synthesised at the tip of the shoot, triggers the growth of tendril around a support. Auxin increase in concentration in the part of the tendril which is away from the support. This results in increased cell division in that part. The part which is nearer to the support has lesser rate of cell division. As a result, the external part of the tendril grows more than the internal part and the tendril coils around the support.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q6
rows.question: How does chemical coordination take place in animals ?
rows.answer: Chemical coordination in animals takes place by the hormones which are secreted by several endocrine glands. The hormones after secretion reach into blood and after that through blood, it reaches to the various parts of the body. For example, adrenaline which is secreted by adrenal gland and through blood it reaches to its target i.e. heart. As a result, the heart beats faster in order to supply more oxygen to the muscles and the target organs get more oxygen and satisfies its need of oxygen to work.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q7
rows.question: How does our body respond when adrenaline is secreted into the blood?
rows.answer:
Adrenaline is a hormone secreted by the adrenal glands (medulla) in case of any danger or emergency or any kinds of stress. It is secreted directly into the blood and is transported to different parts of the body. When secreted in large amount, it speeds up the heartbeat and hence supplies more oxygen to the muscles. The breathing rate also increases due to contraction of diaphragm and rib muscles. It also increases the blood pressure. All these responses enable the body to deal with any stress or emergency.
Fig.: Flowchart of release of adrenaline in blood
Blood rich in oxygen diverted to skeletal muscles from other parts, by reducing blood flow to them
Blood enriched with oxygen by rapid breathing process.
Skeletal muscles ready with excess oxygen for oxidation of food to release energy for fight/flight

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q1
rows.question: How does phototropism occur in plants?
rows.answer:
Phototropism is a directional growth movement which occurs in response to unidirectional exposure to light.
Fig.: Response of the plant to the direction of light
When sunlight/light falls from one direction on the shoot, the growth hormone auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the shoot. This increased concentration of auxin stimulates the cells to grow longer on the shaded side of the shoot. This enhanced growth on one side cause bending of shoot towards light.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q2
rows.question: Which signals will get disrupted in case of spinal cord injury?
rows.answer:
In case of spinal cord injury the following signals will be disrupted:
(i) Reflex actions will be disturbed because reflex arcs are located in the spinal cord. So, the quick responses needed to guard the body will not take place. The delayed responses may pose harm to the body. For example, delayed response to touching a hot object may cause burn of the body part.
(ii) Impulses from various body parts will not be communicated to brain.
(iii) Messages from brain to body parts will not be communicated.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q3
rows.question: How does chemical coordination occur in plants?
rows.answer:
Chemical coordination in plants occurs through phytohormones.
These are the chemical compounds which are produced in the cells of the tips of main stem and branches. These hormones are diffused from the originating cells to the neighbouring cells through diffusion. They are responsible for cell elongation, cell multiplication, opening of flowers etc. The important plant hormones are auxins, cytokinins, gibberellins, abscisic acid (ABA) and ethylene.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q4
rows.question: What is the need for a system of control and coordination in an organism ?
rows.answer: There are various organs and organ systems in a complex organism. These organ systems must be carefully controlled and coordinated for healthy survival of the organism. A lack of coordination between these systems can cause lot of troubles for the organism. In the body of an organism various chemicals are secreted from the glands of the endocrine system. These are referred as hormones which are responsible for the overall growth and development of an organism. All other daily decisions that include voluntary and involuntary action are controlled by central nervous system (CNS).

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q5
rows.question: What is the function of receptors in our body? Think of situations where receptors do not work properly. What problems are likely to arise ?
rows.answer:
Receptors refers to some cells, tissues, and organs which are capable of receiving particular stimuli and initiate impulses to be picked up by sensory nerves. These nerves are located in our sense organs such as the inner ear, nose, tongue, eye, skin etc. In case, any of the receptors do not
work properly then the person will not perceive that particular information, e.g., if our gustatory receptors located in tongue do not work properly then we will not be able to perceive the taste of food such as sour, sweet, salty or bitter.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q6
rows.question: How are involuntary actions and reflex actions different from each other?
rows.answer:
Difference between involuntary actions and reflex actions -
| S.No. | Involuntary actions | Reflex actions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Action which is not controlled by our will is called involuntary action. | Sudden action in response to something in the environment is called reflex action. |
| (ii) | They are connected with functioning of internal body part. | They are connected with response of stimuli. |
| (iii) | It is controlled by brain. | It is controlled by spinal cord. |
| (iv) | Brain takes the judgement and then responds accordingly. | There is no need of thinking. |
| (v) | There is need for thinking and not environmental stimulus for response. | Response is given to the concerned organ just after receiving the stimulus. |
| (vi) | Example- breathing, heartbeat, etc. | Example- Closing the eyes, when bright light is focussed. |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q7
rows.question: Compare and contrast nervous and hormonal mechanisms for control and coordination in animals.
rows.answer:
Comparison between nervous and hormonal mechanisms.
| S.No. | Nervous system mechanisms for control and coordination | Hormonal system mechanisms for control and coordination |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | In this type, there is a network of nerves which connects the cells of all the body organs to the central nervous system. | In this type, there is no such system in the body. |
| (ii) | The coordination is controlled by brain and spinal cord. | There is no controlling organ for this coordination. |
| (iii) | All type of functions are controlled by the nerves and the central nervous system. | Special hormone is responsible for particular function. |
| (iv) | There is no quantification effect. | Quantity of hormones affect the organ and the system. |
| (v) | The whole system is fixed at its own place in the body. | Endocrine glands are situated at various places in the body. |
| (vi) | The stimuli and the responses travel in the nerves. | Blood carries the hormones to the concerning body organs. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (vii) | The system functions according to the environmental stimuli. | The coordination of hormones are not affected by the external environment. |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q8
rows.question: What is the difference between the manner in which movement takes place in a sensitive plant and the movement in our legs?
rows.answer:
Difference between movement in sensitive plants and the movement in legs of human being.
| S.No. | Movement in sensitive plants | Movements in legs of human beings |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | The leaves of sensitive plants can sense external stimuli. | No special organs in leg for such action. |
| (ii) | Plant cells do not have specialised movements. | Animal cells have specialised protein which help muscles to contract. |
| (iii) | There is no special tissue in plants for the conduction of information. | It is controlled by the brain and spinal cord. |
| (iv) | The cells change their shape by changing the amount of water in them. | There is no effect of the amount of water on the movement of muscles. |

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q1
rows.question:
The largest number of neurons are found in:
(a) Brain
(b) Retina
(c) Spinal cord
(d) Tongue
rows.answer: (a) In the human body there are 31 pairs of spinal nerves, one on each side of the vertebral column. These are grouped into the corresponding cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral and coccygeal regions of the spine.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q2
rows.question:
Reflex action immediately involves:
(a) Spinal cord
(b) Cerebellum
(c) Medulla oblongata
(d) Optical lobe

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q3
rows.question:
The nerves leading to the central nervous system are called:
(a) Efferent
(b) Afferent
(c) Motor
(d) Relay
rows.answer: (a) The label X represents Node of Ranvier. It speeds up the impulse transmission.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q4
rows.question:
Which of the following is a plant hormone?
(a) Insulin
(b) Thyroxin
(c) Oestrogen
(d) Cytokinin

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q5
rows.question:
Olfactory receptor organs are sensitive for:
(a) Light
(b) Sound
(c) Smell
(d) Equilibrium
rows.answer: (d) The medulla (a sub-region of the brainstem) is a major control centre for the autonomic nervous system. The hypothalamus acts to integrate autonomic functions and receives autonomic regulatory feedback from the limbic system to do so.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q6
rows.question:
Which of the following is not an endocrine gland ?
(a) Pancreas
(b) Liver
(c) Ovary
(d) Thyroid

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q7
rows.question:
In our body, calcium and phosphorus ions are controlled by
(a) Thyroid gland
(b) Pituitary gland
(c) Adrenal gland
(d) Parathyroid gland

rows.question:
Brain stem is formed by the union of:
(a) Optic lobes
(b) Cerebellum with optic lobes
(c) Corpora striata
(d) Corpora quadrigemina

rows.question:
Breathing rate in mammals is controlled by a part of the brain called the:
(a) Thalamus
(b) Hypothalamus
(c) Medulla oblongata
(d) Cerebellum

rows.question:

(a) Liver
(b) Pancreas
(c) Spleen
(d) Gall bladder

rows.question:
A high concentration of synthetic auxins is generally used for
(a) weed control.
(b) enhancing root initiation.
(c) controlling of cell enlargement.
(d) preventing the growth of the lateral buds.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q12
rows.question:
Which of the following acts both as Endocrine (ductless) and Exocrine (with duct) gland?
(a) Pancreas
(b) Liver
(c) Adrenal
(d) Kidney

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q13
rows.question:
The nervous control of pituitary secretion lies in:
(a) Infundibulum
(b) Pituitary centre
(c) Hypothalamus
(d) Medulla oblongata
rows.answer:
(c) Bipolar neurons is a type of neuron which has two extensions - one axon and one dendrite. Bipolar cells are specialised sensory neurons for the transmission
of special senses. As such, they are part of the sensory pathways for smell, sight, taste, hearing and vestibular functions. They are found in the retina of eye.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q14
rows.question:
Neuron becomes an electrically charged cell by the diffusion of:
(a) K
(b) Na
(c) P
(d) Ca
rows.answer: (a) Sympathetic nervous system and parasympathetic nervous system are subdivisions of autonomic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system controls the involuntary functions of internal organs such as heart rate, digestion, breathing, urination, etc. The parasympathetic nervous system controls homoeostasis of the body and is responsible for "rest and digest" response. The sympathetic nervous system controls perceived threat responses thus is responsible for "fight or flight" responses. Thus parasympathetic nerves and sympathetic nerves are known to function in relaying information of involuntary functions of the body.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q15
rows.question:
Nerve cell do not divide because they do not have:
(a) Nucleus
(b) Centrioles
(c) Golgi body
(d) Mitochondria
rows.answer: (c) Auxin has been clearly demonstrated in the leaf sheath or coleoptile of oat plant (Avena sativa). This plant coleoptile has been used for the test of hormone auxin (IAA) participating in the growth of the plant.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q16
rows.question:
Artificial ripening of fruits is carried out by
(a) Auxin
(b) Gibberellin
(c) Abscisic acid
(d) Ethylene
rows.answer: (b) Auxins helps to prevent premature leaf and fruit fall.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q17
rows.question:
Name the hormone that lowers the blood sugar level?
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Oestrogen
(c) Glucagon
(d) Insulin
rows.answer: (b) Corpus luteum is the temporary endocrine gland formed in the ovary after ovulation. It releases hormones like progesterone, oestrogen etc. for the maintenance of pregnancy.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q18
rows.question:
Chemicals which are released at the synaptic junction are called
(a) cerebrospinal fluid
(b) hormones
(c) neurotransmitters
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>y</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></math>
rows.answer: (c) Pre-synaptic membrane is involved in the release of neurotransmitter in the chemical synapse. The receptors sites for neurotransmitters are present on post-synaptic membrane of neuron.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q19
rows.question:
Which of the following tissues provide control and coordination in animals ?
(a) Nervous and Skeletal
(b) Muscular and Skeletal
(c) Muscular and Transport
(d) Nervous and Muscular

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q20
rows.question:
The pineal body is considered as
(a) an endocrine gland.
(b) an organ concerned with voluntary actions.
(c) an organ concerned with vision.
(d) a vestige of third eye and endocrine gland.
rows.answer: (d) The cerebrum is the largest part of the human brain, making up about two-thirds of the brain's mass.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q21
rows.question:
In reflex action, the reflex arc is formed by
(a) brain → spinal cord → muscles
(b) receptor → spinal cord → muscles
(c) muscle → receptor → brain
(d) muscles → spinal cord → receptor
rows.answer: (a) An example of nastic movement in plants is folding up of the leaves of Mimosa pudica. Nastic movements in plants are reversible and repeatable movements in response to a stimulus whose direction is determined by the anatomy of the plant. The leaves of the Mimosa pudica fold up when touched and returns to full leaf in a few minutes. The leaves of the Mimosa achieve this rapid folding by a change in turgor pressure.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q22
rows.question:
Which of the following receptors is incorrectly paired with what it senses?
(a) Chemoreceptors - chemicals
(b) Photoreceptors - pain
(c) Thermoreceptors - heat
(d) Nociceptors - pain
rows.answer: (b) The limbic system (emotional motor system) is responsible for the experience and expression of emotion but not movement. It is located in the core of the brain and includes the amygdala, hippocampus and hypothalamus.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q23
rows.question:
The main effect of cytokinin in plants is to
(a) improve the quality of fruits
(b) prevent the growth of lateral buds
(c) regulate opening and closing of stomata
(d) stimulate cell division
rows.answer:
(a) A pineapple plant flowers only once, and produces one pineapple. Then it dies. But before it dies it also produces offspring.
Auxin and ethylene can be applied to artificially induce flowering in pineapple to increase yield throughout the year. Applied auxin mimic the action of ethylene by stimulating ethylene formation, and that ethylene, not auxin, causes pineapples to flower. Ethylene initiates flowering and synchronising fruit-set in pineapples.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q24
rows.question:
Which of the following protects the brain from shocks?
(a) Durameter
(b) Cerebrospinal fluid
(c) Arachnoid membrane
(d) Cranium
rows.answer:
(c) Insulin is a pancreatic hormone, secreted from beta cells of islets of Langerhans. Its deficiency leads to diabetes mellitus (characterised by high blood sugar levels over a prolonged period).
Thyroxine is an iodine containing hormone, secreted by the thyroid gland. It plays an important role in regulating the body's metabolic rate, heart and digestive function, muscle control, brain development and bone maintenance. Hyper secretion or hyposecretion of thyroxine can be associated with enlargement of thyroid gland called goitre.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q25
rows.question:
Name the hormone which controls the basal metabolic rate in animals.
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Thyroxine
(c) Aldosterone
(d) Oxytocin
rows.answer: (b) Hypothalamus (a region of the forebrain) is the thermoregulatory centre of brain. It coordinates both the autonomic nervous system and the activity of the pituitary, controlling body temperature, thirst, hunger, and other homeostatic systems, and involved in sleep and emotional activity.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q26
rows.question:
Which of the following comments applies to the brains of most animals?
(a) Within the brain, neurons exchange information with one another.
(b) Brains usually lie as near as possible to the important sensory structures in an animal.
(c) Brains send action potentials to the hindmost portion of the animal by means of major nerves.
(d) All of the above
rows.answer: (a) Neurotransmitters are body's chemical messengers which are used by the nervous system to transmit messages between neurons or from neurons to muscles. These molecules degrade rapidly and can travel only short distances. This makes them perfect for crossing the short synapses between neurons.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q27
rows.question:
Phytohormones are:
(a) hormones regulating growth from seed to adulthood.
(b) growth regulators synthesised by plants and influencing physiological processes.
(c) hormones regulating flowering.
(d) hormones regulating secondary growth.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q28
rows.question:
Cytokinins are known to:
(a) inhibit cytoplasmic movement.
(b) help in retention of chlorophyll.
(c) influence water movement.
(d) promote abscission layer formation.
rows.answer: (d) The curvature induced in plant organs in response to the unidirectional light is called phototropism.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q29
rows.question:
The stimulus for geotropism in roots is perceived by:
(a) Root cap
(b) Quiescent centre
(c) Root tip
(d) None of these
rows.answer: (b) Cytokinin promotes bud and shoot formation and increased cell division; auxin promotes root formation and increased cell elongation.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q30
rows.question:
Thymus in mammals is mainly concerned with:
(a) Regulation of body growth
(b) Secretion of thyrotropin
(c) Regulation of body temperature
(d) Immunological functions
rows.answer: (c) Auxin stimulates cell elongation. It accumulates on the shaded side which results in more elongation of cells towards shaded side of the plant. This causes bending of the plant towards source of light.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q31
rows.question:
The primary function of neurotransmitters is:
(a) rapid relaying of nerve signals.
(b) graded control of development.
(c) regulation of cyclical processes.
(d) communication within a nerve cell.
rows.answer: (a) Aldosterone is produced by adrenal cortex and plays an important role in the regulation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>+</mo></mrow></math> levels in the body.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q32
rows.question:
Which hormone causes the milk ejection from the breasts when the baby begins to suckle?
(a) Prolactin
(b) Oxytocin
(c) Progesterone
(d) Oestrogen
rows.answer: (a) Cerebellum maintains the balance and body posture. It is not concerned with logical part.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q33
rows.question:
The hormone important in the glucose metabolism is produced by:
(a) Liver
(b) Thyroid
(c) Pituitary
(d) Pancreas

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q34
rows.question:
Phototropic and geotropic movements in plants have been traced to be linked with:
(a) Enzymes
(b) Starch
(c) Gibberellin
(d) Auxin
rows.answer: (d) Abscisic acid inhibits the cell growth and promotes dormancy in seeds. Auxin initiates division of the vascular cambium.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q35
rows.question:
Which of the following events would likely occur if a plant tissue culture is treated with a solution containing a relatively high concentration of cytokinin and a relatively low concentration of auxin?
(a) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
(b) Bud and shoot formation; slow cell expansion; increased cell division.
(c) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; increased cell division.
(d) Root formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
rows.answer: (c) The nerve has nerve endings at the end and dendrites at front. The nerve endings send signals through synapse and get transported to other neurons dendrites. In this way, the signals get transmitted.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q36
rows.question:
What causes a green plant exposed to the light on only one side, to bend toward the source of light as it grows?
(a) Green plants seek light because they are phototropic.
(b) Light stimulates plant cells on the lighted side to grow faster.
(c) Auxin accumulates on the shaded side, stimulating greater cell elongation there.
(d) Green plants need light to perform photosynthesis.
rows.answer: (d) The pathway involved in reflex action from stimulus to response is called reflex arc. The stimulus is received by a receptor and passed on to the sensory neuron and then to relay neuron. The impulse is transmitted to motor neuron which carries it to the effector organ.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q37
rows.question:
Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
(a) Aldosterone
(b) Insulin
(c) Glucagon
(d) Cortisone
rows.answer: (d) Auxins are synthesized in the growing tips of stems and roots. When light is coming from one side of the plant, auxin flow towards the shady side. The increased concentration of auxin allows the cells to grow longer on the shaded side. Hence, statements (B) and (D) are related to the picture.

rows.question:

(a) Cerebellum - Language comprehension
(b) Corpus callosum - Communication between the left and right cerebral cortices
(c) Cerebrum - Calculation and contemplation
(d) Medulla oblongata - Homeostatic control
rows.answer:

(c) Cerebrum - Calculation and contemplation
(d) Medulla oblongata - Homeostatic control

[NTSE]

(c) growth hormone retardation
(d) capillary action
rows.answer:
When touched, the leaflets of Touch-me-not plant are closed. Closing of leaflets starts from the point of contact to the leaflets away. The leaflets are closed due to

(c) growth hormone retardation

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q42
rows.question:
Correct sequence of reflex arc is
[NTSE]
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> (a) </mtext></mtd><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Receptor → </mtext><mtext> Motor Neuron </mtext><mtext> → </mtext><mtext> Sensory Neuron </mtext><mtext> → </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd columnalign="left" /><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Effector organ </mtext><mtext> → </mtext><mtext> Relay Neuron </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></math> Effector organ → Relay Neuron
(b) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Motor Neuron → Effector organ → Relay Neuron
(c) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Motor Neuron → Relay Neuron → Effector organ
(d) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Relay Neuron → Motor Neuron → Effector organ
rows.answer:
Correct sequence of reflex arc is
[NTSE]
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> (a) </mtext></mtd><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Receptor → </mtext><mtext> Motor Neuron </mtext><mtext> → </mtext><mtext> Sensory Neuron </mtext><mtext> → </mtext></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd columnalign="left" /><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Effector organ </mtext><mtext> → </mtext><mtext> Relay Neuron </mtext></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></math> Effector organ → Relay Neuron
(b) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Motor Neuron → Effector organ → Relay Neuron
(c) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Motor Neuron → Relay Neuron → Effector organ
(d) Receptor → Sensory Neuron → Relay Neuron → Motor Neuron → Effector organ

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q43
rows.question:
The changes that are likely to happen in the plant in the following picture:
[NTSE]
(A) A hormone abscisic acid is synthesized at the shoot tip.
(B) When light is coming from one side of the plant, auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the plant.
(C) Abscisic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> helps the cells to grow longer.
(D) Cells grow longer on the side of the shoot which is away from light.
Select the correct option.
(a) A and B
(b) B and C
(c) C and D
(d) B and D
rows.answer:
The changes that are likely to happen in the plant in the following picture:
[NTSE]
(A) A hormone abscisic acid is synthesized at the shoot tip.
(B) When light is coming from one side of the plant, auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the plant.
(C) Abscisic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> helps the cells to grow longer.
(D) Cells grow longer on the side of the shoot which is away from light.
Select the correct option.
(a) A and B
(b) B and C
(c) C and D
(d) B and D

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q1
rows.question:
Neurons that carry information to an effector are called
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> neurons.
rows.answer: motor

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q2
rows.question: The initial depolarization of the nerve cell membrane involves the movement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> ions into the cell.
rows.answer: synapse

rows.title: Assertion \& Reason: — Fill in the Blanks: — Q3
rows.question:
The functional junction between two neurons is called
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></mrow></math> .

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q1
rows.question: How do we detect the smell of an agarabatti (incense stick)?
rows.answer: Smell of an agarbatti (incense stick) is sensed by olfactory receptor present in the nose and then information is transmitted to olfactory lobe located in the forebrain which interpret the information and give us a perception of smell.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Why do you need iodine in your diet?
rows.answer: Iodine is a mineral found in some foods. The body needs iodine to make thyroid hormones. These hormones control the body's metabolism and many other important functions. The body also needs thyroid hormones for proper bone and brain development during pregnancy and infancy.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Why are some patients of diabetes treated by giving injections of insulin ?
rows.answer: Insulin is a hormone secreted by pancreas that regulates the sugar level in the blood. When this hormone is not synthesised in proper amount then the sugar level in the blood rises and the persons suffers from a disease called diabetes. This is the reason why diabetic patients are treated by giving injections of insulin.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q4
rows.question: A person walks across a room in barefeet and puts his foot on a drawing pin lying in the floor. He lets outside a cry. Explain what happens in his nervous system in bringing about this response.
rows.answer: It is an example of a reflex action. The stimulus in this example is pain which is felt by the foot on touching the drawing pin. This pain is sensed by a receptor in the foot. The receptor triggers an impulse to a sensory neuron which transmits the message to the spinal cord. Here, the impulse is passed on to a relay neuron which, in turn, passes it to a motor neuron. The motor neuron passes the impulse to a muscle in the foot. The muscle, then, contracts and pulls the foot away from the drawing pin.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q5
rows.question: The tongue may be considered to be a receptor and an effector organ. Why?
rows.answer: Tongue is considered as a receptor because it has taste buds which act as receptors for tastes; tongue can be considered to be an effector because it is a muscular organ having muscles which can respond to a stimulus.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Why is the closing of a Dandelion flower at dusk ( when it gets darker) not a tropism?
rows.answer: The closing of a Dandelion flower at dusk is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement in response to the intensity of light. On the contrary, tropic movement is the movement of plant parts towards the light. It does not depend upon the intensity of light.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q7
rows.question: Why is the folding up of the leaves of a sensitive plant on touching with a finger not a tropism?
rows.answer: The folding up of leaves in a sensitive plant on touching is not a tropism because it is a non-directional movement. Tropism is a directional movement where the direction of the movement of the plant part is towards the stimulus.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why is oxytocin called as 'birth hormone'?
rows.answer: Oxytocin is called as 'birth hormone' because it stimulates the contractions of the smooth muscles of uterus at the end of pregnancy and helps in child birth.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q2
rows.question: How do you support the statement that 'pancreas' is the overall controller of the blood glucose level ?
rows.answer: The statement that "Pancreas is the overall controller of the blood glucose level" is correct. Pancreas secrete insulin which helps the cells to absorb glucose, reducing blood sugar and providing the cells with glucose for energy. When blood sugar levels are too low, the pancreas releases glucagon. Glucagon instructs the liver to release stored glucose, which causes blood sugar to rise.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q3
rows.question: An alcoholic person when drunk, generally walk clumisly. Why?
rows.answer: Alcohol consumption affects cerebellum, which is the area of the brain responsible for coordinating movement and balancing. So, after drinking, the person looses his/ her balance and walks clumsily.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — HOTS Questions: — Q4
rows.question: A farmer is having a big guava farm, he wants big and healthy fruits to improve his economy. What hormones should be used and why ?
rows.answer:
To promote the growth, the farmer should use the following hormonese:
(i) Auxins : It is responsible for the growth of fruits and plants because it promotes cell enlargement and cell differentiation.
(ii) Cytokinins : It is responsible for cell division.
(iii) Gibberellins : It is responsible for the growth of stem.
(iv) Ethylene : It is responsible for ripening of fruits.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q1
rows.question: What happens at the synapse between two neurons?
rows.answer: Synapse is a very small region that occurs between the last portion of axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the other neuron. It acts as a one-way passage in which transmission of nerve impulse can takes place in one direction only. At synapse, the nerve ending of a nerve cell converts the nerve impulse into some chemical substance that travels through the gap towards the dendrite of succeeding neuron. The axon terminal of a neuron releases specialised chemicals called neurotransmitters. These chemicals travel through the synapse and reach the dendrites of the successive neuron. These chemical messengers cross the synapse and start an electrical impulse.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Which part of the brain maintains posture and equilibrium of the body?
rows.answer: Cerebellum, a part of hind brain maintains the posture and equilibrium of the body.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q3
rows.question: What is the role of the brain in reflex action ?
rows.answer:
- Reflex actions are sudden involuntary responses which does not involve thinking. For example, when we touch a hot object, we withdraw our hand immediately without thinking. Though, spinal reflexes are produced in the spinal cord, but the message of reflex action taken also goes on to reach the brain. The sensory nerves that detect the heat are connected to the nerves that move the muscles of the hand. Such a connection of detecting the signal from the nerves (input) and responding to it quickly (output) is known as reflex arc.
- Reflex arcs are formed in the spinal cord but the information is still sent to the brain. The brain doesn't have a significant response to it.
Fig.: Reflex arc

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q4
rows.question: How is the movement of leaves of the sensitive plant different from the movement of a shoot towards light?
rows.answer:
| S.No. | Movement of leaves of sensitive plants | Movement of shoot towards light |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | It is a nastic movement (n o n - d ire c tio n a 1) which does not depend on the direction of stimulus applied. | It is a tropic movement (directional) which depends on the direction of stimulus applied. |
| (ii) | It is sensitive to touch. | It is sensitive to light. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (iii) | It is caused by the sudden loss of water from the swellings at the base of the leaves. | It is caused by the unequal growth on two sides of the shoots |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q5
rows.question: How do auxins promote the growth of a tendril around a support?
rows.answer:
- Tendril is a thread-like growth on the stems and leaves of the climbing plants. These tendrils are sensitive to touch.
- Auxin, synthesised at the tip of the shoot, triggers the growth of tendril around a support. Auxin increase in concentration in the part of the tendril which is away from the support. This results in increased cell division in that part. The part which is nearer to the support has lesser rate of cell division. As a result, the external part of the tendril grows more than the internal part and the tendril coils around the support.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q6
rows.question: How does chemical coordination take place in animals ?
rows.answer: Chemical coordination in animals takes place by the hormones which are secreted by several endocrine glands. The hormones after secretion reach into blood and after that through blood, it reaches to the various parts of the body. For example, adrenaline which is secreted by adrenal gland and through blood it reaches to its target i.e. heart. As a result, the heart beats faster in order to supply more oxygen to the muscles and the target organs get more oxygen and satisfies its need of oxygen to work.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Questions: — Q7
rows.question: How does our body respond when adrenaline is secreted into the blood?
rows.answer:
Adrenaline is a hormone secreted by the adrenal glands (medulla) in case of any danger or emergency or any kinds of stress. It is secreted directly into the blood and is transported to different parts of the body. When secreted in large amount, it speeds up the heartbeat and hence supplies more oxygen to the muscles. The breathing rate also increases due to contraction of diaphragm and rib muscles. It also increases the blood pressure. All these responses enable the body to deal with any stress or emergency.
Fig.: Flowchart of release of adrenaline in blood
Blood rich in oxygen diverted to skeletal muscles from other parts, by reducing blood flow to them
Blood enriched with oxygen by rapid breathing process.
Skeletal muscles ready with excess oxygen for oxidation of food to release energy for fight/flight

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q1
rows.question: How does phototropism occur in plants?
rows.answer:
Phototropism is a directional growth movement which occurs in response to unidirectional exposure to light.
Fig.: Response of the plant to the direction of light
When sunlight/light falls from one direction on the shoot, the growth hormone auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the shoot. This increased concentration of auxin stimulates the cells to grow longer on the shaded side of the shoot. This enhanced growth on one side cause bending of shoot towards light.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q2
rows.question: Which signals will get disrupted in case of spinal cord injury?
rows.answer:
In case of spinal cord injury the following signals will be disrupted:
(i) Reflex actions will be disturbed because reflex arcs are located in the spinal cord. So, the quick responses needed to guard the body will not take place. The delayed responses may pose harm to the body. For example, delayed response to touching a hot object may cause burn of the body part.
(ii) Impulses from various body parts will not be communicated to brain.
(iii) Messages from brain to body parts will not be communicated.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q3
rows.question: How does chemical coordination occur in plants?
rows.answer:
Chemical coordination in plants occurs through phytohormones.
These are the chemical compounds which are produced in the cells of the tips of main stem and branches. These hormones are diffused from the originating cells to the neighbouring cells through diffusion. They are responsible for cell elongation, cell multiplication, opening of flowers etc. The important plant hormones are auxins, cytokinins, gibberellins, abscisic acid (ABA) and ethylene.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q4
rows.question: What is the need for a system of control and coordination in an organism ?
rows.answer: There are various organs and organ systems in a complex organism. These organ systems must be carefully controlled and coordinated for healthy survival of the organism. A lack of coordination between these systems can cause lot of troubles for the organism. In the body of an organism various chemicals are secreted from the glands of the endocrine system. These are referred as hormones which are responsible for the overall growth and development of an organism. All other daily decisions that include voluntary and involuntary action are controlled by central nervous system (CNS).

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q5
rows.question: What is the function of receptors in our body? Think of situations where receptors do not work properly. What problems are likely to arise ?
rows.answer:
Receptors refers to some cells, tissues, and organs which are capable of receiving particular stimuli and initiate impulses to be picked up by sensory nerves. These nerves are located in our sense organs such as the inner ear, nose, tongue, eye, skin etc. In case, any of the receptors do not
work properly then the person will not perceive that particular information, e.g., if our gustatory receptors located in tongue do not work properly then we will not be able to perceive the taste of food such as sour, sweet, salty or bitter.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q6
rows.question: How are involuntary actions and reflex actions different from each other?
rows.answer:
Difference between involuntary actions and reflex actions -
| S.No. | Involuntary actions | Reflex actions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Action which is not controlled by our will is called involuntary action. | Sudden action in response to something in the environment is called reflex action. |
| (ii) | They are connected with functioning of internal body part. | They are connected with response of stimuli. |
| (iii) | It is controlled by brain. | It is controlled by spinal cord. |
| (iv) | Brain takes the judgement and then responds accordingly. | There is no need of thinking. |
| (v) | There is need for thinking and not environmental stimulus for response. | Response is given to the concerned organ just after receiving the stimulus. |
| (vi) | Example- breathing, heartbeat, etc. | Example- Closing the eyes, when bright light is focussed. |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q7
rows.question: Compare and contrast nervous and hormonal mechanisms for control and coordination in animals.
rows.answer:
Comparison between nervous and hormonal mechanisms.
| S.No. | Nervous system mechanisms for control and coordination | Hormonal system mechanisms for control and coordination |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | In this type, there is a network of nerves which connects the cells of all the body organs to the central nervous system. | In this type, there is no such system in the body. |
| (ii) | The coordination is controlled by brain and spinal cord. | There is no controlling organ for this coordination. |
| (iii) | All type of functions are controlled by the nerves and the central nervous system. | Special hormone is responsible for particular function. |
| (iv) | There is no quantification effect. | Quantity of hormones affect the organ and the system. |
| (v) | The whole system is fixed at its own place in the body. | Endocrine glands are situated at various places in the body. |
| (vi) | The stimuli and the responses travel in the nerves. | Blood carries the hormones to the concerning body organs. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (vii) | The system functions according to the environmental stimuli. | The coordination of hormones are not affected by the external environment. |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Text - Book Exercise: — Q8
rows.question: What is the difference between the manner in which movement takes place in a sensitive plant and the movement in our legs?
rows.answer:
Difference between movement in sensitive plants and the movement in legs of human being.
| S.No. | Movement in sensitive plants | Movements in legs of human beings |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | The leaves of sensitive plants can sense external stimuli. | No special organs in leg for such action. |
| (ii) | Plant cells do not have specialised movements. | Animal cells have specialised protein which help muscles to contract. |
| (iii) | There is no special tissue in plants for the conduction of information. | It is controlled by the brain and spinal cord. |
| (iv) | The cells change their shape by changing the amount of water in them. | There is no effect of the amount of water on the movement of muscles. |

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q4
rows.question: What constitutes the central and peripheral nervous systems? How are the components of central nervous system protected?
rows.answer:
Central Nervous System (CNS) - It is hollow part of nervous system that lies along the mid-dorsal part of the the body. It has two parts, brain (located in the head) and spinal cord (located in the trunk),
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) - It is solid lateral part of the nervous system that develops from CNS and connects different parts of the body with CNS. It has two components, voluntary and involuntary. Voluntary peripheral nervous system is under the control of will. It consists of cranial nerves from brain and spinal nerves from spinal cord. Involuntary peripheral nervous system works independent of will. It develops from some cranial and spinal nerves. It is also called autonomic nervous system. It has two parts, sympathetic (for emergency) and parasympathetic (for moderation). They control the functioning of various internal body parts.
The components of central nervous system is protected by cranium (brain) and by vertebral column (spinal cord).

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Name the various plant harmones. Also give their physiological effects on plant growth and development.
rows.answer:
Five types of plant hormones are - auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene and abscisic acid•
Auxin - Cell enlargement, root formation, apical dominance, inhibition of abscission, fruit growth.
Gibberellin - Growth in stem and leaves, higher fruit yield, overcoming dormancy.
Cytokinin - Essential for cell division, differentiation, prevention of senescene and overcoming apical dominance.
Ethylene - Promotes transverse growth, fruit ripening and overcoming dormancy of some parts.
Abscisic Acid (ABA) - Induces dormancy, senescence and abscission, checking excessive activity of growth promoting hormones, closure of stomata under water stress.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q6
rows.question: What are the major parts of the brain? Mention the function of different parts.
rows.answer:
Brain is the most important coordinating centre in the body. It has three major parts or regions namely the forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain.
| Parts | Functions |
| :--- | :--- |
| Forebrain | |
| Cerebrum or Cerebral hemispheres Olfactory lobes Diencephalon | Main thinking part of the brain. Intelligence and voluntary actions. Centre of smell Has centre of hunger, thirstm etc. |
| Midbrain | |
| Optic lobe | Controls reflex movements of the neck, head and trunk in response to visual and auditory stimuli. Also controls the reflex movements of the eye muscles, changes in pupil size and shape of the eye lens. |
| Hindbrain | |
| Cerebellum | Regulates respiration, Relays information between the cerebellum and the cerebrum. Maintains posture and balance of the body. Enables us to make precise and accurate movements. |
| (a) Thyroxine | Regulates metabolism of |
carbohydrates, fats and proteins, release energy for body activity.
(b) Insulin - Regulates blood glucose by its absorption in liver (for formation of glycogen) and individual cells (for metabolic activiry).
(c) Adrenaline - For meeting an emergency by increasing heart rate and supply of blood to various organs.
(d) Growth Hormone (GH) or Somatotrophic Hormone - Regulates growth and development of the body through anabolic activity for growth of bones, muscles and visceral organs.
(e) Testosterone (Male Sex Hormone) - Controls changes in body associated with puberty in males (beard moustache, low pitch voice, increased growth of bones and muscles)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q7
rows.question:

(a) Thyroxine
(b) Insulin
(c) Adrenaline
(d) Growth hormone
(e) Testosterone

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q1
rows.question:
The largest number of neurons are found in:
(a) Brain
(b) Retina
(c) Spinal cord
(d) Tongue
rows.answer: (a) In the human body there are 31 pairs of spinal nerves, one on each side of the vertebral column. These are grouped into the corresponding cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral and coccygeal regions of the spine.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q2

(a) Spinal cord
(b) Cerebellum

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q3
rows.question:
The nerves leading to the central nervous system are called:
(a) Efferent
(b) Afferent
(c) Motor
(d) Relay
rows.answer: (a) The label X represents Node of Ranvier. It speeds up the impulse transmission.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q4
rows.question:

(a) Insulin
(b) Thyroxin

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q5
rows.question:
Olfactory receptor organs are sensitive for:
(a) Light
(b) Sound
(c) Smell
(d) Equilibrium
rows.answer: (d) The medulla (a sub-region of the brainstem) is a major control centre for the autonomic nervous system. The hypothalamus acts to integrate autonomic functions and receives autonomic regulatory feedback from the limbic system to do so.

rows.question:
The nervous control of pituitary secretion lies in:
(a) Infundibulum
(b) Pituitary centre
(c) Hypothalamus
(d) Medulla oblongata
rows.answer:
(c) Bipolar neurons is a type of neuron which has two extensions - one axon and one dendrite. Bipolar cells are specialised sensory neurons for the transmission
of special senses. As such, they are part of the sensory pathways for smell, sight, taste, hearing and vestibular functions. They are found in the retina of eye.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q14
rows.question:
Neuron becomes an electrically charged cell by the diffusion of:
(a) K
(b) Na
(c) P
(d) Ca
rows.answer: (a) Sympathetic nervous system and parasympathetic nervous system are subdivisions of autonomic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system controls the involuntary functions of internal organs such as heart rate, digestion, breathing, urination, etc. The parasympathetic nervous system controls homoeostasis of the body and is responsible for "rest and digest" response. The sympathetic nervous system controls perceived threat responses thus is responsible for "fight or flight" responses. Thus parasympathetic nerves and sympathetic nerves are known to function in relaying information of involuntary functions of the body.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q15
rows.question:
Nerve cell do not divide because they do not have:
(a) Nucleus
(b) Centrioles
(c) Golgi body
(d) Mitochondria
rows.answer: (c) Auxin has been clearly demonstrated in the leaf sheath or coleoptile of oat plant (Avena sativa). This plant coleoptile has been used for the test of hormone auxin (IAA) participating in the growth of the plant.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q16
rows.question:
Artificial ripening of fruits is carried out by
(a) Auxin
(b) Gibberellin
(c) Abscisic acid
(d) Ethylene
rows.answer: (b) Auxins helps to prevent premature leaf and fruit fall.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q17
rows.question:
Name the hormone that lowers the blood sugar level?
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Oestrogen
(c) Glucagon
(d) Insulin
rows.answer: (b) Corpus luteum is the temporary endocrine gland formed in the ovary after ovulation. It releases hormones like progesterone, oestrogen etc. for the maintenance of pregnancy.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q18
rows.question:
Chemicals which are released at the synaptic junction are called
(a) cerebrospinal fluid
(b) hormones
(c) neurotransmitters
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>y</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></math>
rows.answer: (c) Pre-synaptic membrane is involved in the release of neurotransmitter in the chemical synapse. The receptors sites for neurotransmitters are present on post-synaptic membrane of neuron.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q19

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q20

The pineal body is considered as
(a) an endocrine gland.
(b) an organ concerned with voluntary actions.
(c) an organ concerned with vision.
(d) a vestige of third eye and endocrine gland.
rows.answer: (d) The cerebrum is the largest part of the human brain, making up about two-thirds of the brain's mass.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q21
rows.question:
In reflex action, the reflex arc is formed by
(a) brain → spinal cord → muscles
(b) receptor → spinal cord → muscles
(c) muscle → receptor → brain
(d) muscles → spinal cord → receptor
rows.answer: (a) An example of nastic movement in plants is folding up of the leaves of Mimosa pudica. Nastic movements in plants are reversible and repeatable movements in response to a stimulus whose direction is determined by the anatomy of the plant. The leaves of the Mimosa pudica fold up when touched and returns to full leaf in a few minutes. The leaves of the Mimosa achieve this rapid folding by a change in turgor pressure.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q22
rows.question:
Which of the following receptors is incorrectly paired with what it senses?
(a) Chemoreceptors - chemicals
(b) Photoreceptors - pain
(c) Thermoreceptors - heat
(d) Nociceptors - pain
rows.answer: (b) The limbic system (emotional motor system) is responsible for the experience and expression of emotion but not movement. It is located in the core of the brain and includes the amygdala, hippocampus and hypothalamus.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q23
rows.question:
The main effect of cytokinin in plants is to
(a) improve the quality of fruits
(b) prevent the growth of lateral buds
(c) regulate opening and closing of stomata
(d) stimulate cell division
rows.answer:
(a) A pineapple plant flowers only once, and produces one pineapple. Then it dies. But before it dies it also produces offspring.
Auxin and ethylene can be applied to artificially induce flowering in pineapple to increase yield throughout the year. Applied auxin mimic the action of ethylene by stimulating ethylene formation, and that ethylene, not auxin, causes pineapples to flower. Ethylene initiates flowering and synchronising fruit-set in pineapples.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q24
rows.question:
Which of the following protects the brain from shocks?
(a) Durameter
(b) Cerebrospinal fluid
(c) Arachnoid membrane
(d) Cranium
rows.answer:
(c) Insulin is a pancreatic hormone, secreted from beta cells of islets of Langerhans. Its deficiency leads to diabetes mellitus (characterised by high blood sugar levels over a prolonged period).
Thyroxine is an iodine containing hormone, secreted by the thyroid gland. It plays an important role in regulating the body's metabolic rate, heart and digestive function, muscle control, brain development and bone maintenance. Hyper secretion or hyposecretion of thyroxine can be associated with enlargement of thyroid gland called goitre.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q25
rows.question:
Name the hormone which controls the basal metabolic rate in animals.
(a) Adrenaline
(b) Thyroxine
(c) Aldosterone
(d) Oxytocin
rows.answer: (b) Hypothalamus (a region of the forebrain) is the thermoregulatory centre of brain. It coordinates both the autonomic nervous system and the activity of the pituitary, controlling body temperature, thirst, hunger, and other homeostatic systems, and involved in sleep and emotional activity.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q26
rows.question:
Which of the following comments applies to the brains of most animals?
(a) Within the brain, neurons exchange information with one another.
(b) Brains usually lie as near as possible to the important sensory structures in an animal.
(c) Brains send action potentials to the hindmost portion of the animal by means of major nerves.
(d) All of the above
rows.answer: (a) Neurotransmitters are body's chemical messengers which are used by the nervous system to transmit messages between neurons or from neurons to muscles. These molecules degrade rapidly and can travel only short distances. This makes them perfect for crossing the short synapses between neurons.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q27

Phytohormones are:
(a) hormones regulating growth from seed to adulthood.
(b) growth regulators synthesised by plants and influencing physiological processes.
(c) hormones regulating flowering.
(d) hormones regulating secondary growth.

Cytokinins are known to:
(a) inhibit cytoplasmic movement.
(b) help in retention of chlorophyll.
(c) influence water movement.
(d) promote abscission layer formation.
rows.answer: (d) The curvature induced in plant organs in response to the unidirectional light is called phototropism.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q29
rows.question:
The stimulus for geotropism in roots is perceived by:
(a) Root cap
(b) Quiescent centre
(c) Root tip
(d) None of these
rows.answer: (b) Cytokinin promotes bud and shoot formation and increased cell division; auxin promotes root formation and increased cell elongation.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q30
rows.question:
Thymus in mammals is mainly concerned with:
(a) Regulation of body growth
(b) Secretion of thyrotropin
(c) Regulation of body temperature
(d) Immunological functions
rows.answer: (c) Auxin stimulates cell elongation. It accumulates on the shaded side which results in more elongation of cells towards shaded side of the plant. This causes bending of the plant towards source of light.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q31
rows.question:
The primary function of neurotransmitters is:
(a) rapid relaying of nerve signals.
(b) graded control of development.
(c) regulation of cyclical processes.
(d) communication within a nerve cell.
rows.answer: (a) Aldosterone is produced by adrenal cortex and plays an important role in the regulation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>+</mo></mrow></math> levels in the body.

(a) Enzymes
(b) Starch
(c) Gibberellin
(d) Auxin
rows.answer: (d) Abscisic acid inhibits the cell growth and promotes dormancy in seeds. Auxin initiates division of the vascular cambium.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q35
rows.question:
Which of the following events would likely occur if a plant tissue culture is treated with a solution containing a relatively high concentration of cytokinin and a relatively low concentration of auxin?
(a) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
(b) Bud and shoot formation; slow cell expansion; increased cell division.
(c) Bud and shoot formation; rapid cell expansion; increased cell division.
(d) Root formation; rapid cell expansion; decreased cell division.
rows.answer: (c) The nerve has nerve endings at the end and dendrites at front. The nerve endings send signals through synapse and get transported to other neurons dendrites. In this way, the signals get transmitted.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q36
rows.question:
What causes a green plant exposed to the light on only one side, to bend toward the source of light as it grows?
(a) Green plants seek light because they are phototropic.
(b) Light stimulates plant cells on the lighted side to grow faster.
(c) Auxin accumulates on the shaded side, stimulating greater cell elongation there.
(d) Green plants need light to perform photosynthesis.
rows.answer: (d) The pathway involved in reflex action from stimulus to response is called reflex arc. The stimulus is received by a receptor and passed on to the sensory neuron and then to relay neuron. The impulse is transmitted to motor neuron which carries it to the effector organ.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs): — Q37
rows.question:
Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
(a) Aldosterone
(b) Insulin
(c) Glucagon
(d) Cortisone
rows.answer: (d) Auxins are synthesized in the growing tips of stems and roots. When light is coming from one side of the plant, auxin flow towards the shady side. The increased concentration of auxin allows the cells to grow longer on the shaded side. Hence, statements (B) and (D) are related to the picture.

rows.question:
The changes that are likely to happen in the plant in the following picture:

(C) Abscisic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> helps the cells to grow longer.

rows.answer:

(C) Abscisic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> helps the cells to grow longer.

rows.title: Check Your Knowledge
rows.question:
Check Your Knowledge
1. Why is regeneration not always same as reproduction?
2. Why can't all multi-cellular organisms reproduce by fragmentation?
3. What is the benefit of reproduction via spore formation?
rows.answer:
1. At times, regeneration is also required to restore a lost part of an individual's body like autotomy in lizards, wound healing, etc. In such cases, organism doesn't actually reproduce. Hence regeneration is not same as reproduction.
2. Multi-cellular organisms have a very complex form of organization (like tissues and organs) which can't be regained by the process of fragmentation.
3. Spores are covered by a hard protective coat of sporopollenin and can survive under unfavourable conditions. Moreover they are small sized; light in weight and can be dispersed easily.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q1
rows.question:
Which of the following is the correct sequence of events of sexual reproduction in a flower?
(a) Pollination, fertilisation, seedling, embryo
(b) Seedling, embryo, fertilisation, pollination
(c) Pollination, fertilisation, embryo, seedling
(d) Embryo, seedling, pollination, fertilisation
rows.answer: (c) In pollination, pollen grains transfer from stamen to stigma after which fertilization takes place during which germ cells fuse together to form zygote which in turn leads to embryo formation. Fertilized ovule becomes seed and seeds germinate to produce seedling.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q2
rows.question:
The number of chromosomes in parents and offsprings of a particular species remains constant due to
(a) doubling of chromosomes after zygote formation.
(b) halving of chromosomes during gamete formation.
(c) doubling of chromosomes after gamete formation.
(d) halving of chromosomes after gamete formation.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q3
rows.question:
Vegetative propagation refers to formation of new plants from
(a) stem, roots and flowers.
(b) stem, roots and leaves.
(c) stem, flowers and fruits.
(d) stem, leaves and flowers.
rows.answer: (d) Multiple fission is repeated division of organism to produce many daughter cells simultaneously.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q4
rows.question:
Length of pollen tube depends on the distance between
(a) pollen grain and upper surface of stigma.
(b) pollen grain on upper surface of stigma and ovule.
(c) pollen grain in anther and upper surface of stigma.
(d) upper surface of stigma and lower part of style.
rows.answer: (b) Meiosis reduces the chromosome number to half which is then restored by fertilisation.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q5
rows.question:
Offspring formed as a result of sexual reproduction exhibit more variations because
(a) sexual reproduction is a lengthy process.
(b) genetic material comes from two parents of the same species.
(c) genetic material comes from two parents of different species.
(d) genetic material comes from many parents.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: — Exemplar Questions: — Q6
rows.question:
During adolescence, several changes occur in the human body. Mark one change associated with sexual maturation in boys
(a) loss of milk teeth
(b) increase in height
