rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Name the scientist who proposed the theory of natural selection.
rows.answer: Charles Darwin

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q2
rows.question: What is meant by microevolutuion ?
rows.answer: Microevolution is the change in allele frequency that can be observed within a population.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q3
rows.question: What is molecular phylogeny ?
rows.answer: Molecular phylogeny is the study of evolutionary relationships by comparing DNA of different species.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Who gave the concept of evolution.
rows.answer: Charles Darwin, a British naturalist gave the concept of evolution.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q5
rows.question: Name the two purines.
rows.answer: Two purines are adenine and guanine.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Who provided the evidence of DNA as a genetic material?
rows.answer: In 1902, T. Boveri and W.S. Sutton provided the evidence of DNA as genetic material.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q7
rows.question: Give an example of a vestigial organ present in human body.
rows.answer: Vermiform Appendix

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q8
rows.question: What is a gene?
rows.answer: Gene is the unit of inheritance. It is therefore, a unit of specific biological function. Gene is the part of a chromosome which controls the appearance of a set of hereditary characteristics.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q9
rows.question: Define inheritance.
rows.answer: Inheritance is the transmission of particular characteristics from generation to generation by means of the genetic code, which is transferred to offspring in the gametes.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q10
rows.question: Where are genes located ? What is the chemical nature of gene?
rows.answer: Genes are located on the chromosomes at the fixed positions. Genes are composed of DNA which is having a large number of polynucleotides.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q11
rows.question: Which organ of a man is homologous to the limbs supporting wings of birds ?
rows.answer: Forelimbs (arms)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q12
rows.question: Give the three key factors of the modern concept of evolution.
rows.answer: The main three key factors of modern concept of evolution are genetic variation, natural selection and isolation.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q13
rows.question: Mendel proposed result of segregation on the basis of which cross?
rows.answer: Monohybrid cross

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q14
rows.question: Write two reasons of Mendel's success.
rows.answer:
(i) Mendel concentrated on the results of one trait at a time.
(ii) Mendel selected pea plant, in which contrasting pair of characters are found

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q15
rows.question: In pea plants, yellow flowers (Y) are dominant to white (y). Predict the ratio of genotypes and produced by a cross between a homozygous yellow-flowered pea plant and a heterozygous yellow-flowered pea plant.
rows.answer: The genotypic ratio will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>Y</mi><mi>Y</mi><mi>:</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow></mrow></math>

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q16
rows.question: How do traits get expressed?
rows.answer: Traits get expressed through cellular DNA, which is the information source for making proteins in the cell that in turn control the traits and its metabolism.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q17
rows.question: What form the basis for evolutionary process ?
rows.answer: Selection of variants by environmental factors forms the basis for evolutionary process.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q18
rows.question: Which sex chromosome is found only in male human beings?
rows.answer: Y chromosome.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q19
rows.question: What is the biological name of human beings ?
rows.answer: Homo sapiens.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q20
rows.question:
What is speciation ?
[2015]
rows.answer: The evolution of reproductive isolation among once interbreeding populations is speciation

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q21
rows.question:
How many pairs of chromosomes are present in human beings?
[2015]
rows.answer: 23 pairs.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Very Short Answer Questions: — Q22
rows.question:
A Mendelian experiment consisited of breeding pea plants bearing violet flowers with pea plants bearing white flowers. What will be the result in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny?
[2018]
rows.answer: In the first place, there were no halfway characteristics in this first generation, or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny - no 'white' flower in plants. All plants were violet because it is dominant. This meant that only one of the parental traits was seen, not mixture of the two.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q1
rows.question: Differentiate the followings : Homozygous and Heterozygous

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q2
rows.question: Explain the statement - "Dissimilar looking structures evolve from a common ancestral design".
rows.answer: (b) Independent assortment of genes takes place only when they are located on separate non-homologous chromosomes. When two or more than two genes are located on the same chromosome, independent assortment will not be possible, if they are separated by large distance.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q3
rows.question: How can we determine the age of the fossil?

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q4
rows.question: Give the basic features of the mechanism of inheritance.
rows.answer: (b) The law of segregation of alleles is postulated by Mendel, can be related to a gamete, receiving only one of the two homologous chromosomes during meiosis.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q5
rows.question: What is DNA copying ? State its importance. [2015]

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q6
rows.question:
In one of his experiments with pea plants, Mendel observed that when a pure tall pea plant is crossed with a pure dwarf pea plant. In the first generation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> only tall plants appear.
(i) What happens to the traits of the dwarf plants in this case?
(ii) When the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation plants were self-fertilised, he observed that in the plants of second generation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> both tall plants and dwarf plants were present. Why it happened? Explain briefly.
[2016]
rows.answer:
(d) Properties of genetic material:
(i) The replicated genetic material must be transferred faithfully from cells to its daughter cells or from one generation to other.
(ii) Genetic material should be able to express itself.
(iii) There are occasional changes or mutation in the structure and functioning of its genes, which are of permanent nature and inheritable.
(iv) It should be stable.
(v) It should be same in both quantity and quality in all cells of an individual.
All the property mentioned above are fulfilled by DNA, so DNA is the genetic material.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q7
rows.question:
In a monohybrid cross between tall pea plants (TT) and short pea plants ( tt ) a scientist obtained only tall pea plants ( Tt ) in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation. However, on selfing the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation pea plants, he obtained both tall and short plants in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation. On the basis of above observations with other angiosperms also, can the scientist arrive at a law ? if yes, explain the law. If not, give justification for your answer.
[2016]
rows.answer: (a) The fundamental factor that makes one individual different from another is the specific sequence of DNA bases in our genes.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q8
rows.question:
"Evolution and classification of organisms are interlinked." Give reasons to justify this statement.
[2017]
rows.answer: (a) According to Watson and Crick model of DNA, it is a double helical molecule with 10 base pairs and 3.4 nm distance for every turn.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q9
rows.question: Name the plant Mendel used for his experiment. What type of progeny was obtained by Mendel in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generations when he crossed the tall and short plants? Write the ratio he obtained in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation plants.
rows.answer: (d) Speciation through reproductive isolation is one of the important consequences of geographical isolation. Geographical isolation is a group of plants, animals or other living individuals being separated from mixing genes within their same species. Geographical isolation may ultimately lead to the populations becoming separate species by adaptive radiation.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q10
rows.question:
(a) Classify the following as homologous or analogous pairs:
(i) Broccoli and Cabbage
(ii) Ginger and Raddish
(iii) Fore limbs of birds and lizard
(iv) Wings of a bat and Wings of a bird
(b) State the main feature that categories a given pair of organs as homologous or analogous.
[2020]
rows.answer: (b) Finding long-lasting and effective treatments against disease organisms is made more difficult because these organisms are constantly adapting to defeat our treatments. Likewise, the process of domestication involves intense selective breeding for favourable traits that meet the needs of humans, thus leading to new adaptations for plants and animals.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in 2-3 sentences. — Q11
rows.question:
A green stemmed rose plant denoted by GG and a brown stemmed rose plant denoted by gg are allowed to undergo a cross with each other.
(a) List your observations regarding
(i) Colour of stem in their <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny
(ii) Percentage of brown stemmed plants in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants are self pollinated.
(iii) Ratio of GG and Gg in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progency.
(b) Based on the findings of this cross, what conclusion can be drawn?
[2020]
rows.answer: (c) According to Lamarckism (or Lamarckian inheritance), an organism can pass on the characteristics that it acquired during its lifetime. According to Lamarck, a giraffe has a long neck because its ancestors stretched their necks to get the food.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences. — Q1
rows.question: Define 'evolution'. Describe Darwin's theory of evolution.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences. — Q2
rows.question: What causes Evolution ?
rows.answer: (b) Independent assortment of genes takes place only when they are located on separate non-homologous chromosomes. When two or more than two genes are located on the same chromosome, independent assortment will not be possible, if they are separated by large distance.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences. — Q3
rows.question: Explain the dihybrid cross as performed by Mendel.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences. — Q4
rows.question: What is meant by speciation ? List four factors that could lead to speciation. Which of these cannot be a major factor in the speciation of a self-pollinating plant species? Give reason to justify your answer.
rows.answer: (b) The law of segregation of alleles is postulated by Mendel, can be related to a gamete, receiving only one of the two homologous chromosomes during meiosis.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — DIRECTIONS : Give answer in four to five sentences. — Q5
rows.question:
How do Mendel's experiments show that.
(i) traits may be dominant or recessive?
(ii) inheritance of two traits is independent of each other?
[2017]

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why are the traits acquired during the lifetime of an individual not inherited?
rows.answer: The traits acquired during the life-time of an individual not inherited because certain changes occur in the structure or function of the body parts due to environmental factors which cannot be passed on to the DNA of the germ cells. The traits are only inherited if changes take place in the genetic material. Therefore, the experiences of an individual during its lifetime cannot be passed on to its progeny, and cannot direct evolution.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q2
rows.question: Why are human beings who look so different from each other in terms of size, colour and looks said to belong to the same species?
rows.answer: A species is a group of organisms that are capable of interbreeding to produce a fertile offspring. Skin colour, looks, and size are all variety of features present in human beings. These features are genetic but also environmentally controlled. Various human races are formed based on these features. All human races have more than enough similarities to be classified as same species. Therefore, all human beings are a single species as humans of different colour, size, and looks are capable of reproduction and can produce a fertile offspring.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q3
rows.question: Name one organ analogous to the wing of bird. Why are they both analogous ? Can you include the wing of bat also with them under the same category ? Give reason.
rows.answer:
The wings of insects is analogous to the wing of bird. They both are analogous because they perform the same function, but are not similar in structural plan and developmental origin.
Yes, the wing of bat is also analogous to the wing of bird. Because the wing of bat and that of bird are built on the same plan, i.e., same in structure and origin.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q4
rows.question: Give reasons for the appearance of new combination of characters in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny.
rows.answer:
The reason for the appearance of new combinations in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation is given by the law of independent assortment. The law of independent assortment states that,
(i) When two pairs of traits are combined in a hybrid, one pair of character segregates independent of the other pair of character.
(ii) In a dihybrid cross between two plants having round yellow (RRYY) and wrinkled green seeds (rryy), four types of gametes (RY, Ry, rY, ry) are produced. Each of these segregate independent of each other, each having a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the total gametes produced.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q5
rows.question:
A black mouse mates with a brown mouse, and all the offsprings are black.
(a) Why are no brown offsprings produced?
(b) If two of the black offsprings mate with each other, what kind of offspring would you expect and in what proportions? Give reason for your answer.
rows.answer:
(a) No brown offsprings were produced because black colour genes are dominant over brown colour genes.
(b) Three black mice and one brown mouse will be obtained in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation : It is a monohybrid cross.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q6
rows.question: Pure-breed pea plants A are crossed with pure-breed pea plants B . It is found that the plants which look like A do not appear in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation but re-emerge in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation. Which of the plants A and B ar: (i) tall and (ii) dwarf? Give reason for your answer.
rows.answer: (i) B (ii) A : Dwarf pea plants do not appear in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Reasoning Based Questions: — Q7
rows.question: "Only variations that confer an advantage to an individual organism will survive in a population". Do you agree with this statement? Give reason for your answer.
rows.answer: Variations that confer an advantage to an individual organism make it fit to the changes in the environment. The organism will be naturally selected and will be continued in the population.But sometimes accidental killing of organisms with one kind of trait leads to survival of individuals with other kind of traits. This genetic drift produces variations without any adaptations.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — HOTS Questions: — Q1
rows.question: Why is recessive trait found in fewer individuals?
rows.answer:
(i) (b), (ii) (d), (iii) (c), (iv) (b), (v) (a)
Exercise 2
Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — HOTS Questions: — Q2
rows.question: How does creation of variations in a species promote survival?
rows.answer: A number of different types of variations develop in a population. All of them do not have survival value. However, some of them are pre-adaptations which can be beneficial under certain environmental conditions. For example, in a heat wave most of the bacteria will die but a few having pre-adaptation or variation to tolerate heat wave will survive and multiply. Actually selection of variants by different environmental factors constitutes the basis for evolution.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — HOTS Questions: — Q3
rows.question:
A plant has two varieties, one with red petals and the other with white petals. When these two varieties are cross-pollinated, all the offsprings have red petals?
(a) Which gene is dominant?
(b) Choose suitable letters to represent the two genes.
rows.answer:
(a) The gene for red petals is dominant, while the gene for white is recessive.
(b) The gene for red petals can be represented as R and the gene for white petals can be represented as r .

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — HOTS Questions: — Q4
rows.question:
(a) E is the gene for brown eye colour and e is the gene for blue eye colour. Which gene is (i) recessive, and (ii) dominant?
(b) Both father and mother have the genes Ee in their cells. What colour are their eyes?
(c) Which combination of genes in the zygote will produce children with blue eyes?
(d) Which combinations of genes in the zygote will produce children with brown eyes?
rows.answer:
(a) (i) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></math> is recessive; (ii) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow></math> is dominant
(b) The colour of their eyes is brown.
(c) Combination ee will produce children with blue eyes.
(d) Combination <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>E</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></mrow></math> will produce children with brown eyes.
> Case Study Based MCQs
> (CBSE Sample Issued 2021)
> Sex Determination

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q1
rows.question: If a trait A exists in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of a population of an asexually reproducting species and a trait B exists in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the same population, which trait is likely to have arisen earlier?
rows.answer:
In asexual reproduction, cell division results in the creation of similar cells with the identical copies of DNA. Therefore, in such species, a trait may come into existence due to random mutation induced by various environmental factors. It can be observed that the induced trait occurs in few species which is then inherited by future generations, on replication over a period of time.
Thus, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of a population contains this trait, it means that there members have been replicating themselves for a longer period of time than those in the population where the trait is seen in only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the population. Thus, trait B is likely to have arisen earlier.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q2
rows.question: How does the creation of variations in a species promote survival?
rows.answer: A variation is the deviation in characters of an individual from the group to which it belongs or deviation in characters of the offspring from those of the parents. The survival of the variation depends upon its nature. Depending on the nature of variations, different individuals would have different kinds of advantage. These variations may lead to the advantages of the individuals due to positive adaptation of the trait. This is how the creation of variations in a species promotes survival of the species.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q3
rows.question: How do Mendel's experiments show that traits may be dominant or recessive?
rows.answer:
Mendel conducted a monohybrid cross from tall (TT) pea plant with dwarf (tt) pea plant as shown in the figure. He obtained tall pea plants in first generation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow></mrow></math> and when first generation was self crossed, both tall and dwarf plants were obtained in a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math> i.e., 3 tall plants and 1 dwarf plant.
Fig.: Mendel experiments depicting monohybrid cross
Thus, as three-fourths of the plants in second <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow></mrow></math> generation were tall and one-fourth was dwarf. So he concluded that, tall trait was dominant while dwarf trait was recessive.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q4
rows.question: A man with blood group A marries a woman with blood group O and their daughter has blood group O . Is this information enough to tell you which of the traits - blood group A or O - is dominant? Why or why not?
rows.answer: No, the given information is not sufficient to tell that which trait - blood group i.e., A or O is dominant. This is because we cannot predict that whether father or mother is homozygous or heterozygous. According to Mendel, in blood heredity, blood group A is always dominant and blood group O is always recessive. Here, father's blood group can be AA (homozygous) or AO (heterozygous) genotypically, whereas mother can be OA or OO . For daughter to be born with blood group O, she must receive O type gene one each from father as well from mother, for this father must have heterozygous AO blood group and mother must have homozygous blood group OO.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q5
rows.question: How is the sex of the child determined in human beings ?
rows.answer:
In human beings, out of 23-pairs of chromosomes 22-pairs of chromosomes are called autosomes and only one pair is called sex-chromosome. These sex-chromosomes are also of two types i.e. ' X ' and ' Y ' sex-chromosomes.
The sex of a child depends on what happens at fertilisation like:
(i) If a sperm carrying X chromosome fertilises an ovum (or egg), which carries X chromosome, then the child will be a girl (or female). This is because the child will be having XX combination of sex chromosome.
(ii) If a sperm carrying Y chromosome fertilises an ovum (or egg), which carries X chromosome, then the child will be a boy (or male). This is because the child will be having XY combination of sex chromosome.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q6
rows.question: What are the different ways in which individuals with a particular trait may increase in a population?
rows.answer:
There are two ways in which individuals with a particular trait may increase in a population. These are-
(i) Natural selection- Organism with a particular trait selected naturally because it increases the survival rate of the given species, which leads to the increase in a population.
(ii) Genetic drift- An accident in small population may result in surviving of the organism with a particular variant only. This may increase the frequency of some gene in that population which have no survival advantage.
(iii) Poor nourishment may reduce the population of individuals but when nourishment conditions improve the population starts increasing again.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q7
rows.question: Why are the small numbers of surviving tigers a cause of worry is from the point of view of genetics?
rows.answer:
When a population is small, the scope and number of variations is limited which leads to the reduction in their diversity and traits. The small numbers of surviving tigers a cause of worry from the point of view of genetics
because it would cause loss in genetic variability. Small number of tigers means fewer chances of variations which is necessary for the maintaining the stability of a species by natural selection. It can also cause sudden extinction of the tigers and loss of their genes forever, adversely affecting the diversity of nature.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q8
rows.question: Will geographical isolation be a major factor in the speciation of an organism that reproduces asexually? Why or why not?
rows.answer: No, geographical isolation will not be a major factor in the speciation of an organism that reproduces asexually because asexual reproduction involve only single parent. Variations can occur only when the copying of DNA is not accurate. Therefore, geographical isolation cannot prevent the formation of new species in an asexually reproducing organism.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q9
rows.question: Give an example of characteristics being used to determine how close two species are in evolutionary terms.
rows.answer: The more characteristics two species will have in common, the more closely they are related. And the more closely they are related, the more recently they will have had a common ancestor. Forelimbs of humans and wings of birds are closely related in an evolutionary terms. Since, the forelimb of human and wings of birds have similar structure but they both performs different functions. Thus, presence of homologous organs provides evidence for evolution that they have evolved from the same ancestor.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q10
rows.question: Can the wing of a butterfly and the wing of a bat be considered homologous organs? Why of why not?
rows.answer: The wings of butterfly or bat are similar function but dissimilar in the origin. Such organs are called analogous organs. So they are not homologous organs.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Questions: — Q11
rows.question: Why are human beings who look so different from each other in terms of size, colour and looks said to belong to the same species?
rows.answer: Human beings look very different from each other due to the change in phenotypic characters. Their genotypic characters are same so their organs are same but due to change in geographical conditions they become different in size, colour and look to the progeny of some species.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q1
rows.question:
A Mendelian experiment consisted of breeding tall pea plants bearing violet flowers with short pea plants bearing white flowers. The progeny all bore violet flowers, but almost half of them were short. This suggests that the genetic make-up of the tall parent can be depicted as
(a) TTWW
(b) TTww
(c) TtWW
(d) TtWw
rows.answer: (c) Since, all the progeny bore violet flowers, it implies that the tall plant with violet flowers has genotype 'WW' for violet flower colour. Since, the progeny obtained is both tall and short, the parent plant was not a pure tall plant and bears genes that determine short height of the plant. Therefore, the genotype of the plant with respect to height would be ' Tt '. So, if a cross is carried out between tall parent with violet flowers (TtWW) and short parent with white flowers (ttww), the progeny obtained is TtWw (8) : ttWw (4) : ttWw(4). All the progeny bear violet flowers but half of them are tall and half are short.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q2
rows.question:
An example of homologous organs is
(a) our teeth and elepahant's tusks
(b) our arm and a dog's fore-leg
(c) potato and runners of grass.
(d) all of the above.
rows.answer: (d) Homologous organ have same origin but different functions like each of the organ above. Our teeth and elephant's tusks, our arm and a dog's fore-leg and potato and runners of grass are the examples of homologous organs.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q3
rows.question: A study found that children with light-coloured eyes are likely to have parents with light-coloured eyes. On this basis, can we say anything about whether the light eye colour trait is dominant or recessive? Why or Why not?
rows.answer: No, since two copies of traits are inherited from parents, one from mother and the other from father. Unless we know the nature of these two variants of traits we cannot tell which is dominant and which is recessive. Recessive traits appear when both the parents contribute recessive allele. From this statement we can only presume that both parents are contributing recessive allele.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q4
rows.question: How are the areas of study - evolution and classification - interlinked?
rows.answer: Every species or organisms has inbuilt tendency for genetic variation which plays an important role in the origin of new species and forms the basis for evolution. The more characteristics two species have in common, the more closely they are related. Classification of organisms necessarily involves, organising them in different groups, based on the similarities and differences of characteristics. Classifying organisms helps us in recognising the basic arrangement of a hierarchical structure among diverse species. In fact, classification of species is a reflection of their evolutionary relationship. Thus, we can say evolution and classification are related to each other.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q5
rows.question: Explain the terms analogous and homologous organs with examples.
rows.answer:
Homologous organs: The organs of similar structure and origin but different in function are known as homologous organs. e.g. Fore limbs of frog, wings of birds, wings of bat, flappers of whale, forelimbs of horse and fore limbs of man are similar in their origin but different in their functions.
Analogous organs: Organs similar in functions but different in structure and origin are known as analogous organs.
Example: Wings of butterfly and that of bird have similar functions but their origins are different. So they are called as analogous organs.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q6
rows.question: Outline a project which aims to find the dominant coat colour in dogs.
rows.answer:
Dogs have a variety of genes that govern coat colour. Let us select black and white colour for the study of inheritance of coat colour in dogs by performing the following crosses:
(i) Cross the pure black bred male dog with pure bred white female bitch.
(ii) Cross the pure white bred male dog with pure bred black bitch.
Observe the coat colour of the progeny produced.
If progeny is all black then black colour may be dominant and if progeny is all white then white may be dominant.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q7
rows.question: What evidence do we have for the origin of life from inanimate matter?
rows.answer: Evidence for the origin of life from the inanimate matter was provided by the Miller and Urey experiment in which they created an atmosphere similar to the early earth atmosphere where free oxygen is absent. They mixed different gases like ammonia, methane, hydrogen and water vapour and produce a spark in them. At the end of a week, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>15</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math> of the carbon (from methane) had been converted to simple compounds of carbon including amino acids which make up protein molecules found in living organisms. This experiment provides the evidence that the life originated from inanimate matter (lifeless matter) like inorganic molecules.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q8
rows.question: Explain how sexual reproduction gives rise to more viable variations than asexual reproduction. How does this affect the evolution of those organisms that reproduce sexually?
rows.answer: Since sexual reproduction involves two parents, large number of variations is produced. These two parents will already have variations accumulated from the previous generations and hence each generation formed would be novel since it has accumulated variations from two parents. Whereas in asexual reproduction, the organisms have very little variations due to errors in DNA copying while cell division process occurs. The inbuilt tendency for variations in organisms is the basis for evolution. Since sexually reproducing organisms have more variations, they speed up the process of evolution. While the less number of variations mean, asexually reproducing organisms will take long years for evolving into a new species. This is because the generation of variation plays a very important role in evolution process.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions: Passage Based Questions: \section*{Passage} — Text - Book Exercise: — Q9
rows.question: Only variations that confer an advantage to an individual organism will survive in a population. Do you agree with this statement? Why or Why not?
rows.answer: This statement is mostly true, but not always because those variations that confer an advantage to an organism will definitely survive in a population because natural selection prefers these variations, e.g., the accumulation of heat resistant variation in some bacteria will ensure its survival even when the temperature in its environment rises too much due to a heat wave. But there are several other variations which though do not provide an advantage to the organism in present condition and are inherited to the next generations. Such nonadvantageous variations may become advantageous in future when environmental conditions changes.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q1
rows.question:
Mendel formulated some laws which are known as
(a) Laws of germplasm
(b) Laws of origin of species
(c) Laws of recapitulation
(d) Laws of inheritance

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q2
rows.question:
Vestigial organs are:
(a) Primitive organs
(b) Primordial organs
(c) Organs reduced due to disuse
(d) Organs marked only in embryonic stage
rows.answer: (b) Independent assortment of genes takes place only when they are located on separate non-homologous chromosomes. When two or more than two genes are located on the same chromosome, independent assortment will not be possible, if they are separated by large distance.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q3
rows.question:
Who out of the following was of the strong opinion that acquired characteristics are inherited:
(a) Lamarck
(b) Lysenko
(c) Mendel
(d) Huxley

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q4
rows.question:
Guanine pairs with:
(a) Adenine
(b) Cytobine
(c) Thymine
(d) None of the above
rows.answer: (b) The law of segregation of alleles is postulated by Mendel, can be related to a gamete, receiving only one of the two homologous chromosomes during meiosis.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q5
rows.question:
Chemically a nucleotide has a:
(a) Pentose group
(b) Nitrogenous base
(c) Phosphate group
(d) All of these

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q6
rows.question:
Which of the following rediscovered the Mendel's work?
(a) Correns
(b) De Vries
(c) Tschermark
(d) All of these
rows.answer:
(d) Properties of genetic material:
(i) The replicated genetic material must be transferred faithfully from cells to its daughter cells or from one generation to other.
(ii) Genetic material should be able to express itself.
(iii) There are occasional changes or mutation in the structure and functioning of its genes, which are of permanent nature and inheritable.
(iv) It should be stable.
(v) It should be same in both quantity and quality in all cells of an individual.
All the property mentioned above are fulfilled by DNA, so DNA is the genetic material.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q7
rows.question:
Mendel's law of segregation is based on separation of alleles during:
(a) Gametes formation
(b) Seed formation
(c) Pollination
(d) Embryonic development
rows.answer: (a) The fundamental factor that makes one individual different from another is the specific sequence of DNA bases in our genes.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q8
rows.question:
Mendel formulated the law of purity of gametes on the basis of:
(a) Dihybrid cross
(b) Monohybrid cross
(c) Back cross
(d) Test cross
rows.answer: (a) According to Watson and Crick model of DNA, it is a double helical molecule with 10 base pairs and 3.4 nm distance for every turn.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q9
rows.question:
The earliest living organisms were:
(a) Multicellular
(b) Eukaryotes
(c) Prokaryotes
(d) None of these
rows.answer: (d) Speciation through reproductive isolation is one of the important consequences of geographical isolation. Geographical isolation is a group of plants, animals or other living individuals being separated from mixing genes within their same species. Geographical isolation may ultimately lead to the populations becoming separate species by adaptive radiation.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q10
rows.question:
Sudden inheritable change is called:
(a) Recombination
(b) Mutation
(c) National selection
(d) Segregation
rows.answer: (b) Finding long-lasting and effective treatments against disease organisms is made more difficult because these organisms are constantly adapting to defeat our treatments. Likewise, the process of domestication involves intense selective breeding for favourable traits that meet the needs of humans, thus leading to new adaptations for plants and animals.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q11
rows.question:
When one gene pair hides the effect of the other unit, this phenomenon is referred as -
(a) Dominance
(b) Mutation
(c) Epistasis
(d) None of these
rows.answer: (c) According to Lamarckism (or Lamarckian inheritance), an organism can pass on the characteristics that it acquired during its lifetime. According to Lamarck, a giraffe has a long neck because its ancestors stretched their necks to get the food.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q12
rows.question:
The genotype of offspring formed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mrow></math> will be -
(a) TT and tt
(b) Tt and tt
(c) only tt
(d) only TT
rows.answer: (d) According to Lamarck, during the lifetime of an organism, new characters are developed due to internal vital forces, effect of environment, new needs, use and disuse of organs. These acquired characters are inherited from one generation to another. According to him, intelligence should also be inherited and so it fails to explain dull progeny of nobel laureate.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q13
rows.question:
A complete set of chromosomes inherited as a unit from one parent, is known as:
(a) Karyotype
(b) Gene pool
(c) Genome
(d) Genotype
rows.answer: (d) Charles Darwin proposed the theory of natural selection.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q14
rows.question:
Which of the following is Heterozygous?
(a) TTRR
(b) ttrr
(c) TT
(d) Tt
rows.answer: (d) Wings of a bird and flippers of a whale are modified forelimbs. Thus, are the homologous structures.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q15
rows.question:
An experiment to prove that organic compounds were the basis of life, was performed by:
(a) Oparin
(b) Miller
(c) Melvin
(d) Fox
rows.answer: (c) Both statements are correct because primitive atmosphere was reducing and chlorophyll appeared later on. Chemoautotrophs were the first autotrophic organisms unable to perform photolysis of water and never released oxygen.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q16
rows.question:
The idea of "Survival of fittest" was given by:
(a) Darwin
(b) Herbert Spencer
(c) Germplasm RNA
(d) Somatic DNA
rows.answer: (c) When two genes in a dihybrid cross are situated on the same chromosome, the proportion of parental gene combinations is much higher than the nonparental or recombinant type. This is also called as incomplete linkage.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q17
rows.question:
If two parents have the genotypes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mrow></math>, the probability of having an aa genotype in the F , generation is -
(a) 25 percent
(b) 50 percent
(c) 75 percent
(d) None of these
rows.answer: (a) During his experiments, Mendel had taken seven characters in a pea plant in which, nature of trichomes i.e., glandular or non-glandular was not considered.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q18
rows.question:
Heredity deals with the study of
(a) resemblances and differences between the parents and offsprings.
(b) resemblances between the parents and offsprings.
(c) differences between the parents and offspring.
(d) none of the above

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q19
rows.question:
The given figure shows bones in the forelimbs of three mammals.
For these mammals, the number, position, and shape of the bones must likely indicates that they may have
(a) developed in a common environment.
(b) developed from the same earlier species.
(c) identical genetic makeup.
(d) identical methods of obtaining food.
rows.answer: (c) Divergent evolution demonstrates how species can have common (homologous) anatomical structures, whereas eye of octopus, bat and man are examples of analogous organs which have similar function showing convergent evolution.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q20
rows.question:
A male child will be born if
(a) father is healthy
(b) mother is well fed during pregnancy
(c) genetic composition of child has XY set of chromosomes
(d) genetic composition of child has XX set of chromosomes.
rows.answer:
(c) Woman acts as a carrier. Both son and daughter inherit X chromosome. Although only son would be the diseased one.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><msup><mi>X</mi><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup><mi>X</mi><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mspace linebreak="newline" /><mo>↓</mo><mspace linebreak="newline" /><msup><mi>X</mi><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup><mi>X</mi><msup><mi>X</mi><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup><mi>Y</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></math>

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q21
rows.question:
According to the evolutionary theory, formation of a new species is generally due to
(a) sudden creation by nature.
(b) accumulation of variations over several generations.
(c) clones formed during asexual reproduction.
(d) movement of individuals from one habitat to another.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q22
rows.question:
Which of the following is dominant character according to the Mendel?
(a) Dwarf plant and yellow fruit
(b) Terminal fruit and wrinkled seed
(c) White testa and yellow pericarp
(d) Green coloured pod and rounded seed
rows.answer: (b) Allopatric speciation, also referred to as geographic speciation or its earlier name, the dumbbell model, is a mode of speciation that occurs when biological populations of the same species become isolated from each other to an extent that prevents or interferes with gene flow.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q23
rows.question:
From heredity point of view, which marriage is not suitable?
(a) Man <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>−</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> and Woman <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>+</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>
(b) Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>+</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>
(c) Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>−</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>
(d) Man <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>+</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math> and Woman <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mo stretchy="false">(</mo><mo>−</mo><mo stretchy="false">)</mo></mrow></math>
rows.answer: (b) The correct option is (b) because herbicide resistant weeds, drug resistant eukaryotes and man-created breeds of domesticated animals like dogs are examples of evolution by anthropogenic action. Darwin's Finches of Galapagos islands are example of natural selection, adaptive radiation and founder's effect.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q24
rows.question:
Which of the following are fossils?
(a) Pollen grains buried in the bottom of a peat bog.
(b) The petrified cast of a clam's burrow.
(c) The impression of clam shell made in mud, preserved in mudstone.
(d) All of the above.
rows.answer: (d) The correct option is (d) because flippers of Penguins and Dolphins are an example of analogous organs. Analogous structures are a result of convergent evolution.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q25
rows.question:
Which of the following evolutionary mechanisms acts to slow down or prevent the evolution of reproductive isolation?
(a) Natural selection
(b) Gene flow
(c) Mutation
(d) Genetic drift
rows.answer: (d) Embryological support for evolution was disapproved by Karl Ernst von Baer, he noted that embryos never pass through the adult stages of other animals during embryonic development.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q26
rows.question:
In natural selection,
(a) the genetic composition of the population changes at random over time.
(b) new mutations are generated over time.
(c) all individuals in a population are equally likely to contribute offspring to the next generation.
(d) individuals that possess particular inherited characters survive and reproduce at a higher rate than other individuals.
rows.answer: (c) Experimental verification of the chromosomal theory of inheritance was done by Morgan. Sutton and Boveri proposed chromosomal theory of inheritance but it was experimentally verified by T.H. Morgan.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q27
rows.question:
A heterozygous red-eyed female Drosophila mated with a white-eyed male would produce
(a) red-eyed females and white-eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(b) white-eyed females and red-eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(c) half red and half white-eyed females and all white eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(d) half red and half white-eyed females as well as males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
rows.answer:
A heterozygous red-eyed female Drosophila mated with a white-eyed male would produce
(a) red-eyed females and white-eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(b) white-eyed females and red-eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(c) half red and half white-eyed females and all white eyed males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
(d) half red and half white-eyed females as well as males in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q28
rows.question:
Sex-linked disorders such as color blindness and haemophilia are
(a) caused by genes on the X chromosome
(b) caused by genes on the autosome
(c) caused by genes on the Y chromosome
(d) expressed only in men
rows.answer:
Sex-linked disorders such as color blindness and haemophilia are
(a) caused by genes on the X chromosome
(b) caused by genes on the autosome
(c) caused by genes on the Y chromosome
(d) expressed only in men

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q29
rows.question:
The smallest unit that can evolve is a:
(a) Species
(b) Genotype
(c) Gene
(d) Population
rows.answer:
The smallest unit that can evolve is a:
(a) Species
(b) Genotype
(c) Gene
(d) Population

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q30
rows.question:
Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic fossils are found in:
(a) Azoic
(b) Coenozic
(c) Proterozoic
(d) Archaeozoic
rows.answer:
Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic fossils are found in:
(a) Azoic
(b) Coenozic
(c) Proterozoic
(d) Archaeozoic

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q31
rows.question:
(A): Genes pass from one generation to another.
(B): The unit of inheritance is genes.
(a) Statement (A) and (B) both are correct.
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct
(d) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.
rows.answer:
(A): Genes pass from one generation to another.
(B): The unit of inheritance is genes.
(a) Statement (A) and (B) both are correct.
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct
(d) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q32
rows.question:
The given figure shows an example of:
theory of natural selection? environment. survive. material. answer from the codes given below.
(a) A - IV; B - I; C - II; D - III
(b) A - II; B - I; C - IV; D - III
(c) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>I</mi></mrow></math>
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>I</mi><mi>;</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mrow></math> crossed to a plant with the genotype:
(a) AABB
(b) AaBb
(c) aabb
(d) aaBB
(a) Homologous organs
(b) Convergent evolution
(c) Divergent evolution
(d) Both (a) and (c)
rows.answer:
The given figure shows an example of:
theory of natural selection? environment. survive. material. answer from the codes given below.
(a) A - IV; B - I; C - II; D - III
(b) A - II; B - I; C - IV; D - III
(c) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>I</mi></mrow></math>
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mi>;</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>I</mi><mi>;</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>−</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mrow></math> crossed to a plant with the genotype:
(a) AABB
(b) AaBb
(c) aabb
(d) aaBB

(b) Convergent evolution
(c) Divergent evolution

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q33

(a) All organisms are equally suited to their
(b) Random selection will determine which organisms
(c) Organisms better adapted to their environment have greater reproductive success.
(d) Organisms that produce the most offspring are better suited to their environment.
rows.answer:
Which of the following statements best describe the
(a) All organisms are equally suited to their
(b) Random selection will determine which organisms
(c) Organisms better adapted to their environment have greater reproductive success.
(d) Organisms that produce the most offspring are better suited to their environment.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q34
rows.question:
(A): Mendel was successful in his hybridisation.
(B): Garden pea was proved as ideal experimental
(a) Statement (A) and (B) both are correct.
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct
(d) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.
rows.answer:
(A): Mendel was successful in his hybridisation.
(B): Garden pea was proved as ideal experimental
(a) Statement (A) and (B) both are correct.
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct
(d) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q35
rows.question:
Match the following columns and select the correct
| | Column-I | | Column-II |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A. | Non-parental gene exchange | I. | Crossing over |
| B. | Non-sister chromatids | II. | X and Y |
| C. | Sex chromosome | III. | Autosome-linked disease |
| D. | Thalassaemia | IV. | Recombination |
rows.answer: (a) Crossing over takes place only between non-sister chromatids.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q36
rows.question: In order to find out different types of gametes produced by a pea plant having the genotype AaBb , it should be
rows.answer: (c) In order to find out the gamete or the genotype of an unknown individual, scientists perform a test cross. In test cross, the individual in question is crossed with the homozygous recessive parent.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q37
rows.question:
(A): A good example of multiple alleles is ABO blood group system.
(B): When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> alleles are present together in ABO blood group system, they both express their own types.
(a) Statement (A) and (B) both are correct.
(b) Statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.
(c) Statement (A) is incorrect but (B) is correct
(d) Statement (A) and (B) both are incorrect.
rows.answer: (a) ABO system consists of four blood groups-A, B, AB and O . ABO blood groups are controlled by gene I. The gene has three alleles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and i . This phenomenon is known as multiple allelism. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> are completely dominant over i . When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> are present together, they both express themselves and produce AB blood group. This phenomenon is known as co-dominance.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q38
rows.question:
Suppose that in sheep, a dominant allele (B) produces black hair and a recessive allele (b) produces white hair. If you saw a black sheep, you would be able to identify
(a) its phenotype for hair colour.
(b) its genotype for hair colour.
(c) the genotypes for only one of its parents.
(d) the genotypes for both of its parents.
rows.answer: (a) Black is the phenotype of the sheep. Without further information, you cannot identify the genotype of a black sheep because it could be either BB or Bb . The possible genotypes of the parents of a black sheep could <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>,</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow><mo>,</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mrow></math>, or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow><mo>×</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mrow></math>.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q39
rows.question:
Refer the given statements and select the correct option.
(i) Percentage of homozygous dominant individuals obtained by selfing Aa individuals is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math>.
(ii) Types of genetically different gametes produced by genotype AABbcc are 2.
(iii) Phenotypic ratio of monohybrid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny in case of Mirabilis jalapa is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>.
(a) All the statements are correct.
(b) Statements (i) and (ii) are true, but statement (iii) is false.
(c) Statements (i) and (iii) are true, but statement (ii) is false.
(d) Statements (ii) and (iii) are true, but statement (i) is false.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q40
rows.question:
In a case of mammalian coat color, the principal gene identified is ' C ' which codes for a tyrosinase enzyme. In case of rabbits four different phenotypes are observed Full Color > Chinchilla > Himalayan > Albino (in order of the expression of gene ' C' and its alleles). In a progeny obtained after crossing two rabbits, the percentages of Chinchilla, Himalayan and Albino rabbits were 50, 25 and 25 respectively. What must have been the genotypes of the parent rabbits?
(a) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><mi>c</mi></mrow></math>
(b) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mi>X</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><mi>c</mi></mrow></math>
(c) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><msup><mi>c</mi><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><mi>c</mi></mrow></math>
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup></mrow></math>
rows.answer:
(c) Here :
Genotype of Full Colour - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mtext>ch </mtext></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mtext>ch </mtext></mrow></msup></mrow></math>
Genotype of Chinchilla - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></mrow></msup><mi>c</mi></mrow></math>
Genotype of Himalayan - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup><mi>c</mi></mrow></math>
Genotype of Albino - cc

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q41
rows.question:
The gene for hemophilia is present on X chromosome. If a hemophilic male marries a normal female, the probability of their son being hemophilic is
[NTSE]
(a) nil
(b) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>25</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math>
(c) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math>
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>%</mi></mrow></math>
rows.answer:
(a)
Since all the male progeny will get the X chromosome form their mother, they will all be normal.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q42
rows.question:
In the experiment conducted by Mendel, RRyy (round green) and rrYY (wrinkled, yellow) seeds of pea plant were used. In the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> generation 240 progeny were produced, out of which 15 progeny had specific characteristics. What were the characteristics?
[NTSE]
(a) round and green
(b) round and yellow
(c) wrinkle and yellow
(d) wrinkle and green

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q43
rows.question:
A breeder crossed a pure bred tall plant having white flowers to a pure bred short plant having blue flowers. He obtained <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>202</mn><msub><mrow><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> progeny and found that they are all tall having white flowers. Upon selfing these <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>F</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math> plants, he obtained a progeny of 2160 plants. Approximately, how many of these are likely to be short and having blue flowers?
[KVPY]
(a) 1215
(b) 405
(c) 540
(d) 135
rows.answer:
(d) According to dihybrid phenotypic ratio
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mn>9</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>:</mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></math>,
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> TW - </mtext><mn>9</mn></mtd><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> Tw - </mtext><mn>3</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> tW - </mtext><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd columnalign="left"><mtext> tw - </mtext><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></math>
The total number of short and blue flowered plants is
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><mn>2130</mn><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1080</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>135</mn></mrow></math>

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q44
rows.question:
Varieties of vegetables such as cabbage, broccoli and cauliflower have been produced from a wild cabbage species. Such process of producing new varieties of living organisms is called
[NTSE]
(a) Natural selection
(b) Artificial selection
(c) Speciation
(d) Genetic drift
rows.answer: (b) Artificial selection is characterized by intentional reproduction of an organism having desirable traits. It is also called as selective breeding.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q45
rows.question:
Which of the following are pairs of analogous organs?
(I) Forelimbs of horse - Wings of bat
[NTSE]
(II) Wings of bat -Wings of butterfly
(III) Forelimbs of horse - Wings of butterfly
(IV) Wings of bird - Wings of bat
(a) (I) and (II)
(b) (II) and (IV)
(c) (III) and (IV)
(d) (II) and (III)
rows.answer: (b) Analogous organs are such organs which show anatomically different structures but doing similar functions. Example : wings of a bat and wings of a pigeon.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q46
rows.question:

[KVPY]
(a) Devonian period
(b) Cambrian period
(c) Carboniferous period
(d) Silurian period

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q47
rows.question:
Which one of the following options lists the primary energy source(s) for all forms of life on the earth?
(a) Light, inorganic substances
[KVPY]
(b) Inorganic substances, organic substances
(c) Light, organic substances
(d) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></math>
rows.answer: (b) Chromosomes carry genes, which are the hereditary characters to the offspring.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q48
rows.question:
Four important events given below may have led to the origin of life on the earth.
[NTSE]
(A) Formation of amino acids and nucleotides
(B) Availability of water
(C) Organization of Cells
(D) Formation of complex molecules
(a) A, B, C and D
(b) B, A, D and C
(b) A, D, B and C
(d) B, C, A and D
rows.answer:
(a) The genotypes of offspring of parents having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>O</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> blood groups are:
From the above cross, it is shown that none of the offspring will be of blood group O.
∴ The probability of their first child having type O blood is zero.

rows.title: Exercise 3 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) : — Q50
rows.question:
If the genotypes determining the blood groups of a couple are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>O</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math>, then the probability of their first child having type O blood is:
[KVPY]
(a) 0
(b) 0.25
(c) 0.50
(d) 0.75
rows.answer:
If the genotypes determining the blood groups of a couple are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>O</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msup><msup><mi>I</mi><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></math>, then the probability of their first child having type O blood is:
[KVPY]
